Hawley's speech (excerpted from June 2 remarks at American Compass's New World Gala) builds three frames in sequence. First, the covenant frame: America is founded neither on abstract Enlightenment propositions nor on ethnic descent but on a covenant — the Arbella oath of 1630, John Winthrop's distinction between "freedom of the belly" (license, freedom of the strong to oppress) and "moral liberty" (the freedom to do what is good, just, and honest under God) — which Hawley uses to position the eight-hour-workday, Homestead Act, anti-child-labor, and social-insurance traditions as covenant economics rather than market-driven outcomes. Second, the K-shape frame: developers, engineers, investors, and founders bend up while truck drivers, paralegals, local newspaper editors, and recent college graduates bend down. Third, the choose-the-covenant-or-break-it frame: both parties have failed the worker (faculty lounge vs. boardroom, "your values are bigotries" vs. "your wages are externalities"), and AI will force the choice the parties have so far avoided. His program: (1) regulate AI in the economy to ensure it aids the worker but does not displace him, with named carve-outs (commercial driving, medication dispensing, personal counsel, courtroom advocacy — jobs only humans should do), explicitly rejecting UBI as a "stipend" that degrades worker dignity; (2) prohibit further data-center construction until companies agree to bring their own power, protect residential electricity rates, and safeguard local water (the president was right to ask, the speech says, but the asking must become a prohibition); (3) child-safety regulation citing the leaked Meta internal guidelines that authorized "sensual" conversations with minors to drive engagement. The closer is a direct ultimatum to the GOP: be the party of the covenant or the party of the boardroom — you cannot be both.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the most direct statement to date of the national-conservative AI position, and it is unusual in being a speech rather than a paper — meaning Hawley is committing rhetorically (in front of donors, in front of the national-conservative bench he is trying to build) to positions that can be cited back at him in 2027 votes. The speech is best understood as a complement to Cass's "Liberals in the Weeds, Libertarians Between the Screeds" piece this week and Thompson's "The Republic Strikes Back" — together they describe a coherent national-conservative program: covenant-citizenship at the foundational layer (Cass, Hawley), republic-not-empire at the foreign-policy layer (Thompson), and worker-not-stipend at the AI-transition layer (Hawley). The Compact-Commonplace nexus is doing the work of building the intellectual infrastructure for a post-Trump GOP that retains the working-class realignment Trump produced but has actual policy content rather than personalism.

The speech's strongest move is the explicit rejection of UBI. The standard tech-industry response to AI displacement has been UBI-or-some-variant, and the standard left response (Sanders) has been UBI-plus-nationalization-of-AI-firms. Hawley refuses both on essentially the same ground: a republic of free citizens cannot be built on a check; work is dignity, not wage; the eight-hour workday was about Sabbath and soul as much as hours. This is a substantive policy position, not a rhetorical one — it implies regulatory limits on what AI is allowed to be used for (no AI commercial driving, no AI medication dispensing, no AI courtroom advocacy) that are very far from Silicon Valley's current trajectory and that have no natural Democratic counterpart. The data-center prohibition-on-construction-until-they-agree-to-internalize-costs is a sharper version of the same instinct: the firms are wealthy enough to bring their own power and protect residential rates and safeguard local water; therefore they must.

The framework's biggest analytical strain is the historical claim that the U.S. has "forestalled every single time" the worry that machines would take all the work by "choosing to bend the technology to the citizen" — this is a substantial overclaim. Earlier transitions (the McCormick reaper, the textile mill, the assembly line, mainframe computing, the personal computer, the smartphone) were largely not bent by political choice; they displaced labor, reorganized regional economies, and were absorbed via roughly fifty-year painful adjustments in which many workers' lives did not recover. The U.S. did not "bend" textile-mill labor to the citizen; the U.S. moved textile production to lower-cost regions and then offshore. Hawley's argument requires this historical claim to be true to support the prediction that AI displacement can be similarly mitigated by political will; if the actual historical pattern is that political will mostly trailed adjustment rather than directed it, then the prescriptive program collapses to "we should do better than we historically have," which is much weaker.

The other strain is the covenant-framework's load-bearing reliance on the Puritan founding. The Mayflower Compact and the Arbella covenant are real and consequential, but treating them as the U.S. founding moment — rather than the 1787 Constitution and the 1789 Bill of Rights — is a substantive theoretical move that finesses the Constitution's actual procedural neutrality on questions of the good life. Hawley needs the Puritan story to make his political-economic claims about worker-dignity and child-safety into covenant inheritance rather than contingent twentieth-century political settlements. This is rhetorically powerful but conceptually slippery: the Puritan covenant was deeply illiberal in ways modern conservatives would not endorse (theocratic punishment for blasphemy, exclusion of dissenters, Native American expropriation), and reaching back through it for political-economic templates requires choosing which covenant terms count as load-bearing and which are vestigial. This piece is doing real work as a political-coalition document; it requires careful reading rather than uncritical reception. The Pope-Leo-XIV encyclical citation at the close (rejecting the "Tower of Babel" temptation, urging "a city that promotes human dignity") is the most under-noticed move: Hawley is positioning the national-conservative AI program as the political instantiation of a specific Catholic-social-teaching diagnosis, which has implications for the religious coalitions the position can sustain.

↩ Back to TOC

A.I. Critics Must Play A Long Game

Ross Douthat · 2026-06-07 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Princeton's Greg Conti in Compact ("must we have such an age declared already?") and Anton Barba-Kay in The Hedgehog Review (the pope's "valuable tool requires vigilance" framing is like saying "cocaine can be a valuable drug that should be snorted with a pinch of salt") — both arguing Pope Leo XIV's Magnifica Humanitas encyclical conceded the inevitabilist frame.

Douthat partly sympathizes (he wished the pope went deeper on the strangeness of A.I. and its messianic-apocalyptic resonances) but argues a 2026 papal call for mass resistance would be both too late (the technology has built too much wealth/infrastructure/institutional implication) and too early (human nature responds to manifested harms — Hiroshima, industrial-era abuses, the smartphone-kids backlash — not hypothesized ones). For the Christian humanist, providential trust permits patience: God will allow time to tame the technology unless this is the last temptation, in which case columns will not avail. The operative move is to translate the secular discomfort Tyler Austin Harper named in The Atlantic (secular language of harm is inadequate; "sin" works better) into specific actionable condemnations: students cheating with A.I. are doing something gravely wrong; the novelist outsourcing a chapter to A.I. has committed a career-ending literary crime; treating a chatbot as your girlfriend/boyfriend is a sin (not merely a misleading attachment for "less discerning users," as the encyclical timidly puts it). The point is not to stop the disruption but to lay the moral foundation for what's built on the other side — to persuade some people, then more, then almost everyone, that "it might be wrong to use A.I. that way."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This piece is the rare Douthat column where his framework (conservative-Catholic-Trump-skeptic, attentive to providential historical horizons) does generative analytical work rather than producing the predictable culture-war beat. The argument has three layers worth distinguishing. The first is a strategic-rhetorical claim about how moral regulation actually emerges historically — that named, specific, locally-applied condemnations precede and enable structural regulation, and that vague civilizational lament is worse than nothing because it cannot be operationalized. This is the load-bearing claim and it is correct as historical pattern: anti-child-labor regulation came from specific exposés of named factories, not from generalized warnings about industrial modernity; the smartphone-kids backlash crystallized around Haidt's specific "rewire your brain" frame plus mental-health-statistics-by-age-cohort, not around 2010 abstractions about phones being bad. The second layer is the Tyler Austin Harper observation — borrowed but credited — that secular A.I. critics have begun reaching for religious vocabulary because "sin" carries operative force "harm" does not. This is the most interesting cross-orientation observation in the piece and pairs with the broader thread (Persuasion/Linker on postmodern presidency, Compact/Conti on the Pope) that the secular center is reaching for thicker moral vocabulary because the thin liberal frame has empirically failed. The third layer is the specific list — cheating, ghostwriting-by-LLM, A.I.-girlfriends — and this is where Douthat lands the piece's actual contribution: by naming three discrete uses he gives readers (and clergy and editors) something to actually condemn from the pulpit or the masthead next week, which is what the encyclical did not do.

The piece pairs cleanly with the Douthat-Angermayer "Humanmaxxing" podcast also this week. There Douthat presses Angermayer on the Catholic question of whether psychedelic-and-enhancement self-optimization conflicts with the Christian obligation to bodily limit; here he tells secular A.I. critics they should adopt the religious vocabulary of sin. Both pieces are operating from the same diagnostic: the specific concrete condemnation is what produces cultural change, and contemporary religious-humanist criticism has gone gestural at exactly the moment its vocabulary is most needed. Whether the secular A.I. critics will actually take up "sin" as Douthat suggests — or whether Conti's "extirpation" maximalism wins out — is the operative question. Stoller's "AI bubble" frame (BIG, this week) and Smith's "tokenmaxxing isn't shipping products" frame (Noahpinion) are operating from a third axis (commercial-economic) that Douthat does not engage; the three together would make a sharper composite than any one alone.

One thing Douthat does not address: the regulatory-precedent claim ("Hiroshima made nuclear control thinkable") has an awkward implication for his providential-patience argument. Hiroshima was an irreversible mass-civilian harm; the "wait until the bad thing manifests" frame applied to A.I. assumes the bad thing is reversible-after-manifestation in a way nuclear is not. The piece does not engage what counts as the A.I. equivalent of a Hiroshima — what manifestation would be sufficient to organize regulation around — and the omission is load-bearing for whether the strategic frame works on its own terms.

↩ Back to TOC

The Nationalization of American Science

Alex Tabarrok · 2026-06-11 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Office of Management and Budget's proposed sweeping rewrite of federal grant rules (the Regulation for Federal Financial Assistance), joined by ~40 grantmaking agencies including NSF, HHS, DOE, NASA, DOD.

Tabarrok walks through specific clauses: program goals must be "aligned with administration policies and priorities" (§ 200.202); "senior appointees must conduct these reviews" with peer review demoted to "advisory" status (§ 200.205); every grant becomes terminable at will whenever agency priorities change "as they exist at the time of the termination" (§ 200.340); universities must ensure subrecipients don't "significantly damage the reputation of… the Federal Government" (§ 200.332) — a loyalty clause for scientists. The framing as red-tape-reduction is what Tabarrok calls "bullshit": the rules actually add Treasury "Do Not Pay" screening, E-Verify, pre-issuance political-appointee committees, mandatory disclosure of agency-employee contacts, and eliminate Fixed Amount Awards (the one mechanism that paid for outputs not inputs). The drafters openly admit the change is symmetric ideological reversal of Biden-era equity priorities. Tabarrok's structural claim: research runs on decade timescales and administrations on four-year ones, so a system where every grant must serve "the President's policy priorities" introduces destructive volatility. The closing line connects to a recurring Tabarrok theme — America is adopting "the loser policies of authoritarian nations."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Tabarrok in his most directly argumentative register — clause citation, fiscal-mechanism analysis, structural reasoning about timescales — and the piece is the sharpest single account this week of the second-Trump-administration's research-funding architecture. The Vannevar Bush reference is doing load-bearing work: Bush's 1945 Science: The Endless Frontier report established the postwar US science-funding compact — federal money flowing to independent universities via peer review, with the government as patron rather than director. That compact's twin virtues (decentralization and peer-review allocation) are precisely what the proposed rule overturns; Tabarrok's clause citations make the substitution concrete in a way that "Trump attacks science" framings don't.

The piece's analytical move is to refuse the standard symmetric-versus-asymmetric framing. The OMB drafters themselves argue the rule is symmetric (Biden prioritized equity, this administration will prioritize something else, both are within constitutional authority). Tabarrok concedes the symmetry but argues that the symmetry IS the problem — "the new rules don't depoliticize science, they create even more politicization with the sign flipped" — because research's value depends on timescale-stability that political turnover cannot supply. This is the same argument Fukuyama-on-judicial-independence ran in Persuasion last week (the Investiture Controversy through the Glorious Revolution as the institutional precondition for Western economic dominance) applied to research instead of courts. The structural claim is similar: certain institutions only work if they're insulated from short-horizon political control, and the value comes from the insulation itself, not from whose values currently fill the political branch.

The vulnerability Tabarrok glides past is that decentralized peer review has its own well-documented pathologies — what he calls "conformity, gerontocracy, waste" in one phrase. The case for some restructuring is real (NIH study-section conservatism, replication-crisis incentives, the ARPA-style alternative funding model). What's at stake in his argument is that the proposed rules don't replace peer review with a better mechanism — they replace it with senior-appointee discretion, which is worse on every dimension the original critiques targeted. The closing characterization ("commissars") is rhetorically harsh but pointing at something real: when you require every funded researcher to defer to the political branch's current priorities, you get a workforce selected for ideological reliability over scientific judgment. The piece pairs with the Klein NYT column from last week on AI public goods (a positive industrial-policy agenda for federal research investment) — Klein and Tabarrok would disagree about whether the right baseline is more or less federal funding of science, but they would agree that whatever the level, the architecture must protect researchers from short-horizon political volatility. The convergence across orientations on this point is one of the project's load-bearing findings this week.

↩ Back to TOC

Better Sex, Better Hair, Better Sleep: 'Humanmaxxing' Is Here (Interesting Times)

Ross Douthat · 2026-06-11 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Pope's Magnifica Humanitas encyclical critique of transhumanism (which Angermayer has not yet read) — Douthat opens by inviting Angermayer to draw the line between optimization and self-obsession.

Angermayer (German biotech entrepreneur, 48, co-founder of Atai Life Sciences and Compass Pathways) defines "next human agenda" (he abandoned "transhumanism" because it implies becoming something other than human) as using F.D.A.-approved medical drugs plus the five basics (sleep, social relations, no recreational drugs including alcohol/marijuana, gym 2-3x/week, Mediterranean diet). His Enhanced Games (Las Vegas, May 2026) reached one billion original viewers; one world record was broken (Kristian Gkolomeev, swimming); the F.D.A. — not WADA — is the arbiter (testosterone yes, crystal meth no). His personal stack: testosterone replacement therapy since 30s; GLP-1s (he's a stress eater); DHEA; pregnenolone; Quviviq (orexin sleep drug — promotes deep sleep, may reduce dementia risk in elderly); psilocybin once or twice yearly with therapist; has done 5-MeO-DMT, DMT, his whole pipeline except ibogaine. He predicts hair-regrowth drugs in 5 years, AMPK-activator weight-loss-plus-muscle-gain drugs, Kadence premature-ejaculation drug. On psychedelics he reports meeting God ("every single time") via apophatic experience that confirmed his work-and-family priorities; his sharpest theological claim is that "most religions are built on psychedelics" and his sharpest pastoral claim is that the Catholic Church "traded away" mystical experience and direct God-relation "for power and organization." Douthat presses: doesn't this whole regime conflict with Jesus's "give up your body for other people" frame? Angermayer responds with the divine-spark argument — humans' creative drive is the part of God in us; optimization is inherently good — and reframes Douthat's mild overweight as a family-responsibility-failure since his five kids deserve a healthy dad. Notable Angermayer claim: psychedelic group sessions with Palestinians and Israeli Jews produced cross-recognition rather than political resolution.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The analytical work here is in watching where Douthat presses and where Angermayer's frame absorbs the pressure. Douthat's central theological move — naming Jesus as anti-humanmaxxing ("everything Jesus is talking about is giving up your body for other people") — is the sharpest single contribution in the conversation, because it gets at the actual line between Christian humanism and the Angermayer program in a way the encyclical did not. Angermayer's response (the divine-spark argument: humans are God's children and the drive to create is the part of God in us, so optimization is theologically continuous with the imago Dei) is a real and old argument (it has Patristic and Thomistic threads, and current versions in evangelical-tech-right writers like Patrick Collison's circle), but it collapses the specifically kenotic dimension of the Incarnation — the emptying-out, the Cross — that Douthat's pressure is trying to surface. Douthat lets this go in the interview's structure (he is conducting, not prosecuting) but readers should register that the divine-spark response works on the Creation side of Christian theology and not on the Redemption side, where the Christian ethical claim about bodily limit and self-emptying actually lives.

The most striking and least-pressed Angermayer claim is the "most religions are built on psychedelics" assertion, which he attributes to his own Bible-and-Quran reading "with the eye of having done psychedelics." Douthat correctly flags this as "a separate podcast conversation" and "we'll let the claim hang there," but the claim is doing load-bearing work in the interview — it is the move that lets Angermayer frame the Catholic Church as having "traded away" mystical experience for organization. The claim is empirically contested (the psychedelic-origins-of-religion thesis runs from R. Gordon Wasson through Brian Muraresku's The Immortality Key but is not consensus religious-studies scholarship), and Angermayer's deployment of it shifts the rhetorical burden: instead of defending the novelty of psychedelics-plus-Christianity, he gets to frame himself as recovering something organizational Christianity lost. That is a Wasson-via-Muraresku move and worth naming as such.

The piece pairs with this week's Douthat AI-critics column and Scott Alexander's recent ACT essay on Frankfurt-School "negative theology" in interesting ways. Douthat in the AI column tells secular critics to name specific sins; here he is pressing Angermayer to name specific limits — and Angermayer mostly refuses, opting for "freedom plus knowledge plus a doctor" as the limiting principle, which is structurally consumer-sovereigntist. The Persuasion/Borovinsky piece on Milei's AI gamble in Argentina (techno-mysticism in the political register) and Douthat's own Anna Paulina Luna "disclosure populism" podcast last week are the wider Crosscurrents thread this piece sits inside: a 2026 American political-cultural moment in which techno-spiritual claims (UFO disclosure, psychedelic-mediated God-encounter, biotech-mediated life-extension, AI-mediated cancer cure) are flowing into and reshaping mainstream conservative-intellectual discourse in a way that would have been fringe-coded in 2020. Douthat's editorial choice to interview Angermayer at all — and to take the theological argument seriously rather than treating it as billionaire-eccentric — is itself a data point about that flow.

One element worth flagging in the BD sense: Angermayer's pitch is structurally a sales conversation (he is the chairman of publicly-traded biotech companies; his investments are in everything he is advocating for) and Douthat's transparency about that structural fact is partial. He notes Angermayer is a chairman of public biotech companies during the psilocybin section but does not press the conflict-of-interest dimension on the Enhanced Games (which Angermayer founded and runs) or the AMPK-activator drug ("one of my private biotech companies is doing exactly that"). The interview is not a marketing piece — Douthat is genuinely pressing on the theological frame — but the financial-interest substrate of every empirical claim Angermayer makes is treated more lightly than it would be if the same claims were being made by, say, a pharmaceutical executive about cancer drugs.

↩ Back to TOC

Iran after the 12-Day War: spycraft, doctrine, dissent

Spycraft will decide Iran's fate

** Reuel Marc Gerecht ** · 2026-06-08 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Four months into the open-ended Israel-Iran war and a fraying ceasefire, asking what intelligence work — recruitment, counterintelligence, covert action — actually decides whether the Islamic Republic survives.

Gerecht (former CIA Iran operations officer, now at the Foundation for Defense of Democracies) writes from inside the spycraft idiom. He argues Tehran cannot sprint to a bomb because Israeli strikes destroyed too much enrichment infrastructure and because Iranian counterintelligence will paralyze its own scientific establishment hunting for moles. The harder problem for Mossad and Aman (IDF military intelligence) is sustaining real-time recruitment now that the IRGC is on high alert; he is skeptical of any large-scale defection wave but credits Israeli claims that ordinary Iranians have been feeding location data on senior officials. The structural divide he draws: Israel treats the regime as an existential threat, the US does not — so American intelligence will remain in a supporting role, constrained by bipartisan Congressional reluctance and a "let's say hi" reflex toward Tehran that has survived every Iranian outrage since 1979. The operative recommendation he flags as Mossad's most promising lever is arming Kurdish, Baluch, and especially Azeri minorities — with the warning that Persian opposition figures and expatriates will treat minority-arming as a threat to Persian primacy, making the move politically combustible.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a tradecraft-voice piece, and the right way to read it is to separate the operational analysis (where Gerecht is on his home ground) from the policy frame (where his FDD priors are loud). The operational core holds up: the distinction between "covert-action agents" (logistics, sabotage, often team-based, often ethnic-minority) and "foreign-intelligence assets" (lone reporters on regime policy) is real and matters, as does his observation that mixing the two categories is rare because it compromises both. His point that Iranian counterintelligence will turn a "buzzsaw to a lot of innocent people" inside the nuclear establishment is the kind of second-order effect that gets missed in damage-assessment discussions — even a clean Israeli operation produces ongoing degradation through the regime's paranoid response, which is itself the point. Persuasion's Vand argued the opposite case last week in the roundup ("The Islamic Republic Is More Dug In"), reading the war as having consolidated rather than weakened the regime; Gerecht's piece doesn't directly engage that, but the two together name a genuine open empirical question — does intra-regime convulsion strengthen or weaken theocratic durability — that nobody currently has data to settle.

Where the policy frame leans heavy is the implicit answer to "what should the US do." Gerecht's framing of American restraint as the product of guilt-tripping over the 1953 Mosaddegh coup, plus an unspecified "reflex to engage" attributed to Democrats from Clinton onward, is a familiar FDD argument with the structural assumption that the right amount of American clandestine pressure on Iran is "more than current." The Trump-aborted Kurdish operation he describes — Netanyahu pushing it, Erdoğan calling Trump, Trump pulling the plug — is offered as evidence of Israel's frustration with American caution; but it's also evidence that Turkey has veto power here for reasons (Kurdish independence as a direct PKK threat) that have nothing to do with American squeamishness, and Gerecht doesn't engage that. The Azeri-rebellion scenario as the only one large enough to potentially collapse the regime is presented matter-of-factly; the second-order risk — that an Azeri civil war pulls in Azerbaijan proper and possibly Russia — gets one sentence about "internecine strife."

The piece's most useful contribution to the project this week is the analytical move at the end: Mossad and Aman now operate on the explicit premise that this is "a duel to the death," which means escalation logic flows from Israeli intelligence services' operational tempo rather than from diplomatic cycles. That matches what Mitra Vand was arguing from inside Iran last week (the regime is using the war to consolidate; ceasefires are tactical pauses for both sides), and it stands in some tension with the Klein/Bremmer Gracchi-trap framing in which the US is the principal destabilizer. The honest synthesis is probably that both can be true: the US has become the geopolitically unreliable actor while Israel runs its own war on its own timetable, and the period when Washington could constrain Tel Aviv on Iran appears to be over.

↩ Back to TOC

Exclusive: U.S. Secretly Deployed Paratroopers to Israel

Ken Klippenstein · 2026-06-08 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Pentagon's March announcement that the 82nd Airborne was deploying to "CENTCOM" — a deliberately vague formulation that the press echoed as Kuwait or Qatar.

Klippenstein publishes the operative document — an April 7, 2026 Army deployment order directing elements of 2nd Battalion, 501st Infantry Regiment (the "Geronimo" battalion) to Israel on "temporary duty" — and reports that a military source involved in war planning tied the deployment to U.S.-Israeli joint contingency plans completed since February for seizing Kharg Island (Iran's main oil export hub) and carving out coastal territory inside Iran. He situates the secrecy against three context layers: (1) Gulf Arab "host nation sensitivities" — the 82nd staged from Israel avoids needing Kuwait or Qatar's consent for a joint U.S.-Israeli operation; (2) the deepening intelligence-sharing push to make Israel a sixth "Five Eyes" partner (with U.S., U.K., Canada, Australia, NZ); (3) JCS Chairman Gen. Dan Caine's May 5 briefing about the 82nd's readiness to "seize key terrain" framed via WWII / Grenada / Panama precedents, which Klippenstein reads as an Iran-directed message rather than a generic capability statement. Charlie Company of 2nd Battalion remains in Israel; no dispatches in two months. The piece closes carefully: "I'm not saying a ground operation is imminent or inevitable. But if one were coming, wouldn't it be nice to know?"

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is original-document reporting — the closest thing Crosscurrents gets this week to what the LLM Council's gap taxonomy calls "Atoms-not-Bits" primary-reality journalism. The deployment order is the load-bearing artifact. The Pentagon's public posture was "CENTCOM, unspecified," and the New York Times' March 23 reporting placed the 82nd as one option being weighed by senior officials; Klippenstein's contribution is to convert the conditional ("officials are weighing") into a documented fact ("the order was issued April 7, the unit is the 2nd Battalion 501st, Charlie Company is still in Israel"). The named-unit, dated-order specificity is the difference between speculation and reportage, and it's the standard against which Klippenstein's work should be judged.

The piece's strongest move is the structural argument about why Israel as the staging point matters specifically — not as a symbolic alignment but as a logistics workaround. Klippenstein walks the reader through the operational reasoning: Gulf Arab partners (Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi) would have to grant overflight and basing for a Gulf-staged Iran operation; Israel doesn't require that consent; the 82nd's quick-reaction airborne profile is uniquely suited to launching from Israel directly. This is mechanism-first analysis — the kind of "how things actually work" that doesn't require taking sides on whether the operation should happen to be useful as a public-information contribution. The reader comes away knowing something concrete about U.S. force posture that the Pentagon actively concealed.

The piece's weakest move is the single-source attribution for the strategic context. The deployment order is documentary; "a military source involved in war planning" is not. The leap from "82nd is in Israel" (well-supported) to "the deployment is tied to U.S.-Israeli joint plans for seizing Kharg Island and coastal Iranian territory" (single anonymous source) is the kind of inference the reader needs to hold separately from the documented fact. Klippenstein knows this — his closer ("I'm not saying a ground operation is imminent or inevitable") is doing the epistemic-hedging work — but the headline and the strategic framing rest on the single source. This isn't a fundamental flaw; it's the normal limit of a one-reporter operation working from documents and a source network, and the right reading is to treat the Israel deployment as confirmed and the Kharg-Island linkage as one informed reading of why.

The Five Eyes sixth-partner aside is the most under-noticed substantive claim in the piece. If accurate, it represents a structural realignment of the U.S.-Israel intelligence relationship that has been the subject of speculation for years; Klippenstein states it as if it's settled, which it isn't publicly. Worth carrying forward as a structural-claim hypothesis to test against future reporting.

The Caine "seize key terrain" briefing read deserves engagement on its merits. Klippenstein argues that the JCS chairman's WWII / Grenada / Panama precedent-citing in a May 5 public briefing was a signal to Tehran rather than a generic capability statement. This is a reasonable reading — the precedents named (Sicily, Normandy, Grenada, Panama) are all forcible-entry operations, not deterrence postures — but the alternative reading (Caine speaking to a domestic audience about the 82nd's identity and history) is also available. The piece would be stronger if it engaged the alternative reading rather than presenting its preferred interpretation as the obvious one.

↩ Back to TOC

Why Trump Seems Confused By His Own War

Sam Kahn · 2026-06-08 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Stephen Miller's January 6 CNN exchange with Jake Tapper ("the iron laws of the world since the beginning of time"), Trump's March 17 "you could say we did it out of habit" remark about the Iran war, Ludovic Hood's National Interest article "Stephen Miller Channels Hans Morgenthau," the 2025 National Security Strategy and 2026 National Defense Strategy, and the breaking news of fresh Iran-Israel exchanges shattering the ceasefire.

Kahn reads Trump as carrying himself "like a wise fool in a Shakespeare play," cheerfully passive as a doctrine he isn't really driving sweeps him along. The doctrine is political realism — Kahn traces it from Thrasymachus's "justice is the advantage of the stronger" in Plato's Republic, through Thucydides's "the strong do what they can and the weak suffer what they must," through Machiavelli and Hobbes, to Hans Morgenthau's mid-20th-century synthesis. Morgenthau, writing in the aftermath of World War II and the failure of the League of Nations and inter-war treaties, argued that a weak global entity is worse than none, that liberal cooperation lets the rational disarm while the malevolent advance, and that "moral behavior is to engage in power politics." Kahn concedes that Morgenthau is largely right about the limits of international institutions and about the necessity of containment in the Soviet period. But he distinguishes true Morgenthavian realism — clear-eyed strategic positioning across diplomatic, military, and ideological dimensions, with a "satisfied" state's moral burden to curb less-satisfied states via balance-of-power — from what Patrick Porter (via Linda Kinstler in the NYT Magazine) calls Machtpolitik: "violent exhilaration of destruction, nihilism and vengeance." Kahn argues Putin's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, Netanyahu's post-October-7 multi-front campaign, and Trump's 2026 Venezuela-Iran-Cuba moves form a single trans-national realist baton-pass — and that the empirical scorecard is bad: 500,000 Russian dead in a war of choice, Israel demoted from "co-pilot" to "mere passenger" in the Iran operation, and the US discovering it has less control over events than expected. The closer is that Miller and Hood haven't read Morgenthau deeply: realism requires ideological self-justification, alliance-building, and recognition that international institutions, however thin, are an "important fig leaf."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Kahn doing the analytical-essay work the project gets too little of: taking a doctrine that has become operational policy (the realism turn explicitly codified in the 2025 NSS and 2026 NDS), tracing its intellectual genealogy, distinguishing the genuine version from the operational caricature, and showing where the caricature fails on its own terms. The genealogy is well-handled (Thrasymachus → Thucydides → Machiavelli → Morgenthau is the canonical reading-list, and Campbell Craig is a serious Morgenthau interpreter the piece is right to cite). The piece's strongest single move is the distinction between Morgenthavian realism and Machtpolitik — many critiques of the Trump administration's foreign policy stop at "this is dangerous nihilism" without engaging with whether realism is the right intellectual frame at all. Kahn engages, takes the realism frame seriously, and shows where the administration is failing the frame's own standards: a Morgenthavian US would have to think through diplomatic and ideological dimensions, build alliances, and accept the moral burden of a "satisfied" state — and it is doing none of those.

The empirical scorecard at the end of the piece is the right move and where the argument is strongest: 500,000 Russian dead with Ukraine "stronger and more implacable than before" is a real cost-benefit analysis on Putin's own claimed realism logic. Israel's "co-pilot to passenger" demotion is real and recent (cited NYT of May 23). The cumulative case that "realism" as currently practiced produces strategic worse outcomes than the late-period liberal-internationalist baseline is the piece's load-bearing claim and it earns it.

The under-developed move is the Trump-as-passive-vehicle framing. Kahn opens with this metaphor (the Shakespearean wise fool watching tumult around him) and the March 17 "habit" remark is genuinely striking, but the piece doesn't fully commit to whether Trump is in fact a passive recipient of a Miller-Mearsheimer-Hood doctrinal current, or whether the doctrinal current is being post-hoc rationalized around Trumpist impulses that have always been there. The two readings have different policy implications: if Trump is the passive vehicle, the doctrinal current outlives him; if the doctrine is post-hoc rationalization, the cure is a 2028 replacement.

Cross-piece resonance: this is in direct argumentative cousin-relation with Fukuyama's "A Liberal Vision for Europe" (this same Persuasion week) — Fukuyama is making the European-identity argument against the Rubio-Vance ethno-Christian frame, while Kahn is making the foreign-policy argument against the Miller-Hood realism frame. The two together form a Persuasion-week argumentative pair against the operative ideology of the second Trump administration. It is also in conversation with Damon Linker's "Welcome to the postmodern presidency" (last week, one-liner #63) which framed the same phenomena as Bloomian Nietzscheanization. Kahn's contribution to that thread is to specify which doctrine the Nietzscheanization is being routed through (realism via Machtpolitik) and where the operational practitioners diverge from their nominal intellectual ancestor. Useful Persuasion debut by Kahn (associate editor and author of Castalia and Republic of Letters substacks).

Carry-forward: the Kahn-Fukuyama-Linker arc on the second-Trump-administration ideological frame is now substantial enough to warrant a discrete tracking line. The empirical-scorecard contention (the realism turn produces worse outcomes than the liberal-internationalist baseline it replaced) is testable as the Iran ceasefire situation evolves; if Iran emerges from the war as Mitra Vand's "Persuasion - The Islamic Republic is more dug in" piece (one-liner #62 last week) argued, the Kahn empirical case strengthens further.

↩ Back to TOC

How Can Lebanon’s Partners Help Strengthen the Lebanese Armed Forces?

Faysal Itani and Lina Khatib and Fadi Nicholas Nassar and Nancy Ezzeddine · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The April 2026 US-brokered Israel-Lebanon talks and the Lebanese government's 2025 plan to use the Lebanese Armed Forces (LAF) to disarm Hizballah, with Israeli strikes continuing to kill Lebanese soldiers and Hizballah having just rejected a proposed ceasefire.

Itani (Middle East Policy Council) writes the first of four expert contributions to a War on the Rocks symposium; the rest is paywalled to members and inaccessible. The piece is a paywalled symposium teaser — only Itani's section was readable, so this entry covers his contribution alone. Itani's argument: the LAF is more capable than commonly assumed but financially broken — it cannot pay soldiers' salaries, forcing officers into second jobs, which severely degrades any willingness to undertake politically fraught operations. The piece's operative claim is that the binding constraint isn't capability or US training but social composition: a quarter of the LAF is Shiite, the religious community that forms Hizballah's base, and they might refuse orders to confront Hizballah or even defect with their weapons. The implication — that even fully funded and armed, the LAF cannot be the disarmament instrument the US is treating it as — is the part Itani gets to develop before the paywall cuts in.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The piece is too short to evaluate fully, but Itani's framing is consistent with the standard Lebanon-watcher view that confessional composition is the LAF's structural ceiling, and worth surfacing here because it directly contradicts the US policy assumption underlying the April Israel-Lebanon talks. The Biden-era and now Trump-era playbook treats LAF capacity-building as the path to Lebanese sovereignty and Hizballah disarmament; Itani's claim is that more money and training does not solve the problem — and could make it worse if the army splinters along confessional lines under operational stress. The basics he names (unpaid soldiers taking second jobs) are well-documented and reasonable to take on face value.

What the truncation costs us is the comparison across the four experts. War on the Rocks symposia typically use the format precisely to surface disagreement among informed people, and the analytical value of the format is the spread of operative recommendations — Itani's "the social fabric makes confrontation impossible" view is probably the most pessimistic of the four, and without the others we can't see what plausible alternative paths the symposium considers. The piece is included in the roundup because Itani's specific contribution is a real expert argument on its own; the synthesis composer should treat it as a single-source data point rather than a representative symposium reading.

The cross-week link worth flagging: Gerecht's UnHerd piece this week argues Mossad/Aman now treat Iran as a duel-to-the-death, with Israel running its own military operational tempo regardless of US objections. If Itani is right that the LAF cannot disarm Hizballah, then the Israel-Lebanon talks are functionally a stalling mechanism while Israel continues degrading Hizballah by direct strike — which fits the pattern of Israeli operations on its own timetable that Gerecht describes for Iran. The two pieces taken together suggest the formal diplomatic track is decoupled from the operational reality on both fronts.

↩ Back to TOC

The Iran War Critic Under Investigation by the Trump Administration. Plus. . .

Eli Lake · 2026-06-11 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The ongoing U.S.-Iran war (a second night of U.S. bombing on June 10; Iran downing an Apache helicopter; inflation up to 4.2% on energy costs) and the simultaneous administration push to investigate one of its loudest domestic critics.

This is Jay Solomon's lead item in the FP Front Page daily — pointing to his longer FP investigation. Trita Parsi is co-founder of the Quincy Institute for Responsible Statecraft (the restraint-foreign-policy think tank), an Iran-born, Sweden-raised permanent U.S. resident green-card holder, and the most-quoted American critic of the U.S.-Israeli campaign against Iran. The State Department under Marco Rubio has been investigating whether to deport him on the basis that he is more than a pundit — that he has functioned to advance an Islamic Republic agenda in U.S. discourse. The accusation comes from administration officials, Trump allies, and Iranian-American anti-regime activists, one of whom told Solomon the Tehran regime "effectively positioned" Parsi to advance "a strategic agenda." Parsi denies the charge. The political-coalition twist: Parsi has called himself an "informal" adviser to Obama's Iran negotiating team and is currently making common cause with conservative and MAGA leaders who oppose the Iran war and are critics of the U.S.-Israel alliance — the Steve Bannon faction. The open question: will the anti-war right rally to defend him from a deportation his anti-war work helped make possible?

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The Front Page placement is a daily-newsletter teaser format; the substance is best evaluated as the lead-in to Solomon's full investigation, not as a standalone analytic piece. What the teaser is doing analytically is foregrounding a structural test of the Trump-era restraint coalition. The Quincy Institute / Parsi-style restraint argument — that U.S. interests don't require the Israeli alignment, that an Iran war is a strategic disaster, that the "Israel lobby" produces policy distortions — has been an unusually durable cross-coalition position over the past five years, with Bannon-style MAGA nationalists, Tucker Carlson-style anti-interventionist conservatives, the Quincy/Cato libertarian-restraint cluster, and progressive anti-war voices all converging on it. Solomon's piece tests whether that coalition holds when one of its key figures becomes the legal-jeopardy test case. If MAGA leaders (Bannon, Carlson, Massie, Vance) rally publicly to Parsi's defense, the restraint coalition becomes a real political force inside the GOP; if they don't, it's revealed as rhetorical alignment that breaks down when the policy and the politics diverge.

The substantive question Solomon's framing raises but doesn't resolve: what's the evidentiary basis for the "regime-positioned" allegation? Parsi has been a target of accusations from Iranian-American anti-regime activists for over a decade — the National Iranian American Council (which he co-founded before Quincy) faced lawsuits in the 2010s alleging undisclosed coordination with Iranian government interests; those cases mostly went against Parsi's accusers, though a 2012 case found NIAC had engaged in litigation misconduct. That history is the load-bearing context any honest assessment of the new State Department investigation requires. Solomon's teaser doesn't surface it, which is reasonable for a Front Page lead-in but leaves the reader without the framework for evaluating the new charges; the full FP investigation is where that work needs to be done.

The piece's most analytically interesting move is the structural one — Solomon foregrounds the deportation question as testing whether the administration is using immigration enforcement as a policy-disagreement weapon. This is the same structural concern that animated the spring 2026 Mahmoud Khalil case (Columbia grad student / green card revocation), and Parsi's case extends it from foreign-student protestors to a green-card-holding policy intellectual at a Beltway think tank. If the precedent solidifies — that permanent residents can be deported on the basis of policy positions the administration considers aligned with hostile foreign powers — the implications run well beyond Parsi. The roundup-level question worth carrying forward: is this an isolated test case, a category of cases (anti-war foreign-policy critics), or the beginning of a systematic move? Solomon's piece is the earliest reporting in what could become a structural-precedent question.

For the roundup: original reporting from FP that breaks a real story, with the substantive evidentiary work likely happening in the full investigation rather than the Front Page teaser. The right read in the synthesis is to treat this as a load-bearing piece for the Iran-war-domestic-fallout thread and the "administration-using-immigration-as-policy-weapon" structural concern, and to defer detailed engagement with the merits of the deportation case to the full FP piece.

↩ Back to TOC

Maine, Platner, and the Democratic-Party realignment

Platner Wins; Washington Whines

Ken Klippenstein · 2026-06-10 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Graham Platner's June 10 win in the Maine Democratic Senate primary, after weeks of negative coverage focused on his tattoo, PTSD-related struggles, sexting allegations, and threats — none of which dislodged him from a 41-year-old oyster farmer / combat veteran without prior office or donor network.

Klippenstein takes a victory lap on his earlier "Platner had lost Washington's vote, and he'd survive it" prediction, then pivots to a forensic forecast of how the D.C. press will reframe Platner now that "he can't win" has collapsed. He enumerates five genres to watch for: (1) "Now Comes the Hard Part" — concede the primary, immediately pivot to the Susan Collins general; (2) "Platner Won By Less Than Expected" — manufacture an underperformance narrative despite Maine being one of the hardest states to poll (2020: Sara Gideon led ~6.5 points in RCP average, lost by 8.8); (3) "The Real Story Is Whether He Can Win in November" — relitigate electability indefinitely; (4) "Democrats Are Privately Worried" — anonymous-sourcing genre laundering D.C. consensus through unnamed insiders ("experts think," "strategists say"); (5) "He'll Still Have to Answer For..." — repurpose the same scandals from "will sink him" to "will haunt him." He cites a push poll showing Platner's support increased after voters were told the scandals. Frames the underlying drive as the party + press wanting to "pick the successor before Platner has even put his feet up on the desk."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is press criticism written from inside a working hypothesis, not from neutrality, and the analytical question is whether the hypothesis pays its way. The five-genre taxonomy is the load-bearing claim. As a falsifiable prediction it's strong — Klippenstein is staking his credibility on a specific catalog of moves he expects the next two weeks of NYT / WaPo / Politico coverage to instantiate. As an analytic frame it's weaker than it looks at first read, because the genres are broad enough to absorb a lot of coverage that would also be written for any non-establishment nominee in any state. ("Can he win the general?" is not, on its face, evidence of D.C. malice toward Platner specifically — it's the question every primary-night write-up answers.) The piece's strongest moment is the Gideon-2020 polling miss: a concrete numerical anchor (15-point swing from polling to result, against the same Collins, in the same state) that gives the "Platner underperformed" forecast a real predictive edge over generic skepticism. The weakest is the Chris Hayes / NYT paragraph, where Klippenstein conflates a journalist asking a question (Hayes pressing Platner on the sexting-minors allegation) with insinuation — the question is the way zero-evidence accusations get either confirmed or buried, and pressing it is how a friendly interviewer gives the candidate room to dismantle it.

The Mamdani comparison Klippenstein drops in passing — "Mamdani-inspired hand-wringing that Platner isn't ready for Washington" — is doing more work than it looks. It places Platner inside a recognizable category (NYC mayoral candidate Zohran Mamdani, who beat the Cuomo machine and is now the test case for whether the party can absorb a left-populist outsider) and primes the reader to read post-primary coverage as a continuation of a pattern rather than a Platner-specific event. This is the move that distinguishes Klippenstein's piece from a generic "media is unfair" complaint: he's claiming a coordinated apparatus rather than incidental friction, and the Mamdani precedent is his strongest evidence the apparatus exists.

The piece's argumentative core is the "erase the public from the story" move at the end — the framing that Washington's preferred narrative requires denying that Mainers made an informed choice. This is the load-bearing claim across the piece, and it's where the analytic stakes are highest. If Klippenstein is right, the five genres collectively constitute a de-democratization move dressed as horse-race analysis. If he's wrong — if the post-primary coverage actually engages Platner's policy positions, his pre-tattoo writing, his veterans' organizing, and his Collins matchup on the substance — the genre taxonomy collapses into selection bias. The forcing function for the reader: hold the five labels in mind through the next two weeks of NYT / Politico / Atlantic Platner coverage and check what fraction of pieces fit. It's an unusually testable piece of media criticism, which is what makes it worth reading even where its individual moves are arguable.

↩ Back to TOC

Monopoly Round-Up: Graham Platner and Stock Market Democrats

Matt Stoller · 2026-06-08 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The New York Times-initiated scandal around Maine Democratic Senate candidate Graham Platner (an oyster-farmer-and-bartender veteran who won the Democratic primary against Governor Janet Mills) — allegations from former girlfriend Lyndsey Fifield (conservative political operative) of misogynistic behavior and being yanked from a cab by the wrist, layered onto a controversial skull-and-bones tattoo Platner got in Croatia. The piece operates as Stoller's read of why the Democratic Party is fighting over Platner in particular.

Stoller's frame: the financialized post-1970s American political economy was constructed through bipartisan deregulation choices (Reagan through Clinton) and almost ended in 2007-2010 but was rescued by Bush-then-Obama choices (the Simon Johnson "oligarchy" finding); the anti-monopoly movement was born in that aftermath (Lina Khan in journalism; Zohran Mamdani in politics; Platner serving drinks at the Tune Inn). Platner has a substantive populist platform: rejects corporate PAC money, supports breaking up healthcare monopolies, called to "bring the hammer of antitrust" down on the Paramount-Warner merger, went after the Fanatics sports-merchandise monopoly. He also opposes the U.S.-Israel alliance and the broader post-Cold War security architecture — which, Stoller argues, makes him existentially threatening to the political-economic elite whose prestige depends on that architecture. The money trail: Susan Collins's biggest donor is hedge-fund billionaire Ken Griffin ($2.5M; Griffin became a Collins supporter in 2017 after she single-handedly rescued the carried-interest tax break for hedge funds and PE), plus a $23M billionaire-SuperPAC ad reservation; Griffin's Citadel was central to the GameStop saga and helped throw Spirit Airlines into insolvency. On the Democratic side, Stoller catalogs the anti-Platner coalition: Van Jones (who took $100M from Bezos) calling it a "moment of truth," David Frum at The Atlantic urging Democrats to "cut Platner loose," Democratic Majority for Israel consultants posing as ordinary Maine voters, lobbyists who work at firms on the Saudi payroll writing op-eds, centrist Senators Hassan/Slotkin/Shaheen distancing themselves. Nate Silver is the named-and-shamed example of "concerns over policy laundered through the unanswerable question of electability" (Silver's 2009 "Give Geithner a Break" piece is the receipt). Stoller's read: this is a re-run of Obama-era intra-Democratic fights, but the post-financial-crisis generation is now in adulthood and wants oligarchy cracked down on.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Stoller's distinctive frame this week — and the thing that makes the piece worth carrying despite being one of many Platner takes — is that it reads the scandal not as a question of Platner's character but as a fulcrum mechanism through which the Democratic Party debates monopoly-and-finance policy that cannot be debated directly. The analogy he draws is to the Mamdani-anti-semitism debate: a topic that is hard to say out loud (pro-oligarchy, pro-Iran-war) becomes accessible via a personal-conduct-and-decency proxy. This is a specific testable claim — that the same donor network funding the anti-Mamdani campaign funds the anti-Platner campaign, with Griffin as the connecting node — and Stoller anchors it well with the Citadel-GameStop and Spirit-insolvency receipts. The Nate-Silver-2009-Geithner-essay receipt is the cleanest single-pundit example: if Silver is consistently positioned as the prognosticator who counsels deference to experts during financial-system crises, then his Platner-electability arguments do load policy-substance.

What is missing from the analysis — and where the framing gets thinner — is engagement with the Fifield allegations on their substance. Stoller writes that he has "no particular insight in this race or this scandal" and that "what has been revealed so far has not been enough to derail Platner's candidacy among voters," but this is closer to declining to engage than to a finding. The full analytical case requires either (a) demonstrating that Fifield's allegations are inflated or fabricated (which Stoller does not attempt) or (b) acknowledging the allegations may be substantively true and arguing that the political weaponization of true allegations against an anti-monopoly candidate is itself the mechanism the piece is describing. Stoller implicitly does (b) but does not own it explicitly — which is the gap a hostile reader will exploit. The Mamdani analogy is helpful here: there were and are real questions about Mamdani's record on Israel-Palestine, and the question of whether financial-elite donor networks weaponized those real questions for monopoly-preservation purposes is logically independent of whether the questions are themselves substantive. The piece would be sharper if it owned this structure rather than working around it.

Where the piece is sharpest is in connecting the Platner race to the broader Stoller thesis (developed across the year in BIG): the 2007-2010 moment was the last serious opportunity to reorder the financial-political architecture, the moment was missed (Simon Johnson's 2010 "oligarchy" call), and the post-financial-crisis generation that came into adulthood after the missed reorder is now adult enough to vote, run, and be funded. Platner is Stoller's case study for what that political agent looks like in 2026. The connection to Stoller's own AI-bubble piece this week is sharp: the analytical claim that Platner-vs-Collins is a "prelude to a financial crisis" — that the 2028 cohort of candidates will be measured against Platner as the marker of whether the post-crisis political demand can be channelled or has to be suppressed — is consistent with the AI-bubble argument that the crash will come and the question is who holds power afterward. This is Stoller's distinctive frame in the multi-writer Platner cluster (Klippenstein, Silver, Tracey, NR): the others read the race as a candidate-character question or a polling-and-electability question; only Stoller reads it as a reading on the post-crisis monopoly-financialization architecture and its political defenders. That distinctive frame, including the Griffin-as-connecting-node specificity, is what to carry forward.

A subscription gets you:

↩ Back to TOC

The state of play in Maine

Silver Bulletin · 2026-06-10 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Graham Platner's 72%-19% Democratic primary win over Janet Mills on June 9 (Mills had suspended her campaign in April), tightened post-scandal Maine polling, and prediction-market repricing of Democratic odds in Maine.

Silver reads the primary result two ways simultaneously: "Platner won by 50 points on high turnout" and "30 percent of Democrats protested Platner despite essentially running uncontested." His benchmarking: Maine is a D+14 state on the current generic ballot; Collins won by 9 points in 2020 (down from 37 in 2014) so will get less incumbency-bonus weight from Silver's model than usual; Maine polling has systematically overrated Democrats since 2014 and Collins specifically has a documented history of beating her polls. The three post-sexting-scandal polls show Platner +2 average (vs. +7 before), including a Platner internal and a Collins internal — internals usually exaggerate by ~4 points but the directional story is consistent. Tavern Research (the one nonpartisan post-scandal poll) has Platner net -8 favorability, Collins -17, and a generic Democrat beating Collins by 10 (Platner is underperforming the generic by ~8 points). Silver thinks Democrats could have done better than toss-up-to-lean-D with a runner-up from the Maine gubernatorial primary (Troy Jackson named) given Platner's below-average electability post-scandal — though he notes Mills polled worse (-20 favorability, at 78 would be the oldest first-term elected senator). Platner has until July 13 to withdraw; Silver argues if his campaign were trustworthy it would invite full vetting and pre-commit to dropping out if anything worse emerged, but instead it's threatening former staffers. He flags Platner's Reddit posts (offensive to Republicans more than Democrats) and the SS-Totenkopf-tattoo (covered up last year) as ongoing surface-area, with ranked-choice voting cutting in unclear directions.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Silver doing the analytical-discipline thing he's good at: separating the question "did Platner clear the bar he had to clear" (yes, comfortably) from the question "is this the candidate Democrats should have wanted" (no, he's leaving votes on the table). The 30%-of-Democrats-protesting-an-uncontested-primary framing is a real signal — Silver is right that this is uncommon for a candidate the establishment had supposedly given up trying to dislodge.

The piece is in direct argumentative engagement with three other roster pieces this same week: Yglesias's "Seventeen thoughts on Graham Platner" (one-liner #69 last week — "his bigger problem is running a factional campaign in a state he only needs to unite partisan Democrats to win"); deBoer's "Of course this is a coordinated ratfucking" (one-liner #18 last week — that the Atlantic/NYT/NY Mag cascade is engineered Democratic-establishment sabotage); and Klippenstein's "Graham Platner loses Washington's [respectability]" (one-liner #40 last week — class-coded preference for sterile politicians). Silver occupies the median position among these: he is skeptical of Platner's electability without endorsing deBoer's conspiracy frame and without Klippenstein's class-symbolic frame. He is essentially saying "yes, this could be reverse-engineered by the establishment, but the candidate did in fact behave badly enough to make Democratic electability anxiety reasonable on its merits." That's a useful middle position to mark in the synthesis.

The structural carry-forward question Silver is implicitly raising — and that the project has now tracked across deBoer, Yglesias, Klippenstein, Silver, and Douthat (one-liner #54 last week, swing-voter case for Platner over Talarico via amoral-center theory) — is: what does it mean when an open-primary, low-establishment-vetting selection process produces a candidate whose ongoing-scandal-surface is large enough that the candidate's own polling shows below-generic underperformance? This is, in Yglesias's frame, a primary-system design question (closed-primary states would have produced Mills); in deBoer's frame, an establishment-sabotage question; in Silver's frame, a candidate-quality-uncertainty question. The fact that five roster writers across deBoer-Klippenstein-Yglesias-Silver-Douthat are converging on the same race from different orientations is a real Narrative-convergence signal worth flagging for the synthesis composer.

Silver's strongest single move: pointing out that Platner's campaign threatening former staffers is the diagnostic move — if your defense against further scandal is intimidation rather than invited vetting, you are telegraphing that you expect more to come out. The methodological-political-analysis register Silver pioneered (probability-weighted thinking, internal-poll adjustment, fundamentals-vs-polling reconciliation) is on display here in clean form.

↩ Back to TOC

Why Everyone Wants Jon Ossoff to Run for President

Michelle Goldberg · 2026-06-08 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Atlanta re-election kickoff at which Ossoff spent almost no time on his Republican opponents and instead focused on Trump's tungsten-mining-in-Kazakhstan kleptocracy (the president of Kazakhstan granted U.S.-backed mining rights to the world's largest undeveloped tungsten deposit; six days after Eric and Don Jr. Trump's-backed company took a 20% stake, the parent company received $1.6 billion in federal financing). Stanford political scientist Adam Bonica's 2024 essay on anti-corruption movements toppling Marcos, Yanukovych, Pérez Molina, Najib Razak is the operative framework.

Goldberg argues Ossoff has converged on the cross-orientation playbook that worked against Orbán in Hungary (Peter Magyar's "mafia state" frame in April 2026): pair sweeping anti-corruption indictment with a reclaimed national mythology that challenges authoritarian white-nationalism, anchored in civil-rights-tradition pluralism (Ossoff was John Lewis's intern at 17, sworn in on Rabbi Jacob Rothschild's Bible). She tracks his profile (Jewish critic of Israel — signed Sanders's 2024 arms-embargo resolution, one of only 19 senators; documentarian background producing BBC films on ISIS Yazidi atrocities, an alleged Kenyan death squad, and Ghana Football Association bribery — Ghanaian co-investigator Ahmed Hussein-Suale was assassinated in 2019), his stylistic-Obama-imitator-turned-distinctive-cerebral-cool, his old-fashioned reserve (light on cable, light on podcasts, light on TikTok), his Citizens-United-amendment focus, and his "Epstein class" coinage for the bipartisan enabling network. Operatives quoted: David Axelrod (parallels Obama's 2004 "as close to a draft as I have ever witnessed" arc), Sarah Longwell ("President-maxxing so hard"), Tré Easton (Searchlight), Bonica (in polls Democrats are seen as just as corrupt as Republicans — anti-corruption reform is one of the few moves that creates a new axis rather than the entrenched left-right one). Mehdi Hasan: if Ossoff wins re-election he is "one of the favorites for the 2028 Democratic presidential nomination."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Goldberg writing toward two specific Crosscurrents threads. The first is the Gaza-as-litmus-test thread also running through Klein's podcast with Matt Duss this week: Ossoff's 2024 Sanders-resolution vote (over Biden White House opposition) earned him the credibility that Kamala Harris's "too many civilians have died" language did not. Goldberg and Klein/Duss converge on the same operative claim — that Gaza is functioning structurally the way Iraq did in 2007-08, with credibility flowing to whoever was on the right side at political cost — and Ossoff is the cleanest center-pole illustration of that dynamic. The Israeli-paper Haaretz quote about his re-election chances "becoming that much harder" after the 2024 vote is the diagnostic: he took political risk on the moral position and now the moral position has become the median Democratic position (Rahm Emanuel, who once volunteered with the IDF, now calling for aid cutoff). The piece does not say "Iraq" but it is structurally arguing the parallel.

The second thread is anti-corruption-as-cross-partisan-axis, which is also running in Persuasion's Linker piece on postmodern presidency and Klippenstein's "let them drive less" on oil-executive militias. Goldberg's most analytically load-bearing citation is Bonica's claim that authoritarian regimes have been toppled by anti-corruption movements specifically because anti-corruption "creates an entirely new axis of conflict" rather than fighting on the entrenched left-right battlefield where positions are locked in. The Magyar-against-Orbán comparison is the operative real-world case, and the framing is structurally compatible with Duss's claim (in the Klein interview) that campaign-finance reform should be the anchor of the post-Biden Democratic doctrine — both writers are pointing at the same axis of opportunity from different orientations. Where they differ: Duss treats it as a doctrinal opening for the left; Goldberg treats it as a candidate-fit problem the party should solve by drafting Ossoff specifically.

The piece's central tension Goldberg names but does not fully resolve: Ossoff's Citizens-United-amendment position is, as Goldberg correctly says, likely to read to many progressives as a punt (constitutional amendments are practically impossible in current configuration) but it is also exactly the kind of "ambitious but cross-partisan" move Bonica's playbook calls for. Whether that reads as principled or as procedural-evasion depends on whether the campaign can sustain the Magyar-style narrative pressure (which depended on a very specific cultural-political configuration in Hungary that may or may not transfer). Goldberg flags the question without pretending to answer it, which is appropriate given Ossoff is at the moment running for re-election, not the presidency.

One Goldberg-as-columnist note: she is at her strongest in this piece when she is doing operational reporting (the Kazakh-tungsten example, the Hussein-Suale assassination, the Insight TWI documentary background, the Rothschild-Bible swearing-in detail) and when she is letting other voices (Axelrod, Bonica, Easton) do the prescriptive work. The piece is more reported than argued, which is the right register for a "this is the candidate to watch" piece a year and a half out from the primary. Compare to Yglesias on Talarico (Slow Boring, last week), which is more prescriptive but also more contestable; Goldberg's restraint here is well-judged.

↩ Back to TOC

The Left's Reckoning on Gaza, Elites and American Power: The Ezra Klein Show

Ezra Klein · 2026-06-09 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: What would a left foreign policy actually look like in the 2028 Democratic primary, given the Gaza rupture? — Matt Duss (executive VP, Center for International Policy; former Sanders foreign-policy adviser, currently advising A.O.C.) sets the post-Biden agenda.

Klein opens by framing Gaza as a 2008-style rupture (Brian Schatz call for foreign-policy-staff turnover; Chris Van Hollen NYT op-ed naming "complicity"; El-Sayed, Hamawy, Lander primaries running on the issue) and invites Duss to articulate the replacement. Duss diagnoses Biden's policy not as a mere disagreement but as "supporting genocide" and demands specific officials be barred from future administrations — drawing on the 2018 Yemen War Powers Resolution precedent where former Obama officials publicly conceded the "stay engaged for influence" frame was a mistake. His Israel program: end aid; end sales beyond Leahy/Arms Export Control Act compliance; sanction settler officials; accept tacit Hamas-inclusive Palestinian self-governance because disarmament will not occur under occupation. The broader doctrine pairs Crow/Vance's "system is rigged" diagnosis (working-class burden of post-Cold-War interventions) with A.O.C.'s "pre-rules-based-order" reframe — Gandhi's "would be a great idea" applied to the UN system — and Murphy's argument that Trumpism's appeal is the promise that global forces can be brought back under control. Klein presses three times on the protectionism-versus-cheap-goods tension (Chinese EVs, global minimum wage) and Duss largely concedes he is "still working on" the operational form. On China, Duss rejects supplantation theory and argues for coexistence; on immigration he admits the limiting principle is unclear on the values-based left; on corruption he names campaign finance and South Dakota kleptocrat trusts. The closer is Ossoff as the operative messenger ("America had the most corrupt political system in the Western world even before Trump came on the scene").

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the single clearest piece this week on the doctrinal vacuum the post-Biden Democratic Party is trying to fill, and the analytical work is in watching where Duss is precise versus where he is gestural. The Gaza accountability argument is precise: Duss names the specific institutional move (Schatz tweet, Van Hollen op-ed), the specific historical precedent (the 2018 Yemen letter from former Obama officials), the specific legal architecture being violated (Leahy Law, Arms Export Control Act), and the specific operational ask (named officials barred). This is the part of the conversation that resembles 2007-vintage Iraq-war-accountability rhetoric, and Klein registers the parallel explicitly. Where Duss becomes gestural is exactly where Klein presses hardest: the global minimum wage question (Duss: "I don't know what it looks like"), the cheap-goods-versus-American-jobs trade-off on Chinese EVs (Duss: "I do think part of the answer is just addressing the idea that people feel like they're getting hit with costs all over the place" — which Klein correctly identifies as a dodge), and immigration's limiting principle ("the limiting principle of immigration, at a moral level, is a very difficult one"). The pattern is clean: the negative program (end aid, end sales, bar officials) is fully specified; the positive program is a vocabulary (values, working class, anti-corruption, internationalism-rooted-in-domestic-consensus) without operational content. Klein, whose own foreign-policy beat has been mostly the AI/abundance side, is the right interviewer because he keeps demanding the operational form rather than accepting the vocabulary.

The Crow/Vance convergence Duss returns to twice — Trump-as-symptom-not-cause, working-class burden of failed interventions — is also the spine of the Hansen NYRB Hegseth piece from the prior week's roundup, and is the cleanest cross-orientation alignment this week. Both Vance (right-populist) and Crow/Sanders/A.O.C. (left-populist) are now naming the same thing: that the post-Cold-War foreign-policy establishment has lost legitimacy because it imposed costs on a constituency that lacked exit, and that "the system is rigged" reads true to those voters whether spoken by a Republican or a Democrat. The Iran war operates throughout as the live demonstration of this — Duss invokes it twice as proof the security state has not been disciplined, and notes its absence-from-Congressional-debate as evidence the system has continued degrading.

The Israel-as-Iraq parallel deserves explicit naming. Klein and Goldberg this week (the Ossoff column) converge from very different orientations on the same operative claim: that Gaza is functioning as a generational litmus-test the way Iraq did in 2007-08, with credibility flowing to whoever was on the right side at political cost. Goldberg names Ossoff's 2024 Sanders-resolution vote as the cleaner version of the move; Duss/Klein name Mamdani's not-backing-down-under-attack as another. The Iraq parallel has a structural implication the conversation does not quite extract: Iraq accountability ultimately did not produce a coherent Democratic foreign-policy doctrine — Obama campaigned on rupture and governed mostly on continuity. The Duss-articulated program, in its current form, may produce the same gap (clean negative program; vocabulary-only positive program), and the audience for the post-Biden generation should treat Duss's "still working on what exactly it looks like" line on the global minimum wage as the diagnostic of where the doctrine is not yet load-bearing.

One Klein-as-interviewer note: he does not press hard on Duss's claim that the Abraham Accords are a "factor that led to Hamas's thinking about why they needed to take action" on October 7. This is an unusual causal claim (the Hamas-side rationale on Abraham-Accords-as-trigger is contested) and Klein moves past it. The rest of the interview's pressure-testing is sharp, so the omission is notable.

↩ Back to TOC

Can we retire “public safety”?

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-11 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The LA mayoral runoff between left-YIMBY councilmember Nithya Raman and incumbent Mayor Karen Bass (after third-place finisher Spencer Pratt was eliminated), and a broader pattern in Democratic crime rhetoric.

Yglesias diagnoses "public safety" and "gun violence" as 2020-vintage euphemisms — verbal formulas that let center-left politicians signal concern about rising crime without conceding ground to "tough-on-crime racists" (the accusation Alec Karakatsanis-style left-abolitionists were leveling at the time). The euphemisms worked: Biden and big-city mayors stopped short of defunding the police, the 2022 turnaround happened, and homicide declines have continued. But the euphemism is now load-bearing in the wrong direction — it signals deference to the "progressive NGO complex" rather than seriousness about reducing crime. He uses Brandon Scott's Baltimore — where focused-deterrence policing has driven a major homicide drop but Scott calls it "combating gun violence" — as a case where the euphemism actively obscures the achievement. Mamdani's NYC trajectory (retaining the prior police commissioner, NYPD growing in size, crime falling) is the contrasting model: a politician who has stopped caring whether his enforcement decisions draw unreasonable criticism from unreasonable people. Crime is also a useful word because it covers the wider range of disorder voters actually care about (street urination, retail shoplifting tipping points, Pershing Square encampments, rich-guy tax evasion) — broader than violence, more specific than "safety."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Yglesias doing the move he's best at: identifying a piece of political vocabulary that hardened into a habit and arguing it now functions as the opposite of what it was meant to signal. The diagnosis is genuinely sharp. "Public safety" did emerge in 2020 as a way for politicians (often African-American politicians representing high-crime neighborhoods) to talk about constituent concerns without being denounced as racist; the rhetorical concession was useful given the moment. The argument that the 2020-era ladder is now worth kicking away is more defensible than most "stop saying X" essays because Yglesias does the work of explaining why the euphemism originally helped and what specifically has changed (the Court of progressive opinion he was buying clearance from is no longer the binding constraint on Democratic primary politics).

The Mamdani-as-counterexample turn is the piece's most operationally useful claim. The argument isn't that Mamdani has converted to neoliberal policing — it's that he's demonstrating the rhetorical move Yglesias is recommending: a politician's revealed preferences are what the politician does, and what Mamdani is doing is acting like someone who cares more about crime falling than about avoiding criticism from the people who would have denounced him for that posture in 2021. The Gothamist anecdote (29 paragraphs to find a Bronx resident pleased about extra patrols) is the press-side complement to the Baltimore Brandon-Scott observation: even when the substantive story is enforcement-works-and-residents-want-it, the framing apparatus still routes the story through professors and activists.

Where the piece is weakest is in the broader scope-creep claim that "crime" should be the operative word for everything from sidewalk urination to large-scale tax evasion.
