The taxonomy is useful as Yglesias's general framework but obscures a real cost: "crime" does obscure distinctions Democratic voters actually want to draw — between vagrancy/disorder enforcement and prosecuting wage theft, between fare evasion and homicide. The Karakatsanis position Yglesias dismisses ("the whole idea of crime is a mistaken framing") was conceptually overreaching, but the underlying point — that legal categories of crime are political constructions that pool things voters might want decoupled — isn't entirely wrong. "Say crime" is good political advice for Raman; it's not a substitute for the substantive policy debate about which forms of disorder enforcement reduce crime versus merely redistribute it.

The Raman framing is also load-bearing: Yglesias's reading is that Raman has the unique advantage of having been denied the DSA endorsement and therefore being unburdened by the institutional left in a way that lets her credibly run a unifying-reform message Dan Lurie-style. Whether that's actually how she'd play the runoff is a forecast — the piece doesn't really engage the possibility that Raman's actual political base prevents the moderation move it advises.

↩ Back to TOC

The substrate: phones, faith, work, fading youth

Are you finally ready to admit it's the phones?

Noahpinion · 2026-06-11 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Caitlyn Myers and Ezekiel Hooper's new working paper "Is the iPhone Birth Control? Causal Evidence from AT&T's 2007–2011 Carrier Monopoly," and the cluster of skeptical responses (Alex Nowrasteh, Tyler Cowen, Greg Ip).

Smith opens autobiographically — he says he felt foreboding on first hearing about the iPhone in 2007, before the smartphone-social-media era had a name — and frames the piece as a long-deferred admission that the social-media-by-phone bundle has been a civilization-scale mistake. He synthesizes Myers and Hooper's natural experiment (AT&T's 2007-2011 iPhone monopoly let them identify the iPhone's causal effect from spatial variation in AT&T's 3G coverage; they find iPhone access reduced teen births 4.5-8.0% at 15-19 and 3.2-6.6% at 20-24, explaining 33-52% of the 15-44 GFR decline) alongside Hudson and Boedo's parallel-design replication in England and Wales using terrain-ruggedness variation, and Brookes (2026) on the Faroe Islands' 2015 fast-wireless rollout (which postdates the Great Recession). He cites the Bursztyn et al. (2024) "product market trap" experiment finding college students would pay $24/month to deactivate TikTok campus-wide because the network effect makes individual exit non-viable. He adapts John Burn-Murdoch's finding that the recent fertility drop is driven by fewer people becoming parents (Stephen Shaw's demographic work) rather than parents having fewer children, breaking the pre-1940 trend-extrapolation defense. The Castelo et al. (2025) two-week mobile-internet block experiment — 91% of participants improved on at least one mental-health/well-being/attention outcome, with mediation through increased in-person socializing — provides the well-being arm. Smith engages Cowen and Nowrasteh's "long-running trend" dismissal directly (calls it pre-1940 trend-extrapolation that ignores the trend's compositional shift) and Greg Ip's Great Recession interactive-confound objection (Myers-Hooper control for unemployment, median income, poverty, house prices, though imperfectly). He closes with Lyman Stone's "new selfishness" framing (digital technology enables sitting-at-home selfishness as a viable ideology) and a Great Filter speculation that biological evolution simply cannot keep pace with technological evolution past a phase transition.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the longest, most carefully argued of Smith's recurrent phones-cause-malaise posts, and it shifts the dialectical position from "the data is suggestive" to "the data plus the identification strategy plus the mechanism are now mutually reinforcing across multiple countries." The piece's strength is that it engages skeptics' best arguments specifically rather than dismissing them. The Nowrasteh "long-running trend" critique gets a real response: Smith adopts Burn-Murdoch's compositional point that the pre-1940 fertility drop was within-couple while the post-2010 drop is at the marriage/cohabitation margin, which materially breaks the simple trend-extrapolation case. The Ip "interactive confound" critique gets a partial response: he notes Myers-Hooper's controls and adduces Brookes's Faroe Islands replication as a Great-Recession-independent test, though he concedes the controls aren't perfect. The Castelo intervention experiment is genuinely important if it replicates — a 2-week blocking trial with 91% improvement on at least one outcome is a real causal-identification step beyond observational correlation.

The piece intersects directly with two other June 2026 contributions worth tracking. Tyler Cowen wrote "A Simple Reason for Skepticism About the iPhones-Fertility Link" the same week (file marginal-revolution-a-simple-reason-for-skepticism-about-the-iphones-fertility-link-html.md in this articles folder); the disagreement isn't fully resolved by Smith's response here, since Cowen's structural skepticism about treating a single causal mechanism as load-bearing for a multi-causal phenomenon is a different objection from the trend-extrapolation one Nowrasteh raised. Freya India's "When Faith Is Just An App" piece (Free Press, also this week) approaches the phones-as-civilizational-disruptor frame from a complementary religious-formation angle. The convergence across Smith (fertility/economic), Cowen (skeptic position), and India (religious-formation) makes this a candidate cross-orientation thread for synthesis.

The weakest moves in the piece are the closing speculative arms: the Great Filter framing converts an empirical-identification argument into a science-fiction-flavored civilizational-decline narrative that requires the reader to extrapolate from a 2007-2026 timeseries to billion-year cosmological speculation. The footnote distinguishing phones-as-fertility-suppressor from social-media-as-democracy-corruption is analytically helpful — it acknowledges that the social-media-on-laptop counterfactual matters and is open about which part of the bundle is doing what work — though it also concedes that disentangling phone-effect from social-media-effect remains hard. The Lyman Stone "new selfishness" frame imports a normative claim (people should want children, are misled by digital technology into preferring solitude) that is doing real work in the piece without being defended. Strong piece; biggest unresolved question is whether the cross-mechanism identification strategies in Myers-Hooper and Hudson-Boedo really do isolate the iPhone effect from the broader 2010s digital-life shift, or whether they're picking up a more diffuse "post-2007 internet-life transition" that the iPhone happens to time-stamp.

↩ Back to TOC

A simple reason for skepticism about the iPhones/fertility link

Tyler Cowen · 2026-06-11 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Bailey-Currie-Kearney (Federal Reserve / Brookings) paper claiming smartphone diffusion explains 33-52% of the post-2008 decline in US general fertility rates, and Noah Smith's amplification of it.

Cowen's argument is mechanical and dispositive: iPhone subscriptions were 1.6% of all American adults in 2008, 3.6% in 2009, 5.5% in 2010. US fertility was already falling sharply in 2008 (before any iPhone effect could plausibly operate). Conception-to-birth lag is nine months minimum, and family-planning lag is longer. The network-effect channel (the iPhone "messes up your dating processes") requires a critical mass of users that didn't yet exist, and the content available on the early iPhone was a tiny fraction of what's available now. So the proposed causal mechanism is being read backward from 2026 — "the explanations I am hearing seem to be parasitic on world intuitions from 2026, not the time period under consideration." This is direct argument, not Straussian: Cowen names the operative move (anachronistic intuition-projection) explicitly.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a clean piece of methodological discipline of the kind Cowen does best in his short posts — a single numerical fact against a high-profile finding, and the finding can't survive it without major reworking. The Bailey-Currie-Kearney paper is the latest entry in a growing economics literature trying to assign a single technological cause to the post-GFC fertility collapse (other candidates include the 2008 recession itself, contraceptive-access expansion, opioid mortality, housing-affordability deterioration, and the "deaths of despair" labor-force participation drop). Cowen's objection is the simplest possible one — the dose-response curve doesn't work — and it's the kind of objection that papers using high-powered econometric identification often paper over with instrumental-variable cleverness.

Cowen doesn't elaborate the alternative explanation but the implicit one is the prior consensus: post-2008 fertility decline tracks the housing affordability crisis, delayed family formation among the cohort that came of age in the Great Recession, and a broader shift in the cultural script for adulthood that long predates the iPhone. He's not denying that smartphone use eventually affects dating-market dynamics (the 2020s discourse about dating apps as collective-action traps has real evidence behind it); he's denying that the mechanism could have operated through a 3.6%-of-adults user base in 2009 to produce a one-third-of-the-decline magnitude. The post pairs with last week's MR piece on European consent-based-laws-and-aggregate-fertility (which Cowen treated approvingly) — Cowen is selectively skeptical of fertility-causation claims, but he's at least applying the same dose-response discipline rather than just credentialing claims that fit his priors. The signal here: when a high-profile economics paper produces a finding inconsistent with a base-rate sanity check, the finding loses.

↩ Back to TOC

When Faith Is Just an App

Freya India · 2026-06-12 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The (contested) statistics suggesting a Christian revival among Gen Z, and the proliferation of paid Christian apps — Hallow ($6/month), Dwell ($399.99 lifetime), #Bible, Glorify, Ascend, FaithTime, Hallow AI, Holy Bible Chat — that increasingly mediate the experience.

India, a young writer exploring Christianity herself (cross-posted from her Substack GIRLS), argues that her generation may be finding "content about God" rather than finding God. The piece moves through three concentric concerns: (1) commodification — Christianity reduced to a subscription product, with apps offering prayer streaks, leaderboards, and trivia ranks; (2) gamification — Hallow's "Game Streak" and "trivia leaderboard," Ascend's "learn the Bible like a game," a Catholic Duolingo clone, FaithTime's "addictive" mini-games designed for the "2020 brain"; (3) virtual community — Hallow AI, "Holy Bible Chat anything anytime," livestream churches, the Blessed app advertised as "the only online community you'll ever need." Her structural argument is that supplements become substitutes: if you encounter the app version first, before stepping into a church, the app becomes your baseline experience and the real-world version becomes redundant or even painful. She draws an explicit parallel to how online porn distorts expectations of real intimacy. The closer is a plea — if Christianity wants to actually reach her generation, it has to promise something "otherworldly, something that doesn't abide by market logic," and Christians need the conviction to say their faith is "too complicated, too sacred, to turn into TikToks."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the kind of piece where the substantive argument is more careful than its register suggests. India is not making the simple "screens bad" claim; she's making a specific epistemological argument about the order of encounters, drawing on Neil Postman's 1980s "Amusing Ourselves to Death" critique of televised religion and updating it with the gamification mechanics that didn't exist in Postman's frame. Her core move — "We encounter the virtual version of everything first, before the real thing. And so that becomes our standard. Supplements become substitutes" — is a clean version of what media theorists call the first-impression-as-baseline problem, and it's a sharper argument than the standard "online community is shallow" critique because it specifies the mechanism: not that the virtual is intrinsically inferior but that order-of-encounter shapes what the real thing has to compete against.

The piece's analytical strength is the internal-experience honesty. India admits she puts off going to church "because I know that I can" — the phone is there as escape hatch, and the existence of the easier substitute corrodes the harder practice even when the practitioner intends to use the easier version as a bridge. This is the same dynamic that makes weight-loss apps undermine the diets they're nominally supporting, or makes language-learning apps substitute for the harder work of immersion. India's contribution is to name the dynamic in the Christian-formation context and trace its specific apps and pricing — the kind of mechanism-first writing that gives a familiar critique fresh purchase.

The piece's weakest move is the unexamined "real church" framing. India treats stepping into a physical church as the load-bearing alternative without engaging the substantial diversity of what that means — a Catholic Mass, a Baptist megachurch, a college-town nondenominational gathering, an Eastern Orthodox liturgy, a Pentecostal service, and a Quaker meeting are all "the real thing" relative to the apps but they make wildly different demands on the practitioner and offer wildly different things. The argument would be stronger if she engaged the question of why the apps are growing fastest in the Catholic and Evangelical spaces (where Hallow and similar are dominant) and what the absence of an equivalent Mainline Protestant or Orthodox app ecosystem might mean. The piece doesn't have to answer the question, but acknowledging it would prevent the "step into church" framing from doing more rhetorical work than the argument supports.

The cross-thread connection worth noting: India's argument runs parallel to a recurring Crosscurrents thread about whether digital mediation systematically degrades the practices it's supposed to facilitate (dating apps and dating, social media and friendship, telehealth and primary care). The Christian-formation case is interesting because the consumer-base is rapidly growing and self-selecting for spiritual seekers — exactly the group whose first encounter most shapes downstream behavior. If India's argument is right, the Hallow et al. growth curve is producing a generation of seekers whose internalized standard for "what prayer is" was set by a gamified app, and whose subsequent encounter with embodied parish life will feel demanding and slow in ways the apps haven't prepared them for. This is a structural prediction the piece doesn't fully claim but is implicit in its logic, and it's the kind of multi-year carry-forward question that this piece warrants noting.

For the roundup: thoughtful Gen Z Christian writer doing serious cultural criticism on her own beat, executing it with internal experience and concrete app-and-pricing texture. Earns its place in the bundle as a substantive contribution to the "digital mediation and embodied practice" thread.

↩ Back to TOC

The Death of the Summer Job

Larissa Phillips · 2026-06-11 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Last week's Forbes report (citing BLS data) that this is the worst summer-job market in nearly 80 years, with teen employment at its lowest recorded level.

Phillips's "Culture and Ideas" essay weaves a personal memoir of mid-1980s teenage Nantucket summer jobs — mother's helper at 14 (the famous miscommunication: "the job is 9 to 5" meant her employer's job, not Phillips's; she ended up sleeping over six days a week with the kids), busing tables at a family restaurant the following summer, dishwashing at a celebrity-haunted three-floor townhouse restaurant the year after — into a defense of the institution as a transformative adolescent rite. She catalogs what was at stake: physical risk (nearly slicing off a fingertip with a paring knife), social difficulty (the eponymous "Coming in!" shout she couldn't bring herself to make on "the line" until August), exposure to adult dysfunction (a domestic-violence incident at the babysitting job when the divorcing father stormed in shouting threats), and the formation of a value system that registered "the different cogs that go into any complex human machinery." She acknowledges the standard explanations for decline (inflation, cautious employers, bespoke summer camps, safety-anxious parents) without much-litigating them. Notes that ~5-6 million teens (a third) will still work this summer; she salutes them. Phillips's career as a small-business owner / restaurateur informs the piece — she writes from inside the labor market she's defending.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a memoir-anchored cultural essay that does a familiar Free Press job: convert a labor-statistics datapoint into a values argument with concrete personal texture. The structural move is the one to assess. Phillips is not making the strong claim that the BLS decline is the cause of any specific adolescent pathology — she avoids the Haidt / Lukianoff register where "kids don't work summers anymore" gets connected to anxiety, fragility, delayed adulthood. Instead she makes the weaker but more defensible claim that the summer job was a load-bearing experience for her own development and that its loss represents a missing rung, leaving the question of "what fills the gap" open. This is the right altitude for the piece — heavier claims would require evidence the memoir genre can't supply.

The memoir-as-argument has real strengths. The "Coming in!" structure — Phillips spends most of the piece building toward a single moment of having to shout authoritatively in a kitchen full of adult men — works as both literary device and substantive evidence. The reader gets a concrete texture of what "social development through work" actually meant, not in the abstract but in the embodied sense of a 16-year-old's voice finding volume under pressure. Similarly the domestic-violence incident at the babysitting job is doing real work: Phillips doesn't moralize it, doesn't extract a tidy lesson, just observes that her parents' divorce had been "calm, logical conversations" and that this was the first time she'd encountered the version where furniture got thrown. The juxtaposition is the argument — the summer job was where the cushioned upper-middle-class adolescent encountered the world as it actually contains itself, including its dysfunctions.

The piece's weakest move is the gloss on causation. Phillips lists the standard explanations for decline (inflation, cautious employers, summer camps, safety-anxious parents) and treats them as equally weighted without engaging the empirical literature on which actually drive the trend. The labor-economics question — is the teen employment decline structural (minimum wage, employer screening costs, automation of entry-level service work) or cultural (parents' preferences, college-resume optimization, social anxiety) — gets waved at rather than addressed. This is fine for the genre but limits the piece's load-bearing capacity as policy commentary; she's writing as a memoirist, not as an analyst of labor markets, and the reader should hold the causation paragraph as gesture rather than argument.

The interesting structural question the piece raises but doesn't pursue: if the summer job's value was the encounter with adult dysfunction, real risk, and the experience of being a small cog in a complex machine, what contemporary institutions plausibly substitute? Phillips doesn't argue that none exist, but she doesn't name candidates either. The piece would have more analytic teeth if it engaged the question — is the loss of the summer job partially compensated for by, say, sustained athletic team participation, the gig economy for older teens, or the rise of teen entrepreneurship — or whether the things that look like substitutes are structurally different in ways that matter (curated, parent-mediated, low-stakes-relative-to-real-employment). The absence of the question is what marks this as a Free Press lifestyle/memoir piece rather than a structural-analysis piece.

For the roundup: this is a thoughtful, well-executed lifestyle essay with a sharp memoir core, weak causal analysis, and modest analytical ambition. It earns its place in the bundle by being well-written and concrete about a real statistical trend, not by carrying significant load on the larger argument the synthesis would want to make about adolescence, labor markets, or adult formation.

↩ Back to TOC

There Will Be More Henry Nowaks

Ralph Leonard · 2026-06-09 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The December 2025 stabbing death of Henry Nowak, an 18-year-old Southampton man killed by Vickrum Digwa on Nowak's way home from a night out — and the responding officers' decision to handcuff Nowak (the victim) rather than treat him, after Digwa told them Nowak had made racist remarks. The case has detonated into a British political flashpoint with Nigel Farage's "two-tier culture" framing and left-side calls not to "politicize" the death.

Leonard concedes Digwa was promptly tried, convicted, and sentenced — the jury rejected the false self-defense claim — but argues the officer response is the real evidentiary core. He situates the case within a longer historical arc: in the 1970s and 1980s British policing presumed black people guilty by race; post-2020 the apparatus has inverted, now producing "bureaucratic anti-racism" defined by the police's own statement that racial equity "does not mean treating everyone 'the same' or being 'colour blind.'" The operational consequence is officials hesitant to act against wrongdoing when the suspect belongs to a protected category, illustrated through the grooming gangs scandal, the Southport killer Axel Rubakabana (teachers warned not to describe his violent fantasies as "sinister" lest they stereotype a black boy with a knife), and Nottingham killer Valdo Calocane (not committed despite documented psychosis because of detention-overrepresentation research). Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood recently had to intervene to prevent sentencing guidelines that would have granted ethnic-minority leniency. Leonard's prescription is classical civil-rights universalism: the law institutionalizes the general will and must treat individuals as individuals, not as members of a community.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Leonard's piece is the British-policing analog to the American "soft bigotry of low expectations" frame, but the writer-position is what makes it cut differently. Leonard writes as a Black British leftist (his prior Compact work and the rhetorical care with which he handles the right's narrative both signal this), and the piece's central move is to refuse the binary between "this is anti-white racism" (Farage) and "don't politicize the tragedy" (left). The structural argument: the 1970s-80s policing regime that presumed black guilt and the 2020s regime that presumes minority innocence are the same error — both treat individuals as race-avatars rather than as persons. Calling this "anti-white racism" misses the structure; it's race-differential treatment full stop, and its costs land on whoever the discretionary system currently disfavors. The Nowak case is unusually clean evidence because the officers' decision was operative within minutes — Nowak was bleeding out while handcuffed — and turned on uncritical acceptance of Digwa's "racially attacked" claim. The grooming-gangs/Rubakabana/Calocane litany is the same diagnostic applied across three domains: enforcement, education, and mental-health detention.

The piece connects to the project's ongoing thread on socially-isolated young men (the "Henry Nowak frame" framing in the editor's note is the writer-naming pattern Compact often uses to install a case as a recurring reference point, parallel to "Madoff frame" or "Platner Maine"). Where the Platner-Maine thread treats the isolated-young-man problem as electoral-political (deBoer on "smoothgroin politicians" vs. people who actually exist; Klippenstein on Washington's class-coded outrage), and where the faith-app thread (Freya India) treats it as religious-affective compensation, Leonard's piece supplies the institutional-betrayal angle: a regime designed to protect minorities has produced a generation of young men who can be killed in the street and then handcuffed for their own murder, with a press class instructing the survivors not to "politicize" it. The class-and-race composition of Nowak's milieu (white working-class English) maps onto the same demographic the Platner cluster keeps surfacing as politically homeless. That this is happening in Britain rather than the US is significant — it's evidence that the structural pattern isn't downstream of any particular American institutional feature but of the bureaucratic-anti-racism template wherever it has been installed.

The piece's weak point is its prescription's abstraction. Leonard ends on the classical-liberal formulation that the law "belongs to all and regards all equally" — true as principle, but the operative question is institutional: how do you uninstall race-differential discretion from the police, the schools, the mental-health system, and the courts after a generation of training, hiring, promotion, and case-law has been built around it? Leonard doesn't engage this layer, which leaves the piece in the diagnostic register without the prescriptive teeth its closing line implies. The Mahmood-sentencing-guidelines example points at one mechanism (executive intervention can blunt the worst proposals at the margin), but the deeper apparatus is downstream of judicial doctrine, professional training curricula, and inspection-and-reporting regimes that no single Home Secretary can unwind. The diagnostic the piece offers — that bureaucratic anti-racism produces the same individual-erasing logic the 1970s policing regime did — is correct and useful; the operative question of how to uninstall it gets gestured at rather than addressed.

↩ Back to TOC

‘The Vibes Are Not Good’

Oren Cass · 2026-06-11 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Cass's own perception, from dinner conversations, that conservative-leaning college students hold a distinctive diagnosis of their generation's disaffection — and wanting a public consumption-ready transcript of that diagnosis to feed the American Compass Reclaiming American Citizenship campaign.

The conversation is structured around four prompts: vibe check, dominant vices, radicalization risk, and what's working. Wyatt Olson (frustration with normalized sports gambling: SportsCenter now leads with betting lines; in-game live betting; athletes and commentators as casino partners; prediction markets like Kalshi spreading the gambling logic beyond sports) and Bennett Gorman (the marijuana-vs-alcohol substitution where the active-exploratory vice is replaced by the couch-complacency vice; "goy slop" as the antisemitic-trope-bearing buzzword for activities one knows are unworthy but does anyway) both treat the vices as symptoms of an underlying absence: the loss of "worthy national enterprises" and the lexical disappearance of phrases like "noble endeavor" and "national life." Gorman is direct about the radicalization vector: people are explicitly embracing racial-ethnic supremacism online and in person, treating it as a substitute for the healthy national identity their education taught them to be ashamed of. Olson's positive program is interpersonal (talk to the grocery-store cashier, file out of the self-checkout, join communities), Gorman's is national-purpose-restoration (frontier as the operative metaphor — scientific, medical, space — needing some Trump-mentioned "golden age" vision actualized into program). Cass pushes on the gap between individual self-help advice and any institutional or leader-level vision they've actually seen modeled; both concede they can't name one.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a recruitment piece in conversation-transcript form. The American Compass Reclaiming American Citizenship statement, released June 3, makes "thick citizenship" the load-bearing concept for a productivist-conservative political-economic program (industrial policy, restrictionist immigration, family-formation supports, tech regulation, civic-formation curriculum). The student interview supplies the affective ground-truth Compass needs the statement to land on: real young people on elite campuses who have not been politically organized yet describing the absence the statement diagnoses. Read as the document it is — selected interlocutors who already agree with the magazine's frame, lightly edited by the magazine's editor, published in the magazine — it is not evidence about Gen-Z generally; it is evidence about what a self-conscious post-liberal Compass-aligned subculture is producing as its second-generation rhetoric.

Within that frame, the interview is sharper than it could be because Gorman and Olson decline two adjacent moves they could easily have made. Olson does not turn his gambling-normalization observation into a free-market-bad screed; he just describes the texture of SportsCenter integrating betting lines into highlight reels and lets the reader fill in the productivist critique. Gorman does not blame elite-university anti-American teaching alone for the radicalization vector — he is explicit that the explicit ethno-supremacism he sees in people he knows is what fills the void when there is no positive national identity to commit to, which makes the implicit policy argument (provide a positive national identity, or the abdication will be filled by something far worse) substantially more credible than the standard "campuses are radicalizing students leftward" complaint. The most interesting observation in the piece is Gorman's image of the WWII-allied-vs-axis-soldiers meme captioned to ask "why were they fighting each other when their real enemies were people of different races within their respective nations" — that is a real artifact of a real online radicalization vector, and it is one Compact and Commonplace readers are unlikely to be tracking from the conservative-campus angle.

What the piece does not do, and what would make it analytically stronger, is engage with the strong counter-case that Gen-Z conservative-campus alienation is not in fact distinctively conservative — it is structurally identical to the Haidt-Jonathan-Twenge phone-and-social-media diagnosis (which the Cass companion piece this week links via Patrick T. Brown in the Daily Wire), and would obtain regardless of whether the national-purpose-frontier vocabulary were restored. Olson's appeal to Maslow's hierarchy hints at this — material safety as a prerequisite for higher-order needs, the higher-order needs as a function of belonging-and-meaning that no policy program directly delivers — without quite resolving whether the policy program Cass is selling actually addresses the diagnosis, or just brands it. The frontier metaphor Gorman reaches for is genuinely under-specified: scientific advancement, medical advancement, and space exploration are all currently happening at scale; what is missing is the specific shared-enterprise framing, not the underlying activity. The piece is most usefully read as evidence that the post-liberal political project has student-bench depth and a recognizable affective register, not as evidence that the underlying diagnosis is correct.

↩ Back to TOC

America at 250: creed, culture, imperial impulse

America's Eerily Quiet 250th Birthday

Adam Rowe · 2026-06-08 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The muted character of America's Semiquincentennial year so far, compared with the 1976 bicentennial — and the broader question of whether the Founding still functions as the load-bearing national myth.

Rowe (history professor at New College of Florida) refuses the easy explanation that 2026's quietness reflects polarization and pessimism — those were also present in 1976 (stagflation, Watergate, Saigon evacuation, the Boston busing crisis, Philadelphia Mayor Frank Rizzo requesting 15,000 federal troops he didn't get). The 1976 bicentennial generated more civic energy because Americans across the spectrum were fighting over the Founding, not away from it: anti-busing Boston activists in tricorn hats reenacted the Boston Massacre while pro-busing forces invoked the Declaration's equality clause; Jeremy Rifkin's People's Bicentennial Commission denounced "Buy-Centennial" corporate Tories while citing Jefferson and Paine. Rowe's claim is that the 250th year is structurally different — two rival diminishments of the Founding have displaced the older contested-reverence pattern. On one side (often associated with cultural-right paleoconservatism), the Tocquevillean claim that America is an organic people formed from colonial-era ethnic-cultural inheritance, with the Declaration secondary; on the other (associated with the post-2015 progressive shift), the universal-creed reading wholly abstracted from the specific men who signed it (Frum's "whatever these words meant" formula is the marker). Without a shared revered myth, internal conflicts shift from family-quarrel mode to enemy-camp mode.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Rowe's piece is the central reflection of the America 250 thread in this week's bundle, and its analytical move is the unfashionable one: a Lincolnian closing ("better angels of our nature" is the silent quote behind the final paragraphs) defending shared-myth pieties against both right-organicist and left-universalist erosions. The 1976 historical material does the load-bearing work — it forces the reader to concede that the "we're too divided to celebrate" explanation is dispositive only if 1976 was unified, which it spectacularly wasn't. Rowe's two-cities case study (Philadelphia's Rizzo-era racial-political dysfunction; Boston's busing crisis) is the kind of concrete historical anchor that distinguishes a working historian from a culture-war essayist; the Rifkin People's Bicentennial Commission and the Sons of the American Revolution licensing-fee operation showing up in the same paragraph illustrate the period's fierce contest for the Founding rather than the period's contemporary alternative of contesting against it.

The piece's diagnostic of two-sided diminishment is the operative claim, and Rowe articulates it precisely enough that it can be tested. The "organic people" frame (he names Tocqueville's New England-townships emphasis but the contemporary referent is a strain of paleoconservatism that locates American identity in pre-Revolutionary colonial-ethnic substrate) is critiqued on historical grounds — the colonies had more in common with England than with each other prior to 1776, so loyalty to the Revolution was constitutive of American identity, not derivative from a prior shared culture. The universalist-creed frame (Frum's Atlantic line as exemplar) is critiqued on motivational grounds — a creed disconnected from the courage and sacrifice of the people who fought for it ("bloodless and faceless") cannot function as identity. The structural argument is that the Founding worked as myth precisely because it embodied ideals in action; abstract the ideals from the action and you get either organicist nativism (action without ideals) or schizophrenic universalism (ideals without action). The Daniel Webster / Ralph Waldo Emerson contrast at the close — Webster's preserve-the-inheritance vs. Emerson's emulate-the-founders' creative-destruction — is offered as the legitimate American dilemma the contemporary discourse no longer engages.

The piece's weakness is the asymmetric load it places on the two diminishments. Rowe spends substantial analytical attention on the universalist-creed side (Frum specifically, the post-2015 cultural-left turn implicitly) and much less on the organicist side (Tocqueville is the named anchor but the contemporary organicist current — call it the post-liberal-right or the BAPist-vitalist edge of the New Right — gets a structural critique without a named exemplar). For a New College Florida historian writing in Compact (a venue where the post-liberal-right is a substantial portion of the readership), the discretion is understandable but the analytical cost is real: the piece risks reading as primarily a critique of liberal-progressive abstractionism with the organicist counter-error left implicit. A reader of the Yenor Title-IX piece earlier in the bundle, or the Kuhner Odyssey piece from late May, can fill in the missing exemplar — but the piece's structural symmetry is rhetorically asymmetric in execution. Still, the central insight (that fighting over a shared myth is generative, while abandoning the myth is corrosive) is one the project's roster doesn't otherwise articulate this week, and Rowe's historical apparatus is solid enough to make it stick. The thread is the cleanest single piece on America 250 in the bundle, and the most generative as a frame for adjacent material (the Borovinsky piece on Milei-Argentina-as-laboratory, for instance, reads partly as the inverse question: when a country gains mythic resonance, what's the mechanism?).

↩ Back to TOC

Let's Get Something Clear About America's National Identity

Ross Douthat · 2026-06-09 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Yoni Appelbaum (Atlantic), Noah Smith (Substack), and Neil Gorsuch's recent book — all defending creedal centrism against ethnically/religiously chauvinist alternatives on the right and identitarian alternatives on the left. Smith cites July 2025 polling showing Constitution-support and Declaration-principles read as essential American markers by large majorities of both coalitions; only 2% of Democrats and 8% of Republicans treat "being white" as essential.

Douthat concedes the polling: heritage-American, Anglo-Protestant-inheritance, and "white culture" framings have no demographic future and exist mainly in dynamic mimicry of identity politics on the left. But he argues the creedal frame must learn from the culturalist critique that creedal Americanism is itself a culture — a thicker substrate than formal intellectual assent. He gives two examples from Smith's polling. (1) "Supporting the Constitution" thinly means accepting it as law; thickly it means believing the First Amendment's substantive freedoms (free speech that lets people give offense, sacred things get critiqued, lies get told) are good — which most Americans don't fully accept (Trump doesn't; misinformation-policing progressives don't). Europeans are more comfortable with censorship even where they share liberal-democratic institutions; if Truman had imposed the First Amendment on Western Europe as Marshall-Plan condition, or if every American were magically replaced by an EU citizen, the First Amendment would be interpreted differently. (2) The Declaration grounds equality in theological language ("created equal," rights "endowed by their creator") — Jefferson was not an orthodox Christian but the cultural matrix that made equality operative was religious; the 20th-century Communist commitment to formal equality "meant absolutely nothing" in practice. Eisenhower's "deeply felt religious faith, and I don't care what it is" describes the cultural-matrix dependency. The 50-year test is whether American culture can renew the habits — not just the propositions — that make creedal commitment operative.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Douthat doing his most characteristic move: conceding the empirical loss of the right's culturalist case (the polling really does show heritage-American framings are demographically dead) while extracting the structural critique inside it (a creed without its cultural substrate cannot reproduce itself) and turning that into a constructive program for the center. The piece is doing one specific philosophical move worth naming: it is a soft-MacIntyrean move — virtues and creeds are not reducible to propositions, they require a tradition-embedded community of practice — without invoking MacIntyre by name. The Eisenhower citation does the lifting MacIntyre's "tradition" would do, which is rhetorically savvy given Douthat's NYT audience but worth flagging as the structural move under the prose.

The piece pairs sharply with two other roundup pieces this week. First, Adam Rowe (Compact, "Reclaiming American Citizenship") makes the heavier version of the same case: that consumer-subject identity has replaced "thick" reciprocal citizenship and that policy across education, immigration, industrial, housing, and tech-regulation domains needs to be reorganized around citizenship-as-load-bearing concept. Douthat's framing is thinner (he wants only to defend the creedal-center frame against its culturalist critics, not propose a policy program) but the analytical observation is the same — that the formal commitment cannot be sustained without the substrate. Second, the Sekatuka piece on Uganda's Sovereignty Act and the broader America-250 anniversary discourse give the inverse: those operate from "creedal universalism is itself a colonial-cultural-export" framings that would object to Douthat's whole frame as ethnocentric. Douthat does not engage either; the piece is doing intra-Anglo-American discursive work, which is appropriate to its scope but worth flagging.

The First Amendment example is the strongest single demonstration in the piece — the EU-citizen counterfactual specifically works because it isolates the cultural-matrix variable cleanly (institutional architecture held constant, cultural inheritance varied). The Declaration-theology example is more contestable: Douthat correctly notes Jefferson was not an orthodox Christian but glides over the actual mechanism by which secular-Enlightenment-natural-rights frames (Locke, Paine, the French tradition) produced equality regimes elsewhere. The Communist counter-example is doing rhetorical work the actual historical record cannot quite carry — Soviet egalitarianism failed for many reasons, of which "lacked religious grounding" is at best one. A sharper version of the piece would acknowledge the Eisenhower-religious-matrix claim is a sufficient-but-not-necessary argument about American specifically, not a claim about equality-needs-religion in general.

The "Breviary" section closing (Caitlin Myers and Ezekiel Hooper on the iPhone fertility collapse; Eric Levitz on the AI fertility collapse; Geoff Shullenberger on AI unraveling anti-humanism; Arash Azizi on Fjord, faith and liberalism; Phil Magness on left-academic quote doctoring; Scott Sumner on the true scale of the world; Robert Louis Wilken, R.I.P.) shows the cultural diet feeding the column — particularly the Shullenberger and Azizi pieces, which are operating on the same axis Douthat is working here.

↩ Back to TOC

The Emperor Has No Ludus Magnus

Cullen Murphy · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Trump administration's plan to stage UFC Freedom 250 inside an eight-sided steel-mesh cage in a newly-erected dome on the White House lawn, with seven bouts streamed by Paramount, Trump positioned in an arena-level box as a Roman emperor would have been in his pulvinar.

Cullen Murphy — the longtime Atlantic editor and author of Are We Rome? — writes a Swiftian satire that mock-laments the event's insufficient Roman fidelity. Surface frame: the obvious criticism (event is excess) is wrong; what's striking is its lack of ambition. He runs through three missed opportunities. (1) No Ludus Magnus: ancient arenas had attached gladiator schools where combatants trained; the East Wing's current ruins look strikingly like the Ludus Magnus ruins near the Colosseum and could easily fit dorms, training cages, and trauma bays; Steve Bannon has floated a "gladiator school for culture warriors" idea, and Secretary of Education Linda McMahon (former WWE CEO) is uniquely qualified. (2) No pardon-via-arena: Roman emperors granted pardons after notable performances (Titus freed two gladiators on the Colosseum's opening day; recipients carried symbolic wooden swords for life); Trump has issued ~2,000 pardons but hasn't required any of them — Santos, D'Souza, Flynn, Lil Wayne, Conrad Black, Roger Stone, Blagojevich, January 6 defendants — to compete first. (3) No public-health spinoff: gladiator blood and the oil-and-sweat scraping (strigilis) from combatants were sold as therapeutic; gladiators themselves got the best medical care available (Galen began as a gladiator doctor); given that HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has cast doubt on vaccines and promoted raw milk, "he might well have the kind of open mind that would regard the fluids of cage fighters as beneficial." Closes by noting the inaugural Colosseum games ran for 100 days, vs. the one-day Freedom 250, falling well short of the Trumpian benchmark of "such as the world has never seen."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is satire executed at the high end of the form, and the analytical question is what kind of work the satire is actually doing. The Swift / Modest Proposal register — gravely lamenting that the regime under critique has failed to fully realize a horror it's been merely gesturing at — is a specific rhetorical mode with a specific payoff: it forces the reader to confront the gap between the regime's actual program and its logical extension, in a way that direct criticism wouldn't. Murphy's choice to write the piece this way rather than as a straight column makes the load-bearing argument not "this is bad" (obvious, said elsewhere) but "this is structurally continuous with a much darker program whose endpoints the administration hasn't yet adopted." The Bannon-gladiator-school detail is the load-bearing example — Murphy isn't inventing it as a hypothetical, he's quoting an actual Bannon comment, which lets the satire register as forecasting rather than fabulation.

The piece's strongest move is the McMahon / RFK / January 6 chain — each one specific, each one drawing on a real public stance or biographical fact, each one making the satirical extension uncomfortable because it's not actually that far from the explicit positions held by the named officials. McMahon really is the former WWE CEO and now Secretary of Education; RFK Jr. really has promoted raw milk and rejected mainstream pharmacology; the January 6 pardons really happened. Murphy's satirical pivot is to ask the reader: given these starting positions, what's the principled distinction between the regime's stated program and the Roman extension? The implicit answer — that the distinction is mostly aesthetic, that the regime hasn't pushed to the Roman version because the costume isn't yet quite in style rather than because the underlying logic prohibits it — is the piece's real claim, and it's a substantive one.

The piece's weakest aspect is that it's working a frame that's been heavily used since the first Trump term — Trump-as-Roman-emperor, Are We Rome? (Murphy's own 2007 book), the "decadent late empire" register — and the question is whether this iteration adds enough specificity to justify another pass. Murphy's answer is mostly yes, because the actual UFC-on-the-White-House-lawn event is so specifically Roman-coded (steel dome, octagonal cage, pulvinar-positioned imperial box, opening procession from the Oval Office) that the analogy isn't being imposed; it's been adopted by the administration itself. The piece earns its place by being responsive to a specific event whose imagery the regime chose, not by extending a stale frame.

The Bannon gladiator-school quote deserves note as the piece's most underappreciated factual finding — most readers won't know that Bannon publicly floated a "gladiator school for culture warriors" idea, and Murphy's use of it lets the satire double as quiet reporting. Worth carrying forward as a Bannon-era cultural-program detail.

For the roundup: a polished short satire that works at a higher level than most of its genre, anchored in specific real-world details that make the satirical extensions feel like forecasts rather than gags. Earns a place in the bundle as the week's best execution of the late-empire frame, with the McMahon-RFK-January-6 chain doing real analytical work.

↩ Back to TOC

The Voters Who Believe That Trump Defends Their Values

Katy Osborn · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The "save democracy" framing that has dominated anti-Trump messaging since 2020 and its persistent failure to peel off conservative voters, despite continued framing of Trump as an existential threat to the republic.

Osborn (working with Red Associates, the consultancy / pro-democracy organizations she names) reports on in-depth ethnographic interviews with dozens of conservative Trump voters across three rural / small-town counties (Wyoming, Michigan, South Carolina), including observation of their daily lives, families, and neighbors. Core finding: these voters do revere the constitutional system — Constitution, Electoral College, free and fair elections, rule of law — but they do not see Trump as threatening it; they see federal institutions as having drifted from the values that make them legitimate. Four "bedrock values" emerge: faith (moral authority precedes political authority), family (primary unit of obligation), freedom (mainly from federal overreach), place (local community over national abstraction). The reframe of the central question: not "Should America be a democracy?" but "Has American democracy remained faithful to what makes it legitimate?" The piece illustrates through three named participants — Sarah, 30s, Wyoming mother of three (church fed her family when she was a child; she now homeschools, crowdsourced her son's seizure treatment from online mother networks after neurologists dismissed her, joined a Freedom Caucus chapter; her land-surveyor husband was nearly unemployed under Biden coal restrictions, now overwhelmed with work); Thomas, rural South Carolina ("Democrats see government as their god, while conservatives see their god as God, and government as sort of secondary"); Sam, Michigan small-business owner ("Political norms are just like culture — norms just mean how we have always done things. So I think that's fine to disrupt"); Kyle, 20s Wyoming delivery driver ("I do think he's fighting for everyone right now"); Cindy, 50s SC nurse ("I don't like him as a person, but I like him as a president"). The conclusion: the "defend democracy" frame fails because it defends procedural norms that respondents have already concluded stopped serving the country's bedrock values; the rule-breaking IS the defense.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the kind of small-n ethnographic piece whose value depends entirely on whether the reader treats it as evidence about the conservative electorate or as a vivid portrait of how a specific cluster of conservative voters narrates their own choices. The methodological caveats are large — "dozens" of in-depth interviews across three counties (Wyoming is one of the most heavily Trump-coded states in the country, South Carolina's rural Upstate is similar, the named Michigan counties aren't specified but are clearly rural / small-town) is a sample drawn from the most-MAGA end of the conservative coalition, and the framing piece's findings are essentially the well-known "the people who really like Trump like him for these reasons" portrait. The piece does not, and does not claim to, tell us anything about the marginal Trump voter, the soft-Republican who switched in 2024, the suburban-college-graduate Republican, or the union-Democrat-turned-Trump-voter. Read as a portrait of the deepest base it's competent; read as a guide to "why the save-democracy strategy fails," it does what such a portrait can do, which is identify the position the strategy needs to engage but not measure its prevalence.

The piece's strongest analytical contribution is the reframe of the legitimacy question. The shift from "Should America be a democracy" to "Has American democracy remained faithful to what makes it legitimate" is the key move, and it's substantively important because it identifies a position that anti-Trump messaging has consistently failed to engage. The Democrats-defending-institutions-that-Trump-voters-already-distrust dynamic has been documented in polling (Pew's trust-in-government series, which the piece cites, shows trust at historic lows across the board but with sharply diverging values about what restored trust would look like). The Thomas quote — "Democrats see government as their god, while conservatives see their god as God, and government as sort of secondary" — names a real divergence in implicit theology of the state that has structural implications for political strategy.

The piece's weakest move is the conflation of conservative-voter self-description with neutral analysis. The Sarah portrait is the load-bearing example, and it has a specific structural shape: church saved her, public institutions failed her (school, neurologists, COVID policy), Trump restored her family's economic life by reversing coal restrictions. This is the conservative voter's own narrative of her own life, reported with no contextualizing about whether the dismissed-neurologists framing was accurate (was the seizure-treatment-from-online-mothers actually safer / better than the medical recommendation?), whether the coal-restriction story holds (Biden-era policy specifics, the macro picture of Wyoming coal-industry employment trends), or whether the COVID urban-policy framing accurately describes what arrived in her rural town. The piece's commitment to "not to persuade or judge" is methodologically defensible — they are interviewing voters, not fact-checking them — but the reader has to do their own work to distinguish "this is how these voters experience their lives" from "this is what actually happened." The piece would be stronger if it acknowledged the gap between subject-narrated experience and external verification rather than presenting subject-narration as standalone evidence.

The reframe-of-democracy claim deserves engagement as the load-bearing analytical contribution. The piece argues that the "defend democracy" strategy is failing because it asks voters to defend procedures they no longer see as load-bearing. The alternative implied — defending the substantive content (the values, communities, and institutions Trump voters care about) — would mean Democrats articulating a positive vision for what federal institutions should be doing that responds to the faith / family / freedom / place values rather than dismissing them. The piece doesn't go this far — it ends on the diagnostic note — but the implication is structurally interesting: an anti-Trump strategy that engaged the legitimacy-of-institutions question on its merits, rather than the constitutional-norms question, would have to articulate what a federal apparatus loyal to community-and-faith-grounded values looks like. That's a hard project and Democrats have not done it; the piece is most useful as identifying the project rather than executing it.

For the roundup: well-executed qualitative reportage with appropriate methodological caveats, useful as a portrait of the conservative position the "defend democracy" strategy has failed to engage, but limited in what it tells us about the broader electorate. The right read in the synthesis is to treat the reframe-of-legitimacy as the substantive contribution and the named-participant material as illustration, while holding the methodology in mind as bounding what can be inferred.

↩ Back to TOC

The Republic Strikes Back

Katherine Thompson · 2026-06-07 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The disconnect between the 2025 Trump-administration National Security Strategy (NSS) and 2026 National Defense Strategy (NDS) — which Thompson reads as genuinely realist-restraint documents prioritizing homeland defense and the First Island Chain — and the actual operational record of the second year of Trump II, especially the Iran war, Western-Hemisphere interventionism, and the Prioritized Ukraine Requirements List (PURL) initiative.

Thompson sets up three tenets she identifies as the core republican-statecraft tradition: (1) Washington's farewell-address disposition against permanent foreign entanglement; (2) John Quincy Adams's 1821 "she goes not abroad, in search of monsters to destroy" — start within borders, look incrementally outward, the republic as the core from which the strategic environment ripples; (3) Madison's letter to Jefferson on the war power vested in the legislature precisely because the executive is most prone to war. She then traces the thirty-year "unipolar moment" departure: GWOT (Global War on Terror) interventions with no exit strategies, no congressional reauthorizations until the 2018-19 Yemen vote, NATO accumulating 14 new members since 2000 with allied underinvestment shifting cost to the U.S., Ukraine support escalating without treaty obligation or congressional war vote, treaties-by-executive-fiat (Paris, JCPOA). She credits the NSS and NDS for accomplishing a doctrinal course correction (rebalanced allies, revitalized defense-industrial base, China-First-Island-Chain prioritization, looking from homeland outward). The argument's load-bearing claim is the gap between this doctrine and implementation: the Iran war consumes munitions, naval assets, intelligence, and political bandwidth at exactly the moment the doctrine says these must be preserved for higher-order Indo-Pacific priorities; the U.S. is already bumping European allies out of the queue for high-value munitions Europe intended to buy for Ukraine because of Iran-war expenditure; the Western-Hemisphere pivot is correct in principle but reverting to interventionism-and-regime-change in practice; PURL conceptually aligns with burden-shifting but exacerbates the defense-industrial-base bottleneck because the base lacks structural integrity for simultaneous U.S. and European demand. The closer is a call for "ruthless discipline" — the republic can survive difficult tradeoffs but not more of the same imperial decision-making rebranded under improved doctrine.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Thompson is writing inside the project's "republic-not-empire" thread, but doing distinctive work on the inside of it. The conventional version of this argument from the Quincy Institute / American Conservative / Compact-restraint pole describes the unipolar-moment departure and calls for return; Thompson's version takes the NSS and NDS at face value as completing the doctrinal return, and locates the failure precisely at the implementation layer. This is a more demanding analytical position: it requires her to specify which operational decisions show the reversion and to identify the binding constraint that produced them. Her three examples are well chosen: the Iran war is the strongest single case because the contradiction between "Indo-Pacific homeland-defense priority" and "prolonged Iran conflict consuming munitions and naval assets" is documented in the queue-bumping of European allies; the Western-Hemisphere case is the second strongest because it identifies a real risk (correct priority, wrong instruments) the more credulous restraint writers tend to wave through; the PURL case is the most novel because it surfaces a structural problem with restraint-coded policy (burden-shifting to allies presupposes industrial-base capacity that does not exist) that the restraint camp has generally not engaged with.

The piece's strongest move, analytically, is naming the disposition rather than blaming individuals: "the gravitational pull toward empire did not disappear simply because policymakers began announcing republic-centric doctrine." This is the load-bearing observation. Doctrine documents are written by intellectually-coherent staff and announce what the administration intends; operational decisions are made under crisis pressure by people with thirty years of imperial-instinct training and with bureaucratic structures designed to deliver imperial responses. Thompson is naming the institutional-bureaucratic-cultural inertia explicitly, which connects her piece to Klippenstein's archival work on the post-9/11 statutory architecture (the DNI gutting in 2004), Lind's "American Way of War" diagnosis, and the broader project finding that "every prescribed remedy is one the prescriber concedes their own diagnosis makes structurally unavailable." Thompson's prescribed remedy — "ruthless discipline" — is the same kind: she names it as what we lack, calls for it, and acknowledges that the political-bureaucratic structure that should produce it does not. The most-honest read of her closer is that she does not actually expect Trump II to deliver, but is writing the document that names what the failure was when, in three or six years, it becomes uncontested that the doctrine was sound and the implementation was not.

The piece could be sharper on the political-economy mechanism. The reason Iran-war expenditure bumps European allies out of the munitions queue is not just "we're using a lot of munitions" — it is that decades of consolidation in the U.S. defense-industrial base have produced a small number of bottleneck-prime contractors who cannot ramp production on the timescales modern conflicts require (the Patriot-vs-Shahed cost-asymmetry Fukuyama documented; the artillery-shell production constraints that drove the Ukraine surge problems). Thompson names "structural integrity" as the missing element but does not work the supply-side political economy in detail, which would have connected her piece to the Cass-Stoller industrial-policy critique of monopoly-driven defense procurement. That is a missing-link piece of analysis that would have made the implementation-failure diagnosis substantially more constructive. Still, this is a serious contribution to the project's Iran-as-system-level-failure thread, and is the cleanest single-piece exposition this week of the post-Iran-war strategic-realism position. The piece's value is in operating at the institutional-design layer rather than the personality-and-deal-progress layer the press is stuck on.

↩ Back to TOC

Dwight Macdonald's American Century

Geoff Shullenberger · 2026-06-12 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The new anthology Atrocities of the Mind: Essays on Violence and Politics in the American Century (ed. John Summers, foreword by Andrew Bacevich), reviewed against the backdrop of cross-partisan nostalgia for the 1940-1970 "American century" (the Mad Men mood, "this is what they took from you" memes).

Shullenberger reads Dwight Macdonald — the Partisan Review defector who founded the small magazine Politics (1944-49), launched C. Wright Mills and Paul Goodman, and later wrote "Masscult and Midcult" at The New Yorker — as a "bracing antidote" to that nostalgia. Macdonald opposed US entry into WWII, denounced Hiroshima and the Allied bombing campaigns as morally equivalent to Axis atrocities, "chose the West" only tepidly in the Cold War, and rejoined the antiwar fight under the New Left over Vietnam. The unifying thread, Shullenberger argues, is not anti-imperialist ideology but a republican-Jeffersonian objection to bigness — to "the horror of vast technological power exerted in war-making" and to mass-culture "Masscult / Midcult" as the same dehumanizing automatism in a different register. Shullenberger concedes his own habitual hostility to anti-bigness politics (he reads neo-Jeffersonian decentralization rhetoric as cover for the Reagan-through-dotcom consolidation of wealth) but argues mechanized war is spreading again, today's loudest moralists are realists in disguise, and Macdonald's idea of the "little magazine" as a refuge for human individual presence is the operative inheritance — a self-positioning gesture for Compact itself.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the third Shullenberger Compact piece in roughly six weeks to use a midcentury or near-midcentury figure as a frame-borrowing device: Vargas Llosa (Compact Substack, 2026-06-06) on whether the novel can articulate the political nation in an age of popular-music kitsch; Ivan Illich (Compact, 2026-05-30) as the unacknowledged vocabulary behind Pope Leo XIV's AI encyclical; and now Macdonald as the rejoinder to Mad Men-era American-greatness nostalgia. The standing register is intellectual rehabilitation in the service of supplying Compact — and the post-liberal/heterodox-right milieu it speaks to — with a usable past. Shullenberger is Compact's managing editor, and the closing line ("little magazines can still serve as a refuge for human individuals in a new age of cultural and political automatism") is openly a self-positioning move, with Politics's five-year run and 5,000-copy circulation as the operating template.

What's distinctive about this piece is the unusually frank concession in the middle. Shullenberger admits "I often find myself hostile to this sort of anti-bigness stance" and reads the New Left → Reagan → dotcom arc as a "debased neo-Jeffersonian coalition" whose appeals to "decentralization, localism, and individual freedom provided cover for the consolidation of wealth and power in the hands of the few." That is not the standard Compact line — it edges close to the Stoller/anti-monopoly critique of pseudo-decentralization as oligarchic cover, even as Stoller himself would frame the villain as monopoly rather than bigness-per-se. Shullenberger then recovers the Macdonald position by relocating it: not anti-bigness as policy prescription, but anti-automatism as moral diagnosis. The republican-tradition gloss (Jefferson; Bacevich's foreword; the Chronicles and American Conservative appropriation of Macdonald) does real work here — it lets Shullenberger bracket the policy-coalition history of "small government" rhetoric and keep the moral diagnosis intact.

The strongest analytic move is the line that "the most intensely moralizing observers of present-day conflicts are, at bottom, also realists for whom moralistic appeals are a way to pursue a politics of competitive victimhood." This generalizes Macdonald's complaint about his ex-Partisan Review colleagues' "political realism" charge into a structural critique of contemporary discourse on Ukraine, Gaza, and Iran — without naming any of those wars. It is the literal operative claim of the piece (read in the context of Compact's post-Iran-war coverage cluster, where Vighi, Fazi, Rojas, and Schmitz have been doing variations on the same anti-Atlanticist move). Whether the analogy holds depends on whether one accepts that today's pro-Ukraine or pro-Israel moralism is, like the Partisan Review hawks' 1942 turn, fundamentally a "their war and their country" identification dressed in universal language. Shullenberger doesn't argue the case; he gestures toward it through the historical parallel and lets the reader do the work.

What the piece does less well is reckon with the disanalogy. Macdonald could oppose WWII from the left in 1941 because the alternative coalition he wanted to lead — anti-Stalinist, anti-fascist, anti-American-empire — had a recognizable shape, even at 5,000 subscribers. The "moral stance without an investment in which side prevails" that Shullenberger praises in Macdonald is harder to occupy when, as he himself notes, every contemporary antiwar critic is suspected of (and frequently is) playing for one of the existing camps. The Macdonald template — small magazine, individual moral presence, refusal of mass-political affiliation — is being offered as the answer to that problem, but the piece doesn't ask whether the template scales beyond the writer's own byline. The Macdonald-as-self-positioning move is the move; the analysis of whether the move can work for Compact is left implicit.

Anchor: "Götterdämmerung without the gods" is Macdonald's phrase for the technologically mechanized nuclear apocalypse — Wagner's "twilight of the gods" stripped of the metaphysical grandeur that gave the German original its meaning. The line carries the whole essay's argument in miniature: not that the midcentury produced horror (every era does), but that it produced horror as automatism, without authorship, without anyone to confront.

↩ Back to TOC

Niall Ferguson: The World Cup Looks Like a Fiesta of Nationalism. Don’t Be Fooled.

Niall Ferguson · 2026-06-12 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The World Cup's opening on June 11, 2026 — co-hosted by the U.S., Canada, and Mexico — and the persistent American indifference to soccer relative to NBA finals and Stanley Cup playoffs (5% of Americans rank soccer as favorite spectator sport per Gallup vs. 41% for football).

Ferguson's "Things Worth Remembering" column reads Hornby's 1992 memoir Fever Pitch as the definitive text on football fandom as addiction and self-harm rather than entertainment. He weaves autobiography — Glasgow childhood (Rangers Protestant, Celtic Catholic, Partick Thistle for atheists), conversion to Arsenal after moving south, watching his youngest son Campbell weep when Gabriel missed the penalty in Arsenal's Champions League final loss to PSG just weeks after Arsenal won their first Premier League in 22 years — into a thesis that football is "primal, tribal," organized around club not country, and structurally about coping with defeat as preparation for life. He cites Bill Shankly's "more important than life and death" line and Benedict Anderson's "imagined communities" to argue the World Cup's national-team format is actually a thinner experience than club football. The closing twist: the British Empire's two legacies (per Aden's penultimate governor) were Association Football and the expression "Fuck off"; the World Cup is the empire's final joke on the rebellious American republic, replacing the pursuit of happiness with entertainment as pain.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the kind of piece where the right critical question isn't "is it correct" but "what is it doing in the roundup at all" — a literary essay on sport-as-religion published in a window where the news corpus is dominated by the U.S.-Iran war, an LA federalization-of-the-National-Guard crisis, a Maine Senate primary upset, and AI-and-jobs anxiety. The honest answer is that Ferguson's "Things Worth Remembering" column is a regular FP rotation and gets covered when it runs, not because the World Cup is a structural issue for the project. The piece is well-executed: the Hornby reading is generous and accurate (Hornby's central claim is that fandom is a form of inherited unfreedom — "I didn't choose Arsenal, Arsenal chose me" — and Ferguson hits this without belaboring it), the Glasgow texture is autobiographical capital used efficiently, the Gabriel-penalty scene with Campbell crying functions as the piece's emotional load-bearing image, and the closing "British Empire's last joke on the republic" inversion is genuinely funny.

The piece's intellectually interesting move is the gap between club and country. Ferguson argues — and it's a position serious football writers have held for decades — that World Cup nationalism is a thin imitation of club fandom rather than its apotheosis. The cosplay charge is real: Arsenal fans hate Tottenham fans with structural intensity; England-vs.-Germany is a quadrennial pageant where most actual football fans feel less invested than they do in any random Premier League fixture. This is the kind of internal-discourse observation that doesn't translate well across the Atlantic, where Americans default to the framing that sport-nationalism IS football fandom rather than its degraded form. Ferguson is right that the World Cup format flattens what makes football meaningful — and his closing inversion (the World Cup as the empire's punishment of the republic) lets him hold a position that's simultaneously pro-football and anti-World-Cup, which is the genuinely heterodox stance.

The piece's weakest aspect is the unexamined assumption that football fandom is masculine-tribal and should be. Ferguson notes that the crowd at Hornby's first Arsenal match was "tens of thousands, nearly all men" and treats this as a feature rather than a historical artifact. The piece's "through football we learn not to cry / through football we learn how to converse with strangers / through football we learn to deal with death and divorce" sequence reads as a Ferguson-typical move: the working-class male socialization apparatus of mid-century Britain reinterpreted as a universal pedagogy. It's not wrong, but the universalism is doing rhetorical work the piece doesn't acknowledge.

For the roundup, this piece reads as a Ferguson cultural-criticism set piece, executed at his usual register, with no significant claim that affects the larger news cycle. The right read in the synthesis is to note it briefly as the FP rotation entry, treat the club-vs.-country distinction as the substantive contribution worth tagging, and not over-extend the analysis. It's the kind of piece that earns its place in a long-form bundle by being well-written and self-contained, not by being load-bearing.

↩ Back to TOC

Liberal institutions, state capacity, and the West's working architecture

A Liberal Vision For Europe

Francis Fukuyama · 2026-06-09 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Marco Rubio's February 2026 Munich Security Conference speech (which received a standing ovation for framing US-Europe ties via "shared history, Christian faith, culture, heritage, language, ancestry"), the Vance "heritage Americans" formulation, Pope Leo XIV's recent AI encyclical Magnifica Humanitas, and Trump's January 2026 declaration after capturing Maduro that he is constrained only by "my own morality."

This is a long-form speech (delivered as the Erhard Busek Memorial Lecture at the Europe's Futures Retreat on Cres, Croatia) that draws together several Fukuyama threads from prior columns into a single argument with three movements. First: Western Civilization can be read either as a Christian-heritage civilization (Rubio's frame, which Fukuyama reads as a deliberate move toward "Christian faith" rather than "Christian heritage" and as ethnocultural via Vance's "heritage Americans"), or as a post-Enlightenment liberal civilization rooted in the historical compromise that pushed religion into private life after the 150-year post-Reformation religious wars and in the separation of empirical inquiry from religious dogma that made modern natural science possible. Fukuyama acknowledges the genuine Christian root (Galatians 3:28 as the textual ancestor of universal human equality) but argues the only viable basis for contemporary European identity is the second understanding. Second: the trans-Atlantic alliance is not recoverable even if Trump is succeeded by a more conventional president, because the Republican Party itself has been transformed from a free-trade, alliance-defending, limited-government party to an America-First nationalist party whose voter base has been changed beyond recognition; any future Europe-friendly Democratic president could be followed by a similarly nationalistic Republican, so European trust in American commitments cannot be reconstructed. Third: AI compounds this, raising two distinct dangers — first, that frontier-AI capability concentrates in the US and China (Europe has no path to competitive frontier labs and only US/Chinese data-center bases can power leading models), creating inter-state and intra-state inequality; second, that AI is being developed by private "technocracy" (Pope Leo XIV's term) whose values are non-transparent and whose operators (Musk via Starlink as the named exhibit, granting and revoking Ukrainian battlefield connectivity) function as independent foreign-policy actors. Fukuyama acknowledges that neither government regulation nor nationalization is a clean solution. He closes on a deliberately upbeat note: elections still work (the recent Hungarian rejection of Orbán; expected Democratic gains in the 2026 US midterms; the Trump-as-unifying-glue-of-European-far-right thesis hasn't panned out), and a bipartisan US backlash against unbridled AI development is growing.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Fukuyama is doing his most political-essayistic work here — this is a speech-as-policy-essay rather than an analytical-academic paper, and it is intentionally bringing together themes ("dont-define-western-civilization" from earlier; "world simply does not trust America"; "what the pope is telling us") for a European audience that needed the synthesis. The argumentative structure is clean: identify the frame-contestation (Christian-civilizational vs. Enlightenment-liberal), make the case that only one frame is available to Europe given who Europe actually is, then deliver the unwelcome conclusion that the American partner in the alliance has structurally exited.

The piece's strongest move is the irretrievability argument. Fukuyama is not making the easy point that Trump is bad and Europe should hedge until 2028; he is making the considerably harder point that the Republican Party's voter coalition has been rebuilt around an ideology — America-First nationalism — that is structurally incompatible with the alliance, and that future Republican presidents will face the same incentive structure regardless of personal disposition. That is the genuine policy implication for European defense planning and it is correct. The corollary — that Europe must build a coherent identity-basis for its own continuation — is what motivates the Enlightenment-liberalism frame.

The piece is in direct conversation with several others in this corpus and prior. Most obviously with Fukuyama's own "Why we need judicial independence" (one-liner #64 last week) — the present essay is the political-philosophy companion to last week's institutional-history argument, and the two together form a Fukuyama mini-arc on the institutional and ideological preconditions of liberal modernity. It is in argumentative tension with Damon Linker's "Welcome to the postmodern presidency" (one-liner #63 last week) — Linker argues that Trump's second term vindicates the Bloomian warning about postmodern Nietzscheanism overwhelming liberal politics, while Fukuyama is arguing that the same institutional ground can still be defended. It is in cousin-relationship with Sam Kahn's "Why Trump Seems Confused by His Own War" (this week, Persuasion) — Kahn is parsing the same realism-doctrine question that Fukuyama implicitly invokes when he frames the Western inheritance as universalist-rights ideology against the Rubio-Miller realist turn.
