And it touches Mchangama's free-speech piece (this week) on what European liberal institutions are choosing to be: Mchangama indicts the European Court of Human Rights for betraying free-speech protections; Fukuyama indirectly assumes European institutions remain capable of defending the Enlightenment inheritance — an assumption Mchangama's reporting weakens.

The under-developed move is the AI-as-second-front argument. Fukuyama correctly identifies the Musk-Starlink case as a paradigmatic private-foreign-policy moment, but the encyclical-citation chain (Leo XIV's Magnifica Humanitas and Leo XIII's Rerum Novarum) is doing more rhetorical work than analytical work. The actual argument — that private-developer technocracy values are non-transparent, non-democratic, and capable of unilateral international intervention — is correct, but Fukuyama doesn't engage the European-policy response options (Mario Draghi's productivity report; the EU AI Act's compute thresholds; whether public-option frontier models à la Ezra Klein's recent proposal could be a European rather than American move). The optimistic closing arms are the thinnest part: the Hungarian-election reading is one election, the predicted US midterm Democratic recovery is undefended, and the "AI backlash" reference is gestural.

Carry-forward: Fukuyama is in the process of constructing what may become this year's project-defining account of Western institutional crisis — judicial independence (last week), Enlightenment-identity basis (this week), the AI governance front (closing of this week). The three together describe what he takes to be the institutional preconditions of liberal modernity and what is currently corroding each. The synthesis composer should track whether subsequent Fukuyama pieces extend the trilogy (suspects: bureaucratic-Weberian-state institutions; or universal-suffrage-electoral institutions next).

↩ Back to TOC

The European Court of Human Rights Is Betraying Its Purported Values

Jacob Mchangama · 2026-06-08 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) ruling against Irakli Miladze, a Tbilisi food courier who was fined €180 (later upheld) after a profane November 2022 TikTok video accusing Georgian city-hall, ministry, and State Security Service personnel of using bus lanes with tinted-window impunity while delivery drivers got fined for the same conduct.

Mchangama frames the case as a betrayal of the ECHR's own Handyside precedent that free expression protects ideas that "offend, shock or disturb." The seven-judge panel upheld the fine on three grounds Mchangama parses critically: that the video's profanity was "wanton denigration" devoid of informational value (Mchangama: it carried substantive critique of class-coded enforcement); that public officials may enjoy special protection against verbal abuse (a 19th-century European reactionary trope used historically to silence radicals demanding universal suffrage and religious freedom); and that the platform mechanics of online speech (TikTok's "rapid algorithmic amplification and particularly high youth engagement") warrant stricter regulation, treating reach as an aggravating factor. The court accepted the Georgian state's characterization of the video as "violent verbal aggression" — even though no threats, intimidation, or incitement to actual violence were present. Mchangama contrasts this with Hungary's recent Tisza-party two-thirds-supermajority defeat of Orbán: corruption stories breaking on small online outlets; Magyar's career launched via a viral YouTube interview; social media routing around Fidesz propaganda. He notes the current Georgian context: a Putin-aligned government cracking down on protest, civil society, and media; the Georgian Young Lawyers Association calling out a "severe human rights crisis" with administrative-offense proceedings on insult grounds now "routine for the Interior Ministry." His closer is that Europe is "sleepwalking ever deeper into a free speech recession, guided by the very institutions supposed to sound the alarm."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Mchangama doing classic Future-of-Free-Speech-Institute work: take a specific case ruling, read its actual reasoning carefully, identify the doctrinal moves, and trace the policy implications. The piece's strongest analytical move is identifying that the ECHR is treating reach as an aggravating factor — the more people heard the criticism, the more punishable it becomes — which inverts the democratic logic that fierce criticism of public officials matters more, not less, when it reaches more citizens. The 19th-century parallel (the same official-dignity arguments were used to silence universal-suffrage advocates) is doing real historical work; Mchangama is correctly pointing out that "you may speak but only respectfully toward those in power" was the doctrinal terrain of pre-democratic Europe.

The Hungary contrast is the piece's most politically consequential move. Magyar's Tisza-party victory is recent and was largely driven by small online outlets and viral social media — exactly the channels the ECHR is endorsing tighter regulation of. The argument structure here is: if you support the ECHR's logic, you must also condemn the channels by which Orbán was defeated, which most ECHR-supporters do not actually want to do. That is a strong reductio.

Two structural caveats worth flagging. First, Mchangama is operating from a specific free-speech-maximalist tradition (his book The Future of Free Speech with Jeff Kosseff is the canonical statement); readers should know that his framing treats any state-imposed cost on speech as a betrayal of the Handyside standard, while many European jurists view Handyside as setting a floor (the state may exceed it). The ECHR ruling, on its own terms, is arguing that the floor is met and the state's chosen restriction proportionate. Mchangama does engage this — the "proportionality" rejoinder gets the explicit response that the principle of free speech has been eroded regardless of fine size — but readers should know that the disagreement is partly about which standard applies. Second, the Miladze video reportedly included extensive profanity directed at unnamed security officers; the ECHR's "targeted and degrading attacks on identifiable individuals" framing is doing work, and Mchangama's "no threats, no intimidation, no incitement" defense is correct on the criminal-law dimension but doesn't fully engage with the civil-defamation-or-harassment dimension the court was operating in.

Cross-piece resonance: this is the project's first piece this year that places the European-institutional retreat from free-speech-protections at the center of the frame, and it pairs interestingly with Fukuyama's Enlightenment-defense piece this same week (Fukuyama assumes European institutions still defend the Enlightenment inheritance; Mchangama shows them retreating from it on the actual ground of speech regulation). It also pairs with Tara Henley's Canadian-graves-story piece this same week, which is a longer-term meditation on what happens when journalism abandons objectivity-as-norm; both pieces are about institutional self-betrayal in the name of nominally-protective doctrines. The Henley and Mchangama pieces together form a useful Persuasion-week thread on Western liberal-institutional decay.

Carry-forward: track whether the ECHR ruling becomes a precedent for further "reach as aggravating factor" rulings against online speech in EU member states; if so, the Hungary scenario Mchangama gestures at (state-aligned media plus restrictive online-speech enforcement) becomes the modal European case rather than the Hungarian anomaly.

↩ Back to TOC

A “Failure of Journalism” in Canada

Tara Henley · 2026-06-12 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The May 2026 Globe and Mail belated reckoning ("what if all of it was dedicated to 215 burials that don't exist?") and the Globe editorial board's characterization of the five-year coverage as a "failure of journalism," approaching the fifth anniversary of the May 27, 2021 Tk'emlúps te Secwépemc announcement.

Henley's reconstruction: the 2021 Tk'emlúps press release claimed "confirmation of the remains of 215 children" at Kamloops Indian Residential School based on ground-penetrating radar (GPR); the NYT introduced the "mass grave" framing globally; Trudeau called it "a painful reminder" of a "dark and shameful chapter"; statues fell, two dozen churches burned, flags were at half-mast for five months, the Pope eventually apologized. The problem: GPR cannot confirm remains without excavation, and Tk'emlúps has not excavated. At a July 2021 press conference, GPR specialist Sarah Beaulieu publicly walked the claim back to "probable burials" / "targets of interest" — but Canadian press largely missed or buried the correction. Henley situates the failure in a weakened press corps (fewer than 11,000 journalists covering Canada, federal subsidies, two decades of business-model crisis), an "obsession with American media" that imported Margaret Sullivan / Jim Rutenberg / Jay Rosen "state of exception" Trump-era arguments for suspending standards, a 2020-21 internal CBC push against objectivity-as-norm ("Objectivity Is a Privilege Afforded to White Journalists" / National Magazine Award), and an illiberal enforcement culture that punished dissent — Terry Glavin's 5,500-word National Post exposé in May 2022 was denounced as "residential school denialism" by academic organizations and ultimately classified that way in a 2023 RCMP threat-assessment report. The destructive feedback loop: the press's information vacuum let cynics like BC politician Dallas Brodie call it "the greatest lie in Canadian history" and a Senate human-rights committee voted (rejected at the Senate proper, but 41-32) on a bill making "residential school denialism" punishable by up to two years in prison. The recovery move Henley credits is by Aaron Pete, Chief of Chawathil First Nation, whose Nuanced podcast did the actual investigative work the national press would not.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Henley doing thoroughly-reported press-criticism work, and the piece is documentary in a way that the project benefits from having on the roster. The strongest single move is the Sarah Beaulieu walk-back at the July 2021 press conference — that is the load-bearing fact the piece adduces, because if the radar specialist herself publicly retracted "confirmation of remains" within six weeks of the original announcement, and the national press did not adjust its coverage, the diagnosis of objectivity-norm collapse has its smoking gun.

The piece's structural argument — that the press corps abandonment of objectivity-as-norm during 2020-21 created the conditions for this specific reporting failure — is well-supported by the documentary evidence Henley assembles (CBC internal-memo language, the Walrus essay, the CBC Radio sub-headline). But it is also doing more political work than its empirical reach quite justifies: the failure to follow up on the Beaulieu walk-back is real, but the broader claim that "objectivity-norm abandonment" caused it requires more than the documents Henley cites can establish on their own. It is at least equally plausible that the failure was institutional risk-aversion in the face of indigenous-political sensitivity (Henley quotes Harrison Lowman: "holes that could be poked in this story, but that you should definitely not be the one poking them") and that the objectivity-norm debate was a parallel rather than causally upstream phenomenon.

The piece's strongest analytical contribution is the demonstration of how doctrinal feedback loops calcify: Glavin's denunciation in 2022 → academic-organization "denialism" framing → RCMP 2023 threat-assessment classification → Senate-committee-passed criminalization bill → near-passage at the full Senate (41-32). That is a chain of institutional self-reinforcement worth flagging as a general phenomenon — when a contested empirical question gets routed through human-rights and security institutions before the empirical question is settled, the legal-criminalization endpoint forecloses the eventual evidence-based resolution. The Aaron Pete contrast — a Chawathil First Nation chief whose grandmother attended St. Mary's and suffered abuse, and who did the actual investigative work via podcast interviews — is the piece's resolution: the people most positioned to be entitled to the original narrative were also positioned, when curious in good faith, to do the work the institutional press would not.

Cross-piece resonance: this pairs naturally with Mchangama's free-speech piece this week — both are about Western liberal institutions abandoning their nominal protective functions while invoking those functions as cover (the ECHR claims to defend speech while restricting it; the Canadian press claims to defend truth while abandoning objectivity). The two together substantiate a Persuasion-week thread on Western liberal-institutional self-betrayal. It also resonates with Geoff Shullenberger's Compact piece on Vargas Llosa and populist novel-form (last week #15) — both are arguing about what happens when journalistic/literary objectivity-as-norm gives way to populist or activist frameworks. And it should be tracked against the Aurelien "Democracy" piece (one-liner #79 from May 30): both critique Western institutional self-betrayal but from opposite directions — Aurelien from a left-anti-liberal frame, Henley from a liberal-objectivity-norm frame.

Carry-forward: the Henley-flagged Senate bill criminalizing "residential school denialism" deserves tracking — even though it was rejected at the full Senate, the close 41-32 vote means it is likely to return; if it passes, Canada becomes a notable case study in formal criminalization of contested empirical claims via human-rights framings.

↩ Back to TOC

People Are Strange.

Aurelien · 2026-06-10 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: His own prior essay on the gulf between Western elites and ordinary people, plus the continuing crises in Ukraine and Iran — and the broader question of why Western political-class beliefs remain stable across decades of contrary evidence.

Aurelien extends his standing critique of the "Professional and Managerial Caste" (PMC) by adding an explicitly psychological layer to the structural-procedural diagnosis. He begins with the procedural: today's senior decision-makers came of age in the post-Cold-War window with no other reference frame; the overproduction of graduates, the proliferation of NGOs, the disappearance of inter-party ideological distinction, the cross-marriage and overlap of government / media / NGOs / intelligence / courts produce a near-total homogeneity within a transnational PMC speaking globalized English. From this he moves to the historical: the West got two things wrong at the end of the Cold War (that the Cold War caused the world's crises, and that the West "won" by virtue of system-superiority) and constructed a normative ideology with vague gestures toward "freedom," inflexible and doctrinaire, that has only hardened. The bulk of the essay then sits in the psychological register: Realist IR theory wrongly assumes states act rationally; in fact decisions are made by individuals with personal histories, jealousies, and fragile egos. He introduces "Apophenia" (the tendency to see patterns that aren't there) as a diagnostic for conspiracy-mindedness in political analysis, with Anatoly Golitsyn (the KGB defector whose all-encompassing Soviet-deception theory was unfalsifiable) as the case study. He invokes Freud on non-declarative memory (early-childhood absorption of how-the-world-is, proof against later evidence) to argue that domineering-parent upbringings predispose people toward Hegemony-and-Empire frameworks while repression-and-secrecy upbringings predispose toward hidden-power conspiracies. The load-bearing claim is "Ego Death" — the institutional-status loss that follows public dissent is structurally equivalent to ego death, and this, more than ideology or careerism, is the binding constraint on realism about Ukraine and Iran.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Aurelien doing his characteristic move — wide-aperture diagnosis of Western elite incapacity — but with an explicit pivot from structural-procedural arguments (his usual register) to psychological ones. The pivot is partly forced: he has now written enough essays diagnosing the PMC structurally that the marginal essay needs a new vector. Psychology is the available one, and it does some real work, but it also lets him reach more impressionistic conclusions than the structural arguments could carry.

The strongest part of the essay is the early procedural-historical scaffolding. The "everyone they meet, everyone they work with, everyone they socialise with, probably operates within the same limited intellectual perimeter" passage is genuinely sharp on the homogenization of the transnational PMC, and the observation that "two European Ministers... discussing Ukraine... virtually the whole of the conversation would amount to agreement that (1) we must keep up the pressure on Putin and (2) we must do more to help Ukraine" is the kind of operative claim that would be falsifiable if anyone wanted to test it — and that's largely consistent with what reporting on European foreign-policy councils does show.

The end-of-Cold-War historical argument is also useful: the West's twin errors (that the Cold War caused the periodic crises; that the West won) are genuinely identifiable, and the consequence — that the West proceeded as though Liberal-libertarian ideas were the "Hegelian final chapter" and all that remained was to apply them — is a defensible characterization of 1990s-2000s foreign policy. The Afghanistan-as-free-fire-zone-for-every-normative-crusade observation lands.

Where the essay becomes weaker is in the psychological scaling-up. The "Ego Death" framing is the operative claim and it's evocative but underspecified — it explains why an individual politician won't dissent, but it doesn't explain why an entire generation of dissenters can't form. The Golitsyn case is well-told but does specific work: it's a case study of apophenia that conveniently flatters the readers most likely to find Aurelien (skeptics of the contemporary Russia-collusion / hybrid-warfare narrative) without committing him to defending the implication that all such narratives are apophenic. The Freud-on-non-declarative-memory citation is the weakest piece — it lets Aurelien speculate that hegemony-thinkers had domineering parents and conspiracy-thinkers had secret-keeping parents, with no operational way to test the claim, while gesturing toward the kind of biographical-determinist reading that, applied symmetrically, would dismiss most of his own readership as projecting parental dynamics onto international politics.

The essay's running gestalt — Western policy is failing because the people making it are psychologically incapable of acknowledging the failure — is the kind of move that's hard to make falsifiable. It is consistent with the carry-forward thread the project has been tracking (Aurelien's "you the people" piece, his ongoing PMC diagnosis): every prescribed remedy is structurally unavailable to the prescriber. But it also approaches the territory where any contrary view from inside the PMC can be dismissed as the operative individual being too ego-bound to see clearly. That's a closed system. The essay's redeeming move is the closing acknowledgement that people are different and react differently, which gestures (without committing) to the limits of his own framework. The Doors-song titling ("People Are Strange") is the cheekier version of that admission.

Aurelien's piece is most useful when read against the Yglesias methodological-disputes piece of the same week. Both are arguments about why what people confidently know turns out to be wrong; Yglesias's mechanism is technical (methodological choices buried in data construction); Aurelien's is psychological (Ego Death plus apophenia plus non-declarative memory). Yglesias's is the more testable framework; Aurelien's is the more sweeping. Whether one finds the sweep persuasive or the testability dispositive is largely temperamental.

↩ Back to TOC

Degrowth would make Europeans into "Europoors"

Noahpinion · 2026-06-13 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A June Guardian op-ed ("We've done the maths") signed by Thomas Piketty, Joseph Stiglitz, Jason Hickel, Olivier De Schutter, Jayati Ghosh, and Kate Raworth (Pangram flagged it as 100% AI-generated), promoting a "roadmap for eradicating poverty beyond growth."

Smith argues degrowth is a sociologically European phenomenon — its proponents (Piketty, Hickel, Raworth, Parrique), conferences, EU policy reception, and literature reviews are all overwhelmingly European, while US progressives want Green New Deals, Canadians and Australians ignore the idea, and developing countries reject it. He frames it as a postcommunist Big Idea for the European left: a coalition cement that smuggles together decolonial, climate, socialist, social-democratic, and unionist factions behind the unifying conviction that Europeans "ought to be poorer." Smith cites Savin and van den Bergh's (2024) systematic review of 561 degrowth studies finding 90% are opinion rather than analysis, with little quantitative work, ad hoc policy advice, and a recurring "reverse causality" confusion that treats exogenous economic decline as deliberate degrowth. The strategic stakes for Smith are urgent: Europe faces the Second China Shock, Russian-energy cutoff, Trump tariffs, drone-warfare unpreparedness, and supply-chain dependence on Chinese components — precisely when degrowth policies (nuclear shutdowns, AI regulation, mandated working-hour cuts, construction bans) would disable the "last few remaining economic engines of the free world." A footnote suggests anti-Westernism is the unspoken motive.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Smith in his sharpest mode: a pieces-of-evidence argument (cited studies, named authors, geographic distribution of conferences) deployed in service of a strong political-strategic claim. The empirical core — that degrowth is functionally a European movement and that its scholarly literature is thinly evidenced opinion — is well-anchored to Savin and van den Bergh's systematic review, which is doing most of the load-bearing work. Smith's geographic-functional argument is the more novel move: rather than rebutting degrowth's economic claims head-on (he says he's done that before, citing his Hickel and Raworth pieces), he reframes degrowth as a sociological-coalitional artifact of European leftist politics in the post-communist vacuum. That frame has explanatory traction but is also load-bearing in a way the piece doesn't fully test: degrowth-adjacent strains in US Democratic Socialism, in Buen Vivir discourse in Latin America, and in some Japanese left-academic circles complicate the strict Europe-only mapping, though Smith would presumably say these are token rather than agenda-setting.

The strategic-urgency frame in the closer is where the piece does its real political work, and it's worth flagging that this is rhetorically continuous with his "Why Europe should put up trade barriers" piece from earlier this month (one-liner #52 in the rolling index, Smith arguing Europe should tariff Chinese high-tech goods because drone-component dependence is militarily catastrophic). Smith is constructing a unified Europe-as-civilizational-bulwark frame across multiple posts: Europe is the last institutional champion of the postwar liberal order; its industrial base is under simultaneous economic, military, and ideological assault; ergo any policy framework that electively shrinks European productive capacity reads to him as objectively pro-adversary. The footnote attribution of anti-Westernism to degrowth proponents is the weakest move in the piece — it converts a structural critique into a motive imputation that he himself frames as speculation ("one suspects").

The Piketty co-authorship is the under-developed angle. Piketty's prior research career is built on careful quantitative work; his presence on an op-ed Pangram flags as AI-generated, that Smith catches making the unsupported "decoupled from shared prosperity" claim, is a real reputational data point that the piece notes but doesn't develop. The Pritchett (2022) growth-and-poverty chart Smith cites is genuinely powerful as a rebuttal to the decoupling claim, and represents the kind of mechanism-first counter-evidence the piece needs more of.

Carry-forward: the Smith Europe-bulwark frame deserves tracking against the Aurelien "nothing in common" thread (one-liner #3 from last week) — they are arguing from radically different premises (Aurelien: forty years of neoliberalism stripped European cohesion; Smith: Europe is the last cohesive bulwark) about the same continent's strategic readiness, and the discourse will benefit from holding them in tension rather than collapsing to one.

↩ Back to TOC

Liberals in the Weeds, Libertarians Between the Screeds

Oren Cass · 2026-06-12 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Jerusalem Demsas (editor of The Argument) attacking American Compass's Reclaiming American Citizenship statement with a "Who gets to decide the good life? Joe Biden? Donald Trump? How are they going to stop me?" tweet — and, on the back end, the Swiss population-cap referendum debate the WSJ news section covered against its own editorial line.

Cass argues that both economic libertarianism (e.g., Duke economist Michael Munger insisting any industrial-policy claim collapses into Donald-Trump-makes-all-investments) and what he calls "social libertarianism" (Demsas) use the same rhetorical move: reducing democratic deliberation to a binary between unfettered individual choice and one-man tyranny, with no middle ground for republican lawmaking. The move is incoherent because Demsas herself endorses a substantive policy agenda (easy housing, abundant energy, anti-licensing, opt-into-single-earner households) that requires precisely the kind of contested conception of the good life she dismisses when others advance it. The deeper pattern is an adverse-selection dynamic: a community whose ablest members "opt out" of obligations leaves the remainder unable to sustain the village's burdens, which is the American-Compass diagnosis of elite disaffiliation. The back-of-the-issue takes the same frame to immigration: the WSJ news section quietly endorses (via Switzerland) what its editorial board has decades-denied — that mass migration lifts top-line GDP while degrading per-capita measures, depresses native-worker wages, suppresses productivity investment, and creates structural employer-cheap-labor dependencies (Haitian eldercare workers as the U.S. analog).

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The piece is a vintage Cass move: take an opponent's pithy rhetorical gesture, locate the operative tradeoff it obscures, and force the opponent to either own the substantive commitments their slogan denies or concede they are smuggling. The strongest part is the Demsas-tweets-against-Demsas-tweets construction — the same thread containing both "people who want to evaluate single-earner-family viability are crypto-patriarchal" and "I also want a world where people can opt into being a single-earner household" is genuinely incoherent on its own terms, and Cass is right to surface it rather than steelman his way around it. The Lighthizer-quote shot ("libertarianism is a philosophy for stupid people") is gratuitous and weakens the rest, but the structural point survives: if your stated end-state requires substantive policy, you cannot disown the question of what substantive policy is for.

What's distinctive about this piece in the broader Cass corpus is that it operates outside his usual industrial-policy register — it's a foundational political-theory argument about whether liberalism-as-procedural-neutrality is achievable at all, with Lincoln (government does for the community what individuals cannot) and the Constitution's preamble as the constructive frame against which the procedural-neutrality position is judged. This connects directly to the Hawley speech and the broader American Compass Reclaiming American Citizenship statement: the project is trying to define "citizenship" as a load-bearing political-economic concept that does substantive work the proceduralist vocabulary cannot. The back-of-issue Switzerland item is the piece's empirical anchor: when even the WSJ news section concedes (about Switzerland, not about the U.S.) that immigration suppresses productivity investment and creates structural cheap-labor subsidies, Cass treats it as confirmation of the productivist-critique frame his side has spent years building.

The analytical weakness is that Cass doesn't engage the strongest version of the Demsas position — that "abundance liberalism" is not value-neutral but explicitly endorses a thin liberal conception of the good (autonomy + capacity), and that the disagreement is over which substantive conception is correct, not whether to have one. Demsas isn't quite as confused as the screenshotted tweet sequence makes her look; her abundance-liberal coalition does have a positive conception (build more, free more, regulate less of the supply side), it's just that this conception leaves intentionally underdetermined what the goods of life are (whom you marry, what you do with abundant energy, etc.). Cass's argument is that this underdetermination is unstable — that the policy reforms abundance liberals want cannot be sustained without thicker commitments — which is a serious claim but one he asserts rather than demonstrates. The "everyone wants a village, no one wants to be a villager" framing is the strongest available version of the demonstration; the eldercare-on-Haitian-TPS example is the cleanest empirical instance.

↩ Back to TOC

Trump’s war on state capacity is coming home to roost

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-10 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The first New World screwworm cases in US cattle since the 1960s; 2,030 measles cases through June 2026 (on pace to exceed 2025's 2,288, the highest since 1991); the eastern DRC Ebola outbreak; and DOGE's broader cuts to USAID, CDC, and APHIS (Animal and Plant Health Inspection Service).

Yglesias uses Rachel Carson's Silent Spring (1962) and Sarah Zhang's 2020 Atlantic profile of the Panama screwworm-eradication barrier as historical scaffolding for a long-form-success story now unraveling — the sterile-male radiation program that eliminated the screwworm from the US, Mexico, and Central America and held the line at a Panama frontier for decades. He traces the breakdown: Covid disrupted Panama fly-breeding operations and surveillance in 2020; the 2021-22 Darién Gap migration surge made cattle-smuggling enforcement harder; by 2023 the Biden administration was tapping CCC funds for APHIS and asking Congress for a backup US breeding facility. Trump's DOGE then canceled USAID surveillance contracts for screwworm, bird flu, and other diseases — what fact-checkers will call narrowly accurate ("not the core production line") but what Yglesias names as the actual operational failure. The same pattern recurs with Ebola (Trump's response is Americans-only quarantine center in Kenya plus a tactical travel ban on three poor Central African states, paired with reluctance to restrict European World Cup travel) and measles (most state childhood vaccination rates have dropped beneath the herd-immunity threshold, with breakthrough infections still rare but only because immunity has not yet decayed enough). The unifying diagnosis: DOGE's filter for what counts as worth funding was political salience at the moment of cutting, not objective importance — bird flu staff got rehired in February 2025 because eggs were a salient issue; the screwworm and Ebola staff didn't, because nobody was talking about them.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Yglesias doing the policy-explainer mode he does best: take a specific operational program (the New World screwworm sterile-male barrier in Panama) most readers have never heard of, walk through its history with enough detail that the reader understands why it's a success worth defending, then show how a generalized administrative posture ("cut anything that isn't politically salient this quarter") destroys it. The screwworm narrative is genuinely well-chosen — eradication of the New World screwworm from North and Central America via Carson-era radiation sterilization is one of the better-documented public-health-meets-agriculture successes of the 20th century, and the Panama containment line is exactly the kind of program where the cost of failure becomes visible only after the failure is already irreversible. The "of course surveillance is part of containment" line is the operative analytical move — it names the specific category of state capacity (monitoring infrastructure invisible to political salience cycles) that the DOGE filter is structurally biased to eliminate.

The Ebola section is where Yglesias's argument is sharpest in its policy implication. The Trump administration's response — a quarantine center for Americans in Kenya, a travel ban on three poor countries, pressure on Europeans to impose the same travel ban in advance of the World Cup — exposes the operative trade-off: even a maximally migration-skeptical administration won't impose travel restrictions that carry significant economic costs (European World Cup tourists), so the only viable long-term strategy is the one that requires the institutional capacity (USAID, CDC) being cut. The framing extracts the underlying logic without needing to claim Trump caused the outbreak — a discipline Yglesias is unusually careful about here, repeatedly saying "this isn't Trump's fault, but..."

The measles section is the weakest of the three because Yglesias gestures at the RFK Jr.-vaccine-skepticism vector without doing the structural argument: the herd-immunity threshold breakdown is largely downstream of state-level exemption-broadening for years, not a DOGE phenomenon at all. Folding it into the "war on state capacity" frame elides that it's a different (and arguably older) story — one about the political organization of vaccine skepticism as a coalition factor rather than about staffing cuts. Including it weakens the framing's analytical edge; the screwworm and Ebola cases alone would have been the cleaner argument.

The structural diagnostic — DOGE filters for political salience, but the entire point of state capacity in public-health infrastructure is to maintain monitoring for things that aren't salient until they are — is the kind of one-line operational summary that's hard to argue with. The Biden administration's CCC-tap and APHIS-fund-request in 2023-24 is offered as the responsible-management counterfactual, which is roughly correct but elides that the Panama breakdown started under Biden — a point Yglesias notes glancingly but doesn't develop. The story is still mostly DOGE's, but the cleaner framing would acknowledge that maintaining the screwworm barrier was already harder by 2023 than the system was treating it.

↩ Back to TOC

Hard work is good

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-12 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Sunday mailbag reader questions on parenting, DIY, alternate-history Eisenhower, post-2029 Democratic health-care strategy, UK economic fixes, NBA international MVPs, Jamelle Bouie's case for a Reconstruction-scale Democratic vision, and best-songs-by-decade.

Yglesias structures the mailbag around a single recurring complaint: that American institutions have responded to rising demand by watering down the product rather than raising the standard. College aspiration was advanced by making coursework easier rather than admitting fewer students — "the missing dark matter" in the too-many-or-too-few-go-to-college debate. He uses his son's swim team as the proof that rigor still works when applied (the clock doesn't lie), endorses Alex Bores in NY-12 partly as a defensive move against AI-industry super-PAC dollars trying to make AI regulation electorally toxic, and argues Democrats post-2029 should "subtract" two or three bad ideas for every new one added — Bouie-style "bold visions" are exactly the wrong instinct. On Medicaid expansion in red states, Democrats should stop trying to legislatively solve a problem GOP governors are choosing not to solve and just "point and laugh" the way conservatives do at blue-state homelessness. UK economy: YIMBY-plus-infrastructure is in fact sufficient. Each answer collapses into the same shape — institutions and electorates do better when scarcity, rigor, or focus is imposed from the supply side.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Yglesias in mailbag mode, which means the format is doing some of the work of making the underlying argument seem incidental rather than programmatic. But it isn't incidental. The through-line — too many of our institutional debates have collapsed because we keep responding to "more people want X" by making X less demanding — is the same argument across philosophy classes, swim practice, the Democratic policy agenda, and Britain's economy. The answers in which the heuristic does the most work are the college-rigor one and the Bouie-rebuttal one. On college, the move is genuinely useful: "college is broken" debates almost always elide that the median humanities course is now structurally easier than it was a generation ago, and Yglesias's solution (raise the floor of the course, let enrollment fall as a downstream consequence) is a cleaner framing than either side of the more-or-fewer-students debate usually allows. On Democrats and "bold visions," his "subtract two bad ideas for every new one" rule is genuinely a discipline most party-strategy memos refuse to impose on themselves; the constitutional-reform-versus-plastic-straws example is funny but also load-bearing.

The Alex Bores plug — and the framing that an anti-regulation AI super PAC trying to crush a low-profile House primary candidate has accidentally elevated him — is the most directly self-interested piece of the mailbag, and Yglesias is transparent about that (Bores grew up in his House district; he made the early call). The implicit move is to construct a defensive perimeter around even-very-mild AI-policy expertise in Congress before AI-industry money makes such expertise prohibitively expensive to acquire. Whether or not Bores wins, the framing is itself doing work: signaling to the AI industry that targeting AI-curious Democrats may backfire.

The weakest answer is the Medicaid one, which is more revealing than Yglesias intends. The instruction to abandon legislative effort and just "tweet and point and laugh" because the Court already nullified the ACA's compulsory mechanism is a counsel of comfortable despair dressed as strategic discipline — a Democratic president with a 62-seat Senate majority (the questioner's stipulation) and limited fiscal space could plausibly invest in any number of mechanisms (federal-direct-enrollment alternative, conditional grants, threatening preemption of state insurance regulation) before falling back to symbolic shaming. The "purchaser of moral high ground" frame works for Democrats when the substantive route is fully closed; with full-trifecta control and 62 senators, it's a posture, not a strategy.

The DIY answer is the most candid: Yglesias acknowledges that his "I have a marginal wage" framework is structurally inapplicable to the salaried workers it's most often deployed against. That this is the third or fourth time he's flagged it suggests the point lands with his subscribers, who keep asking versions of the same question.

The Eisenhower counterfactual is sport — a counter-history exercise of the sort Yglesias clearly enjoys — but the operative move (Taft wins, dies, Driscoll becomes a counterfactual-Eisenhower) is a useful demonstration of how much of "Eisenhower-era America" was actually median Northeastern Republican policy preferences rather than a single great man.

↩ Back to TOC

The neglected importance of boring methodological disputes

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-08 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Saloni Dattani's recent roundup of methodologically-spurious trends in mainstream media; Auten-Splinter's empirical contestation of Piketty-Saez-Zucman on top-1% income share; the Eric Zwick / Owen Zidar forthcoming book The Everywhere Millionaire; and Noah Smith's recent reconsideration of the neo-Brandeisian antitrust movement.

Yglesias walks through a series of cases where confidently-cited "facts" turn out to depend on contested methodological choices. The first is benign: stagnant real wages 1979-2014 is an artifact of using the unchained CPI (which overstates inflation over long horizons); deflate with chained CPI or PCE and the picture is significantly rosier — most qualified professionals now agree the chained index is correct. The Piketty-Saez-Zucman versus Auten-Splinter dispute on top-1% income share is harder: four axes of disagreement (how to allocate underreported income, retained corporate earnings, government spending, and the differential prevalence of married couples in higher-income units), and Yglesias is confident PSZ are wrong on underreporting allocation but unsure on the other three. The Jan De Loecker / Jan Eeckhout markup-and-concentration literature has spawned a counter-wave of empirical rebuttals; Yglesias is honestly uncertain which is right, though he insists Lynn-Stoller-style neo-Brandeisians don't care about the evidence. Zwick-Zidar's descriptive finding on small-business owners (people like Yglesias himself, drawing modest salaries while their wholly-owned LLC books high profit margins) accounts for roughly a third of the apparent fall in labor share. Kevin Erdmann's housing-wealth finding (real-estate wealth growth since 1980 is mostly scarcity-induced rent inflation, not new investment — Americans are poorer on average from it). Dattani's catalog of fake trends: the US maternal mortality spike was a death-counting change; the interstate-mobility collapse is mostly methodological; the autism "rise" reflects diagnostic-criteria changes, with no rise to explain. Closing meta-point: methodological errors get amplified asymmetrically because "look, a trend!" is a better story than "things are the same," and negativity bias compounds it, so the fake trends you see in media are systematically negative ones.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Yglesias doing a piece that's substantively important precisely because it's structurally difficult to do well. Most "many widely-cited facts are wrong" essays land as cherry-picked skeptic-posturing; Yglesias avoids that by being genuinely uncertain on the cases where he is (the markups-concentration dispute, three of the four PSZ-vs-Auten-Splinter axes) and walking through the methodological mechanisms rather than just citing authorities. The CPI-versus-chained-CPI walkthrough is the kind of methodological literacy most policy commentary won't bother with; the Auten-Splinter discussion is even more careful (Yglesias is confident on one axis, agnostic on three).

The piece's sharpest claim is the meta one at the end: methodological errors that produce false trends will systematically skew negative because "look, a trend!" beats "things are the same" in the attention market, and negativity bias compounds this. The Dattani-catalog cases are the strongest evidence — US maternal mortality, interstate mobility, autism. Each produced years of policy discourse downstream of a measurement artifact, with no countervailing institutional incentive to debunk in the original direction. The autism case is the most consequential, because (as Yglesias notes) we are now positioned for a second wave of damage from RFK Jr. and the Trump administration trying to identify Tylenol as the cause of a "rise" that isn't actually happening.

The Stoller-Lynn jab is sharper than it looks. Yglesias is conceding that the original De Loecker-Eeckhout markup literature gave the neo-Brandeisian movement empirical legitimacy it now retains even though the empirical picture has become genuinely uncertain — and arguing that Stoller and Lynn are now operating in an evidence-independent mode. This is consistent with the wider Yglesias take on neo-Brandeisianism over the last several months (the project's running record of Yglesias on neo-Brandeisianism vs. Stoller's BIG is the cross-pole disagreement that's anchored multiple weeks).

The Zwick-Zidar small-business observation is the most underrated finding in the piece. The "about a third of the fall in labor share is owner-employee tax optimization, not corporate overlords exploiting workers" claim — if it survives scrutiny — substantially changes one of the load-bearing factual claims in the post-2008 progressive economic narrative. Yglesias is being self-disclosive (he is one of the small-business owners whose tax optimization shows up as artificially-high "corporate profit margins"); the disclosure makes the piece more credible.

The Erdmann housing-wealth point is the operationally-useful one for Yglesias's broader YIMBY project. The framing — Americans on average have become poorer via scarcity-induced housing-rent inflation, even though it shows up as wealth — is the conceptual move that lets YIMBYism redirect the "we'd be destroying $20T in homeowner wealth" objection. Wealth tied up in rent-extraction-from-scarcity isn't real wealth at the population level; eliminating the scarcity makes people richer on average even as paper wealth falls.

Where the piece is weakest is its lack of any treatment of the symmetric case — methodological choices that understate true negative trends (climate, debt, biodiversity loss). The argument structure works only because the cases Yglesias walks through are ones where the methodological correction is in the "things are better than reported" direction. The full version of the argument would need to address whether the asymmetry is genuine (most measurement artifacts will be negativity-biased) or whether Yglesias is selecting examples that confirm his prior. The piece would be substantially stronger if it acknowledged this and argued the asymmetry on the merits.

↩ Back to TOC

There's No Such Thing as "The Trades"

Freddie deBoer's Blog · 2026-06-08 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The current right-coded / contrarian-populist conventional-wisdom advice that young people should "go into the trades" instead of college, and the broader genre of slogan-as-economic-advice.

deBoer argues that "the trades" is not a meaningful occupational category — it's a label stretched over jobs with almost nothing in common beyond the absence of a four-year degree and looking unfamiliar to the educated classes. An elevator mechanic, a residential drywaller, a journeyman lineman, a diesel technician, a flooring installer, and a stationary engineer have radically divergent earnings, working conditions, physical toll, job security, and long-term trajectories. Some involve strong unions, structured apprenticeships, and pensions; others are gig-style subcontracting. Some leave you with a working body at 60; others destroy your knees, shoulders, and lungs. Within each occupation, geographic variance compounds this — a Gulf Coast pipeline welder earns several times what the same welder earns in a stagnant rural town; the spread between the 10th and 90th percentile within each individual trade is larger than the spread between the median of the lowest- and highest-paid trade occupation. The "go into the trades" slogan is the structurally-identical mistake to "everyone should go to college" — flattening a hugely diverse outcome distribution into a monolithic recommendation. The deeper claim: we're terribly bad at predicting the job market (manufacturing, law, "learn to code" each proved confidently wrong), so the right response isn't to pick winners but to equip people with adaptability — transferable competencies, willingness to retrain, and a humane safety net so labor-market disruption doesn't destroy basic access to shelter, medicine, and education.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is deBoer doing the form he does best: identify a piece of advice currently treated as obviously-correct contrarian wisdom, show that it has the same structural defect as the conventional wisdom it claims to displace, and route the conclusion to his standing case that the labor market is a roulette wheel and the only defensible policy is a robust safety net. The "the trades" piece is a clean example of the pattern — and the wage-variance data (10th-to-90th percentile spread within each trade exceeding the spread between the median of different trades) is the operative empirical move, displayed in the BLS chart at the top.

The strongest analytical move is the form-versus-content distinction: "the problem with pushing everyone to go to college wasn't the content of the advice but the form." This is the kind of categorical reframe that's hard to argue with and that immediately spreads to other slogan-driven policy genres. It's also a reframe deBoer has been working in different domains for years (his ed-policy writing makes the parallel claim about credentialism), and the trades essay extends the operating principle without naming the broader through-line.

The "we're terribly bad at predicting the job market" passage is the piece's most consequential operational claim. The cases deBoer cites — manufacturing's offshoring collapse, law school's glut, "learn to code"'s AI-driven obsolescence — are each genuine examples of confident decade-out labor-market predictions falsified within a generation. The implication, that betting a teenager's life on any specific occupational forecast is exactly the predictive game we've kept losing, is consistent with the standing-themes carry-forward the project tracks (working-class plight, working-class livelihoods under technological disruption). The conclusion — equip people with adaptability rather than picking winners, and pair it with a generous safety net — is recognizably deBoer's standing socialist position, here arrived at via labor-market-epistemics rather than via political-philosophy.

Where the piece is weakest is in what it doesn't engage. The "go into the trades" advice is usually offered to a specific class of young people — those for whom a four-year college is genuinely a worse bet than an apprenticeship — and deBoer's "the variance within trades is huge" rebuttal applies symmetrically to "go to college" (the variance in college outcomes is also huge). The cleanest version of his argument would be that all categorical advice is structurally defective, and individuals need person-specific, place-specific guidance — but the piece sometimes reads as if the trades-specific variance is unusually large, when in fact the analytical move is general. He glances at this near the end ("a specific apprenticeship, in a specific place, suited to a specific person, can be a genuinely excellent choice"), but the framing throughout has positioned the trades as the bad slogan rather than as the symmetric case to the college slogan.

The piece's value to the roundup is mostly as a vector — deBoer is consistently the writer most willing to insist on the limits of labor-market forecasting and to route the conclusion to a safety-net defense rather than to occupational guidance. That makes him a useful corrective to the abundance-liberalism mode (which still tends to imagine that better policy will produce predictable labor-market outcomes) and to the populist-right mode (which has hardened the trades-trumphalism into a doctrine). The lasting analytical move is the form-versus-content distinction: any slogan that promises a single category as a safe haven is structurally lying about how labor markets work.

↩ Back to TOC

Insurers aren't the main villain of the U.S. health care system

Noahpinion · 2026-06-10 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A reposting of his December 2024 post (originally written after the Brian Thompson / UnitedHealthcare CEO murder) with a new framing tied to his earlier-June piece on the anti-monopoly movement's empirical sloppiness, and a comment-thread exchange with reader "Matthew" defending insurer-as-villain framing.

Smith's empirical claim is that US health-insurer profit margins are too small to drive the US cost gap with peer countries: UnitedHealth Group's 6.11% net margin is roughly half the S&P 500 average; Elevance, Centene, and Cigna run 1-4%; donating UnitedHealth's entire profit to paying for care would buy only 9.3% more care. He cites the Kaiser Family Foundation (KFF) decomposition attributing the US's excess spending to providers (hospitals, pharma, doctors, nurses, tech suppliers) rather than to insurance administration. He notes that Americans pay a smaller share out of pocket than several European countries, that ROE on the insurance line is underwhelming, and that the providers with the highest ROE are pharma/distribution companies (HCA 272%, Cencora 234%, AbbVie 84%, Novo Nordisk 72%, Eli Lilly 59%). The Bruenig rejoinder (provider-side administrative costs include insurer-wrangling, possibly intentionally inefficient) gets a "smell test" rebuttal that low insurer profits would have to be hiding somewhere if Bruenig were right. Smith adopts Jeremiah Johnson's "sin-eaters" frame: insurers are hired bad-guys whose modest fee buys providers a Mother Theresa role. His policy prescription is national-insurance bargaining power against providers, with caveats about innovation suppression risk.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The empirical argument is, on its own terms, well-constructed. Insurer profit-margin data is real, the KFF decomposition is the leading peer-country comparison, and the Bruenig "hidden inefficiency" rejoinder genuinely doesn't have a clean answer to "where are the missing profits, then?" The piece's value as a discourse intervention is high: there is in fact a populist coalition that conflates insurer denials (which are concretely awful) with the locus of the system's cost problem (which is provider pricing power), and a tighter mechanism-first frame would help that coalition pick higher-leverage targets.

But there are two structural under-developments. First, the "low profit margins prove low extraction" argument applies cleanly to insurance-line profit but Smith's own numbers show UnitedHealth Group has 26% ROE because it integrates downstream — pharmacy benefit management (Optum Rx), provider services (Optum Health), and data — which is exactly the vertical-integration story Stoller, Bruenig, and the anti-monopoly movement Smith is rebutting have been pointing at. PBM rebate flows in particular are a venue where insurer-affiliated entities extract from the drug-pricing chain in ways that look like "provider profits" on the KFF decomposition. So the question "if insurers are extracting, where's the money?" has a partial answer Smith mentions but doesn't develop: it's in the integrated downstream lines that show up as provider/distributor profit rather than as insurance-segment margin. The Cencora ROE he himself cites is the punchline; Cencora is a drug distributor that PBMs negotiate against.

Second, the "Americans pay less out of pocket than several Europeans" point is interesting but doesn't engage the policy claim it's mobilized against. The claim is not that out-of-pocket exposure is the cost-driver; the claim is that the combination of denials, surprise billing, and network-management practices produces the rage even when the gross numbers favor the US. Smith treats this as a sin-eater displacement, which is a real phenomenon but doesn't address whether the displacement is welfare-reducing on its own terms (the insurer-denial-fight has real consequences for treatment access independent of its share of cost-driving).

The cross-week intersection worth noting: this is in tension with Matt Bruenig's PPP piece from last week (one-liner #59, "Bruenig shows that once you add employer-sponsored-insurance premiums to the US tax wedge, average-wage American workers pay as much or more in effective labor taxes than Nordic workers") — both Smith and Bruenig are arguing that ESI is the load-bearing distortion in the US system, but they're drawing different implications: Smith says the locus of action is provider bargaining, Bruenig says the locus is recognizing that the US already has Nordic-equivalent labor-tax burdens hidden in private premium form. The implicit agreement (US system extracts at near-Nordic levels but worse outcomes) is a project-level finding the synthesis composer should flag.

↩ Back to TOC

Standalones / shorter notes

Title Author Publication Summary
Washington Is Treating Africa as a Target, Not a Partner — and Africa Knows It Habib Badawi African Arguments (publication) Badawi argues Frank Garcia's bloc-vote confirmation as Trump's top Africa diplomat — after a 15-month vacancy and 37 of 51 ambassadorships still empty — formalizes the transactional pivot that has already cost the US influence African governments are now leveraging against it.
Translation: Foreign Forces, Fake Patriots, and Adulterated Berries SW China Digital Times (discovery) China Digital Times translates a viral WeChat essay (Old Xiao's Random Jottings) anatomizing the three types of Chinese commentator who blame "foreign forces" for the Fujian adulterated-bayberries scandal — a rare unedited domestic attack on the patriotism-as-deflection rhetoric.
Estonia: consent law passed Euro|topics (curated debate) Euro|topics (discovery) Euro|topics aggregates two Estonian-press defenses of the new "yes model" consent law (69-8 vote), framing the shift from no-means-no to affirmative-consent as moral progress and Istanbul Convention alignment — both excerpts read the law approvingly.
Mario Vargas Llosa and the Possibility of a Populist Novel Geoff Shullenberger Compact Substack Shullenberger reads Vargas Llosa's posthumous final novel I Give You My Silence as the late-career attempt to imagine a populist novel — one where national-cultural unity is sublimated from demotic music (the Peruvian vals criollo) rather than imposed by elite art or revolutionary politics.
David Mamet's Theory of the Lindbergh Kidnapping Joe Nocera Free Press Mamet, in a Free Press bonus podcast with Joe Nocera, advances a dramatist's hypothesis that Charles Lindbergh accidentally killed his own son during a "hide-the-baby" practical joke and then staged the kidnapping — staking the case on the voice-ID problem (Lindbergh had 10,000 hours behind loud aviation engines), the Violet Sharp suicide, and the trial-orchestration anomalies.
Some Leftists Are Boycotting This Film. Everyone Should Watch It. Michelle Goldberg NYT Goldberg defends Israeli director Nadav Lapid's "Yes" — an impossibly scathing film about Israeli national-anthem-for-genocide complicity — against a European-left boycott of its Israeli-state-funded creator, arguing the boycott vindicates Israel's claim that critics target Jewish identity rather than state conduct.
How quickly do electric car sales translate into cars on the road? Hannah Ritchie Sustainability by Numbers (Ritchie) Ritchie reports 2025 IEA data — one in four new cars sold globally was electric, China crossed 50%, Norway near 100%, US stuck at 10% — and offers a fleet-turnover model showing the lag between sales share and on-road share.
Bring Back the SAT Svetlana Jitomirskaya Free Press Jitomirskaya argues UC's 2020 SAT abolition — paired with Newsom's Higher Education Compact and high-school grade inflation — is producing a statistically significant negative correlation between a California high school's math proficiency and its students' UC-Berkeley admission rates.
Biological Evolution and Information Acquisition Brian Potter Construction Physics (Potter) Potter shows via toy simulations that sexual reproduction's advantage over asexual reproduction is an information-theoretic one: gene recombination converts a high-dimensional global search into many parallel local searches.
Lee Raymond, 311 ppm – 421 ppm Robinson Meyer Heatmap (publication) Meyer writes the obituary for ExxonMobil's Lee Raymond as a structural argument that the climate-denial-funding campaign Raymond ran from the late 1990s prevented domestic climate legislation and forfeited U.S. leadership in solar, wind, and battery industries.
Safety and nation-building in Mexico Tyler Cowen Marginal Revolution Cowen argues Mexico is "still not a mature nation-state" — drug-cartel territorial control reflects a 200-year pattern of central-state weakness, and US policy should treat the country as an ongoing nation-building project.
Why is America less of a 24/7 society? Tyler Cowen Marginal Revolution Cowen surfaces the observation that American physical-retail nightlife has thinned dramatically since the 1990s and proposes the load-bearing causes are the migration of 24/7 activity into logistics/digital infrastructure and the improvement of at-home online entertainment.
Does U.F.O. Disclosure Threaten Faith? Ross Douthat NYT Douthat reads Spielberg's "Disclosure Day" film as a marker of the current U.F.O. moment and argues the operative Christian anxiety is not that aliens disprove God but that "brothers from another planet" could pose as shepherds-of-souls — a deception risk that justifies whistleblower protections rather than continued anomalous-video hype.

Washington Is Treating Africa as a Target, Not a Partner — and Africa Knows It

Habib Badawi · 2026-06-10 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Senate's bloc-vote confirmation of Frank Garcia — a former Navy officer with no Africa-policy publications — as Assistant Secretary of State for African Affairs after a 15-month vacancy, alongside the first 16 months of Trump-II Africa policy outcomes.

Badawi (writing for African Arguments, a publication run from SOAS that aggregates African analysts) uses the Garcia confirmation as a hook for a strong-Africa-perspective accounting of the first 16 months of Trump's "trade not aid" pivot. The empirical case: USAID funding suspended (the $11B 2023 baseline gone), PEPFAR effectively wound down (Malawi HIV/AIDS services collapsed, Ethiopia food aid 16M people short, DRC lost 70% of humanitarian assistance), Prosper Africa lost $165M unspent, and the Bureau of African Affairs cycled through three interim heads. Garcia's testimony-named flagship — the Lobito Corridor, the 1,300 km Angola-DRC-Zambia mineral railway — is presented as the model and the rhetoric is investment-not-aid; Badawi argues this is the playbook already running for 16 months with visible negative results. The Washington Accords (DRC-Rwanda peace deal, formalized December 2025) are conceded as real but framed as peace bartered for resource access via a "Regional Economic Integration Framework" built around critical minerals. The tariff escalation (30% on South Africa, 50% on Lesotho, AGOA expiry October 2025) is producing the predictable diversification — South Africa toward BRICS, Kenya signing a duty-free deal with China. Badawi quotes Herman Cohen (the H.W. Bush-era Assistant Secretary, watching the bureau for decades) calling the trust gap Trump's own doing. Garcia's confirmation hearing received four questions before the committee moved on.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is an African-press accounting of an American policy story, and the analytical value is exactly the perspective gap — most US coverage of Garcia has been bureaucratic (the vacancy is over) or technical (the Lobito Corridor is interesting). Badawi's framing reverses the figure-ground: the policy continuity from the past 16 months is the news, and Garcia's confirmation merely formalizes it. The aggregated empirical case is solid and well-sourced — the funding numbers, the embassy vacancy ratio, the AGOA expiry, the DRC-Rwanda framework details are all checkable, and the African Report, Al Jazeera, and Foreign Policy citations carry their weight. The diversification claim (Kenya's duty-free deal with China) is the kind of detail that confirms the structural prediction Tooze and the China-rise writers have been making for years: when American institutional presence retreats, China and BRICS+ don't merely fill the vacuum — they accelerate their own pull through it.

Where the piece is doing the most work analytically is the distinction between "trade" and "influence." Badawi's claim is that transactional partnership cannot substitute for the slow accumulation of institutional presence, expertise, and willingness to stay engaged when nothing is extractable — and the African Union, G20, BRICS+, and multilateral forums are now the leverage African governments use precisely because they have those institutional muscles and the US increasingly does not. That's a structural argument that doesn't depend on judging the Lobito Corridor itself; even if mineral railways are good infrastructure, you cannot run an Africa policy on them. The piece names what the project's writers have mostly missed: when the US dismantles soft power and substitutes deal-making, the deals themselves get worse terms because the counterparties now have alternatives.

The non-Anglophone-epistemic-gap value here is that this piece reads like an African policy memo rather than an American debate. It does not litigate Trump-personality questions; it presents the AU, BRICS+, and South-South diplomacy as the structural fact American policy must now operate against. That register is what the project added discovery feeds like African Arguments to surface, and Badawi is delivering. It also pairs usefully with last week's Bright Simons piece on the KOKO Networks collapse (African carbon markets reproducing colonial extractive templates) and the Dangote Refinery piece two weeks earlier (Nigeria breaking the export-crude-import-refined architecture) — three pieces from African Arguments in three weeks naming a consistent thread: the relationship between African institutions and Western capital is being renegotiated by Africans, and the American policy apparatus hasn't noticed.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Translation: Foreign Forces, Fake Patriots, and Adulterated Berries

SW · 2026-06-09 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Fujian provincial scandal in which nearly two dozen officials were punished for oversight failures around bayberries illegally soaked in the controlled preservative sodium dehydroacetate (banned for use on fresh fruit in China), and the subsequent online claim that "external forces" had hyped the story to harm Chinese agriculture.

CDT (the long-running Berkeley/Hong Kong outlet that translates censored Chinese-internet writing) presents a single WeChat essay from the public account "Old Xiao's Random Jottings" with framing context. The translated essay performs a precise typology of three commentator classes who deflect domestic food-safety failures onto "external forces": (1) patriotism-as-business commentators who weaponize the unpatriotic label to silence critics, (2) shills for the actual adulterating businesses who use foreign-forces conspiracy theories to hide local profiteering, and (3) regulators ducking their own oversight failures by blaming foreign enemies for sabotage. The essay then rebuts the standard "trace residues are harmless" deflection by distinguishing legal-compliant residue from illegal additives at any dose, and the "but exposure hurts farmers" deflection by pointing out that the conspiracy theorizing itself harms the whole fruit sector by blocking the regulatory response. Closing line: "almost inevitably, whatever the situation, when someone brings up hostile foreign forces, they have no intention of being reasonable about it." CDT's framing situates this in a longer cycle — the MSS's April claim that foreign forces are funding youth slackerism, and earlier cases like the detained investigative journalists Liu Hu and Wu Yingjiao — showing the "hostile foreign forces" trope has become a generalized deflection mechanism the domestic internet is now openly mocking.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

The piece's analytical value is the gap it surfaces between official CCP information control and what actually circulates on the Chinese internet. The Ministry of State Security has been pushing the "hostile foreign forces" frame aggressively over the past year (the slackerism claim was the most-mocked example), and the standard Western reading is to treat the trope as effective propaganda — what Chinese citizens hear, internalize, and reproduce. CDT's translation shows the opposite: an essay this acid, this specific in its typology, this confident in its mockery, would not be circulating on WeChat if popular sentiment had been captured by the official line. The "Old Xiao" account has presumably been censored by the time CDT translates it (the standard pattern), but the fact that it ran first, in WeChat's mass-audience format, is the load-bearing observation. The CCP's information apparatus is generating frames that domestic commentators are publicly and competently dismantling — and the dismantling itself becomes viral enough to merit translation.

For the project's non-Anglophone-epistemic-gap function, this is exactly the kind of piece the discovery feeds were added to surface. Most American China-watching reads from outside the Great Firewall and infers what Chinese citizens think from official media plus émigré commentary; CDT's value is showing actual within-system discourse — sometimes pro-regime, sometimes (as here) acidly anti-deflection, often more legally sophisticated than Western readers expect (the writer cites the GB 2763 food safety standard by name and distinguishes the regulatory question precisely). The line "fruit exports to the EU consistently meet standards far higher than those we have at home" is the kind of move that signals the writer understands the regulatory system isn't incapable, it's selectively enforced — a sharper critique than most Western coverage manages.

The cross-week connection is to the project's standing US-China theme and to last week's CDT translation of Sino-American summit sensitive words. Three CDT pieces in two months have surfaced the same pattern: the CCP information apparatus is increasingly resorting to deflection frames (foreign forces, sensitive-word censorship, narrative control around summits) while domestic commentators are increasingly willing to mock them on their face. That divergence — official propaganda escalation paired with apparent loss of grip on educated-urban internet discourse — is a structural data point the regime's strength/weakness debates rarely sit with. The honest synthesis isn't "the CCP is losing control" or "the CCP has captured discourse"; it's that the information-control apparatus is generating frames that no longer pass the laugh test for a meaningful slice of the educated population, even as those frames remain officially enforced.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Estonia: consent law passed

Euro|topics (curated debate) · 2026-06-11 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Estonian Parliament's 69-8 passage of a Penal Code amendment making sexual contact without clear consent legally rape, bringing Estonia into formal alignment with the Council of Europe's Istanbul Convention on preventing violence against women.

The aggregator presents two excerpts, both Estonian-press, both supportive — Delfi (Hannaliisa Uusma) frames the law as ending the regime in which victims had to explain why they didn't scream or fight back, shifting responsibility from victim to perpetrator; Õhtuleht draws the property-law analogy (we legislate against theft even though stealing is obviously wrong) and emphasizes the operative legal principles — voluntary and clearly expressed consent, and that consent once given does not extend to everything that follows. There is no dissenting excerpt; the European-press spread that Euro|topics typically curates does not appear this time, suggesting either that critical voices were absent in the Estonian discourse or that Euro|topics editors did not surface them. This is the direct legislative continuation of the affirmative-consent debate that ran heavily in last week's roundup — Tyler Cowen's June 6 Marginal Revolution post flagging the Journal of Health Economics finding that European tacit-consent laws (Mehic) reduced fertility by 4% via risk-averse withdrawal from dating markets. Estonia has now joined the legislative wave; the empirical question Cowen raised — what these laws do to fertility — applies directly.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

The aggregator's analytical interest is the curatorial choice, and the choice here is conspicuous: two domestic Estonian supportive excerpts and no cross-European spread. Euro|topics usually surfaces French, German, Polish, or Italian press to show convergence or divergence; the absence here is itself a data point — either the law passed without significant European-press commentary outside Estonia, or the editors decided the framing was uncontested enough not to require it. The latter would be unusual for a piece of legislation that has been politically contested everywhere it's been introduced (Spain's "Solo sí es sí" law famously had to be partially walked back after sentencing reductions for convicted offenders), so the former — that Estonia's passage was a quiet domestic event in pan-European terms — is the more probable reading. That itself is informative: the affirmative-consent legislative cycle in Northern and Baltic Europe has become routine enough that it no longer generates the Europe-wide debate it did in Spain, Sweden, or Iceland.

The continuity with last week's Cowen post is the load-bearing analytical link. Cowen's "tekl" reaction to the Journal of Health Economics paper was a Straussian signal that the finding — a 4% fertility reduction in countries with tacit-consent laws, driven by risk-averse men withdrawing from dating markets — is significant and underweighted. The Estonian passage is the live test case: a country with already-low fertility (Estonia's TFR is around 1.4) just adopted exactly the legal regime the paper studied. If the Mehic-type effect is real, Estonia's fertility data over the next 3-5 years will register it; if not, the paper's finding will not generalize. Notably, neither Estonian excerpt engages the trade-off: the Delfi piece treats the law as pure moral progress, the Õhtuleht piece grounds it in human-nature pessimism. The fertility-cost question that runs through Marginal Revolution comment threads is entirely absent from the Estonian framing, which is itself characteristic of the regional discourse — the affirmative-consent case is treated as a moral imperative whose costs (if any) are out of scope.

For the project's purposes, the piece is most useful as the legislative bookend to the discourse arc Cowen opened: the affirmative-consent legal regime is now being adopted by countries with already-stressed demographic baselines, and the empirical question whether this changes fertility through dating-market behavior is becoming testable in real time. The Euro|topics framing doesn't engage that question, but the synthesis composer can: this is the convergence of a feminist-legal-progress thread (which most Western readers see) with a fertility-economics thread (which Cowen has been flagging) into a single empirical test running in Estonia.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Mario Vargas Llosa and the Possibility of a Populist Novel

Geoff Shullenberger · 2026-06-06 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Shullenberger's own 2025 Compact obituary "The Great Neoliberal Novelist" (referenced in the opening) and the broader question of what literary form is adequate to the populist era — with Mario Vargas Llosa's posthumously released final novel I Give You My Silence (now in Adrian Nathan West's English translation) as the proximate text.

The argument runs in three layers. (1) Vargas Llosa's career-long project, anchored by the question from his 1969 Conversation in the Cathedral — "¿En qué momento se había jodido el Perú?" ("At what moment had Peru fucked itself up?") — followed Fredric Jameson's controversial 1986 "Third-World Literature in the Era of Multinational Capitalism" thesis that third-world texts are necessarily national allegories. Vargas Llosa's early "total novels" (Conversation, Time of the Hero, The Green House) used Faulkner-Dos-Passos modernist technique to contain Peru's fragmented social reality and aligned with revolutionary projects (the Cuban Revolution connection). (2) The 1970s repudiation of revolutionary commitments produced the second Vargas-Llosa: classical-liberal, Hayek-and-Thatcher, with novels (Mayta, Feast of the Goat) that used novelistic form to ironize utopianism, plus a turn toward lighter-genre work. (3) The current novel's protagonist, Toño Azpilcueta — a "proletarian intellectual" of lower-middle-class Lima writing about the vals criollo virtuoso Lalo Molfino — is Vargas Llosa's vehicle for a third late position: petty-bourgeois nationalist populism where class and racial differences are sublimated into transcendent cultural unity (the Amancaes-as-pre-political-fellowship vision). Shullenberger anchors the political content via Vargas Llosa's own 1983 essay on huachafería (an untranslatable Peruvian aesthetic combining outlandish pretension and utter earnestness), which the novel cribs and applies to Peruvian populist orators: a politician who fails to gesticulate, who gets to the point, will struggle to reach Peruvian listeners — Víctor Raúl Haya de la Torre (the socialist founder of aprismo) named as exemplar. The biographical irony: Vargas Llosa lost the 1990 presidential election to Alberto Fujimori, who then implemented Vargas Llosa's own Thatcherite program while building a populist machine that became "fujimorismo," a Peruvian proto-MAGA Vargas Llosa eventually endorsed (backing Keiko Fujimori against Pedro Castillo in 2021). The novel, Shullenberger argues, is Vargas Llosa's attempt to imagine novelistic form adequate to populism rather than only ironizing it — but the great writer "can now give us only his silence."

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is the strongest single piece of literary-political analysis Shullenberger has produced for Compact this year, and its distinctive contribution is the structural argument that Vargas Llosa's career maps onto three sequential political positions (revolutionary, classical-liberal, populist) and that the final novel is a deliberate attempt to test whether the novel form can serve the third the way his earlier work served the first two. The argument is well constructed because Shullenberger does not over-claim: he names that Toño's positions coincide with the mature Vargas Llosa's own, that the cribbing from the 1983 huachafería essay is direct, that the Amancaes-as-fellowship-vision has the same kind of national-cultural-unity move as the populist-right turn but located in demotic culture rather than elite reaction.
