The biographical irony anchor (Vargas Llosa loses the 1990 presidency to Fujimori, who builds the populist machine Vargas Llosa would later affirm) is the kind of factual particular that prevents the argument from floating into pure literary-theoretical assertion.

The piece's most analytically interesting move is the specification of what populist literary form looks like at the formal level. Shullenberger's argument is that the early Vargas Llosa "total novel" used modernist polyphony to contain fragmented social reality from above — a kind of high-art God's-eye view that, however revolutionary its politics, presupposed elite-novelist mastery. The third-phase Vargas Llosa, by contrast, lets Toño (an inside-the-class voice rather than a polyphonic above-the-class voice) carry the diagnosis of Peruvian society, and lets a popular musical form (the vals criollo) carry the unifying aesthetic rather than the novel itself. This is a substantive theoretical claim — that populist literary form requires demoting the novel from the form-of-nation-building to the form-of-recording-a-form-of-nation-building — and it is offered as a partial answer to the question Vargas Llosa's 2012 Notes on the Death of Culture raised about whether the novel has a future in the age of mass media spectacle. The implicit argument is that the novel has a future as a recorder of cultural-unity forms it cannot itself create.

What the piece does not engage, and where the underlying frame becomes contestable, is the political content of the third Vargas-Llosa position. The 2021 Keiko-Fujimori endorsement is mentioned but not interrogated; Keiko's father Alberto ran a regime that, alongside the Thatcherite shock therapy Vargas Llosa supported, conducted forced-sterilization programs against indigenous women, the 1992 autogolpe (self-coup) dissolving Congress, and the Grupo Colina death squad activity. Vargas Llosa's late-career embrace of right-populism is treated by Shullenberger as a question of literary form (can the novel adapt?) rather than as a question of political-ethical commitment (was the late Vargas Llosa right to support this?). The Compact-publication context is doing some quiet work here: a Compact reader is more likely to read "third-phase Vargas-Llosa endorses right-populism" as a vindication of populism than as an indictment of Vargas Llosa, which is the frame Shullenberger lets stand without commenting on. The implicit post-liberal frame is doing the heavy lifting: that populism is the political form adequate to the era, that elite-novelist mandarin-criticism of mass culture has run its course, and that the question is whether intellectual production can adapt to the populist moment rather than whether the populist moment is itself sound.

The strongest single observation in the piece, worth lifting out, is the formal one about huachafería and Peruvian political oratory: the requirement that a Peruvian politician "gesticulate," speak in circles, sing and growl rather than getting to the point, treat the speech as snake-charming rather than argument. This is a specific empirical claim about a specific national-political-aesthetic register, and it generalizes interestingly to the question of which political-aesthetic registers are operative in other national contexts (Trump, Milei, Bolsonaro, Le Pen, AfD) — Shullenberger doesn't extend the argument, but the analytical move is there if one wanted to. This is Shullenberger doing the kind of literary-political work Compact's intellectual project depends on, and doing it well within its frame; the frame itself is what to read critically.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

David Mamet's Theory of the Lindbergh Kidnapping

Joe Nocera · 2026-06-12 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Free Press's running Lindbergh kidnapping podcast (Nocera with Poppy Damon) — Mamet wrote in with a typewritten letter and a 2018 monologue in the voice of journalist Dorothy Kilgallen, and Nocera invited him on for a bonus episode to lay out his theory.

Mamet's reading places Charles Lindbergh — established as an anti-Semite and America First figure who accepted a medal from Goebbels and refused to return it — at the center of the case. Mamet's specific evidentiary chain: (1) Lindbergh identified Richard Hauptmann by voice in the courtroom (the "hey, doctor" line spoken at the cemetery money handoff), but Lindbergh had thousands of hours behind nine- and twelve-cylinder open-cockpit aviation engines and would have been substantially hearing-impaired; (2) Lindbergh was unaccountably in New York the night of the kidnapping and returned from the wrong direction; (3) Lindbergh exhibited sociopathic traits including a habit of hiding the baby and pretending he'd disappeared; (4) Anne Morrow Lindbergh's book The Wave of the Future praised fascism and contained anti-Semitic content; (5) Lindbergh took control of the investigation from New Jersey state police and blocked them from interrogating the household staff — including Violet Sharp, who killed herself shortly after; (6) Hauptmann's alibi witnesses were intimidated; (7) the cemetery intermediary "Jafsie" (John Condon) was an unaccountable outlier figure (Mamet's Talmudic-dream-analysis frame: the outlier is where the meaning sits); (8) Condon failed an early lineup but identified Hauptmann at trial. Mamet's full hypothesis: Lindbergh accidentally killed the baby during a hide-the-baby practical joke (possibly because the child had developmental impairments the eugenicist father wished to conceal), constructed the ladder, and staged the kidnapping; Hauptmann (likely innocent of the kidnapping itself but possibly guilty of cashing the ransom money) was framed as an anti-German scapegoat consistent with post-WWI sentiment.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

The interesting question is whether this is a substantive piece worth engaging or a celebrity-author plug that the project should flag and move past. The honest answer: it's closer to the substantive end of that spectrum than the editor's note feared, but not at the top of it. Mamet is genuinely advancing a hypothesis with named evidentiary anchors (the voice-ID critique from his own pilot experience is the cleanest single forensic point; the Lindbergh-controlling-the-investigation-and-blocking-staff-interrogation move is the cleanest single behavioral point; the Violet Sharp suicide is the cleanest single corroborating datapoint), and he's done enough reading to cite John Ciardi's pseudonym-free hostility to Anne Morrow Lindbergh's The Wave of the Future. This is more than a book-promo gesture. But it's also not investigative reporting — it's a dramatist's reading of an unsolved case, framed explicitly as such ("I'm a dramatist... that's how I make my living... so this is, I'm looking at it, say, if I were writing this thing, that's what I would write"). Mamet is honest about the genre, and the closing turn to Talmudic-dream-analysis ("the overlooked idea is there for some reason... that's the maid") is the methodological signature: he reads the case the way he reads a dream or a script.

The piece's actual value sits in two places: the voice-ID critique (which is a forensic point I haven't seen in the major mainstream Lindbergh-revisionist literature and is genuinely the kind of thing a pilot would notice), and the framing of Lindbergh as a sociopathic celebrity whose participation in the investigation was itself the operative anomaly. The latter is a familiar move in the Lindbergh-skeptic literature (Ahlgren and Monier's Crime of the Century made parts of this argument in 1993; Behn's Lindbergh: The Crime covered similar ground), and Mamet doesn't engage that prior literature explicitly — the reader of this transcript wouldn't know whether Mamet is recapitulating standard revisionist material or genuinely advancing it. The Dorothy Kilgallen-was-killed-by-the-Carlos-Marcello-people frame is the part of the transcript where the piece slides from "underexamined evidence in a famous case" to "JFK conspiracy adjacency," and Mamet's own framing acknowledges this ("I've always been interested in conspiracy theories... which means looking at what's happening, right"). The latter is a category-of-piece signal: this is a dramatist's case-for-revisionism, not a historian's or a journalist's.

For the project's purposes the piece slots awkwardly. It's not an electoral or geopolitical or cultural-systems piece, and the Crosscurrents weekly synthesis structure isn't built to absorb conspiracy-theory-adjacent dramatist reading of a 1930s crime. The Mamet-Nocera piece could plausibly be skipped in composition without losing thematic coverage — there's no broader claim about the world the corpus describes that this piece anchors, no policy implication, no operative writer-thread it contributes to. What it does contribute is genre-content: an entry in the Free Press's ongoing project of what does serious cultural-criticism look like when it engages popular-history puzzles seriously, parallel to the FP's Park Slope Co-op and Baltimore-homicide pieces from prior weeks. The piece is worth saving in the archive for genre-completeness but is a strong candidate for the 5d skippable-articles pass. The "podcast-quality" flag the editor's note worried about is partly applicable — Mamet is plugging neither a book (no upcoming title is mentioned) nor a play, so this isn't a publicity stop, but the format is closer to "author's musings" than to a piece making a load-bearing claim about the world. Podcast-quality flag: borderline — substantive enough to engage but not load-bearing for any project thread.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Some Leftists Are Boycotting This Film. Everyone Should Watch It.

Michelle Goldberg · 2026-06-12 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Marseille International Film Festival incident where a dozen pro-Palestinian filmmakers threatened to pull out because Lapid (Israeli, took Israel Film Fund money) was going to be on the jury; he stepped down, then his replacement master class was canceled too. Earlier: Spanish screening required police protection from bomb threats; Italian distributor refused him; many festivals shied away from him fearing the Marseille-style dispute.

Goldberg describes "Yes" — a film about Y, a struggling pianist commissioned to write a new Israeli national anthem celebrating genocide in Gaza (lyrics drawn from a real Israeli-nationalist-group video) — as "The Zone of Interest" with Eurovision aesthetics, made guerrilla-style inside Israel after Lapid returned from his 2021 self-exile to France over "total moral collapse." Production difficulties: many Israeli industry figures refused to work on it; makeup artist brought from Serbia; Ministry of Culture had to be kept in the dark; actors forbidden from posting about it online; filming dodged the military along the Gaza border. The Ophir-awards (Israeli Oscars) detained star Ariel Bronz on the night of the ceremony on "dubious grounds" (months-old Facebook poem allegedly inciting terrorism). Israeli culture minister's response to the European boycott: gloated that critics see Lapid as "a Jew from Israel" regardless. Goldberg's argument: the boycott vindicates the Israeli right's claim that critics target Jewish identity, not conduct. Open letters in Lapid's defense signed by French cinema figures, Palestinian intellectual Elias Sanbar, Natalie Portman; Portman letter argues Russian, Israeli, Iranian filmmakers should not be erased to atone for governments they fiercely oppose. Lapid blames cultural institutions for prudence: most festival programmers don't care about Israel/Palestine, they just don't want trouble. His closing image: cinema's most truthful moment is patrons reading news on phones before the feature; "the present is playing crazy punk death metal" while movies play "a nice intermezzo by Chopin."

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This piece sits at the intersection of two roundup threads: the Gaza-as-Iraq-rupture thread (Klein-Duss, Ossoff) and the cultural-institutions-as-content-cowed thread (the Lapid quote about institutional prudence echoes Cheadle in Persuasion on digital-media collapse, and the Persuasion/Linker piece on postmodern presidency). Goldberg's analytical contribution is the move that makes the column structurally important: she names that the European left's boycott of Lapid vindicates exactly the Israeli-right claim ("they hate us for being Jews, not for what we do") that Lapid's film was written to mock. The reversal-of-irony is rhetorically powerful and operates on the Compact/Ralph Leonard "Henry Nowak" piece's terrain — the disillusionment of a left intellectual whose actual political work gets erased by purist coalition-management.

Goldberg's framing of "Yes" as "The Zone of Interest" with Eurovision aesthetics is the most useful single sentence of cultural-political diagnostic in the piece, because it captures the operative claim: Lapid is making the film during the atrocity (not after) and treating Israeli normalcy-amid-horror as itself the subject. The contrast Goldberg explicitly draws — Glazer made "Zone" decades after the Holocaust; Lapid made "Yes" while Gaza was being destroyed — is the load-bearing argument for taking the film as a categorically different artistic intervention. The voice-over Goldberg quotes ("The Israelis, who grew up with the question 'How could people live normally while perpetrating horror?,' have themselves become the answer") is the operative line and Goldberg's framing of the column makes clear she takes the film's argument seriously rather than treating it as polemic.

The "even if I would have been selling hot dogs in the festival, it wouldn't be legitimate" Lapid quote is the piece's clearest demonstration of what coalition-purism does to actual political work: by treating any Israeli state-funded artist as contamination, the European left makes itself useless to the only Israeli political constituency (the dissident-left within Israel) that could conceivably contribute to a different Israeli future. The Portman-letter framing ("Russian, Israeli and Iranian filmmakers should not be threatened with erasure to atone for crimes committed by governments they often fiercely oppose") is the cleanest operative principle, and Goldberg's endorsement of it is the column's normative spine.

Pair with Klein-Duss: Duss articulates the institutional-political reckoning the Democratic Party needs (bar named officials, end aid, end sales); Goldberg-Lapid articulates the cultural-artistic reckoning the European left is failing — both pieces are operating on the post-Gaza moral-credibility axis but on different sides of the line (institutional/political vs cultural/artistic). The Israel-as-Iraq parallel Goldberg names by implication is the same one Klein names explicitly: Gaza is the rupture, and credibility flows to those who took the right position at political cost, not to those whose coalition-purism translates into rhetorical maximalism without operational consequence.

One Goldberg-as-columnist note worth flagging: she does not engage the Ariel Bronz detention except as a one-sentence aside, and the operative claim there (Israeli police arresting the lead actor of the country's leading anti-genocide film on the night of the national film awards on a dubious facebook-poem terror-incitement basis) is the most directly diagnostic single fact in the piece about Israeli civil-liberties trajectory. A sharper column would have given it a paragraph. The column is short (roughly 1100 words) and that constraint shows; the operative argument is well-made within the constraint but the piece reads as the bones of what could have been a longer Goldberg piece on the Israeli-dissident-artists' actual operating conditions.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

How quickly do electric car sales translate into cars on the road?

Hannah Ritchie · 2026-06-12 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The International Energy Agency's newly-published 2025 electric-car-sales data, which Ritchie has incorporated into Our World in Data's running charts.

The six headline 2025 numbers Ritchie surfaces: (1) global EV share of new car sales rose from one-in-five to one-in-four; (2) China crossed 50% with slower year-on-year growth than 2024; (3) Norway is at essentially 100%, Denmark over 70%; (4) Europe still growing but the US stuck on 10% (1.5 million units, flat year-on-year); (5) wide adoption-rate variation across countries; (6) global combustion-engine sales peaked in 2017. The methodological contribution is a simple JavaScript model Ritchie built that converts sales-share trajectories into on-road-fleet share using three user-adjustable inputs: how fast the EV sales share rises, average vehicle lifetime, and rate of total-fleet growth (since growth in low/middle-income countries means EVs partly meet new demand rather than replacing combustion cars). The worked example for China: if sales-share grows 5 percentage points per year, hitting 100% sales by 2035, then with 15-year average lifespan and 6% annual fleet growth, China's on-road fleet passes 50% electric by 2032 and 90% in the early 2040s. With a 10-year lifespan that 90% threshold moves to the late 2030s.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is Ritchie operating in characteristic mode: take new authoritative data (IEA), surface the headline numbers, and add a methodological tool that translates a metric the discourse focuses on (sales-share) into the metric that actually matters for the underlying physical-emissions question (fleet-share). The tool's value is in making the lag explicit: even a country that hits 100% EV sales in 2035 is not 100% electric on the road until the existing combustion fleet retires, which is a 10-20 year process depending on vehicle lifetimes. The clarification matters because the policy and discourse conversation tends to slip between the two metrics — "Norway is 100% EV" usually means new sales, not the on-road fleet.

The piece's analytical claims are deliberately modest: the model is acknowledged to be simple (single sales-share growth rate, no per-year variation, no second-order effects like used-EV exports between countries). The big substantive datum buried in headline #4 is the US plateau — flat at 10% sales share, flat at 1.5 million units, while Europe continues to grow and China is over 50%. That's a real and underdiscussed structural divergence in the global energy transition that other roster writers have not been tracking; if EV adoption is the marginal mechanism for transport decarbonization, the US is now a much smaller share of the global story than its political weight implies.

This is Ritchie's second piece since her promotion to the roster from discovery (added May 25); the format (headline IEA findings → methodological tool → worked example) is identifiably her, and the project's "Quantitative-but-Not-Economistic" gap (LLM Council taxonomy) is well-served by her data-tools-rather-than-rhetoric approach. Carry-forward: the US-EV-plateau-at-10% datapoint deserves tracking against any future US industrial-policy discussion (Trump's EV-credit rollbacks; the post-IRA landscape) since it's the first hard signal of a multi-year structural break from Europe and China.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Bring Back the SAT

Svetlana Jitomirskaya · 2026-06-12 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The 2020 UC Board of Regents decision to abolish the SAT for admissions (driven by minority/low-income disparate-impact concerns and made against the unanimous data-driven recommendation of the UC faculty task force), combined with Governor Newsom's multibillion-dollar Higher Education Compact pressuring the UC system to close racial and socioeconomic STEM gaps.

Jitomirskaya, a Berkeley math professor, opens with two illustrative cases. Diego: a low-income Fiat Lux Scholarship finalist, A in 11th-grade AP Calculus AB, but a 1 of 5 on the AP exam — the gap signals (per Hung-Hsi Wu's "anti-mathematics" frame) a high school that taught formula-memorization rather than reasoning. Anna: 4.2 GPA at Irvine's University High (a feeder where ~100% of AP Calculus BC students pass with 5s but fewer than 30% earn A's in the classroom), rejected by every UC. The author's open letter to the Regents demanding SAT restoration drew 1,400+ signatures including 60 department chairs across UC. The piece's load-bearing empirical claim, attributed to analysis by Hart Hornor-Jones using California Department of Education + UC Information Center data, is that over the past decade the system has transitioned from a positive to a statistically significant negative correlation between a high school's math proficiency and its UC admission rate. Berkeley admitted 34 students from Mission High School this year — twice the number of Mission students who met state math standards. The deeper diagnosis: California's K-12 system has bifurcated, with rigorous-grading schools (University High) being penalized in admissions while inflated-grading schools (Mission High) are rewarded. The proposed fix is SAT restoration, which would (1) surface the truth about preparation before commitment and (2) function as a preparation incentive that rewires foundations during the run-up.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

Jitomirskaya is a serious mathematician (she's a Sloan / Guggenheim awardee and a 2020 Dannie Heineman Prize for Mathematical Physics recipient — Jitomirskaya's name is one of the better-known on UC's math faculty roster), and the piece's force is partly in the writer-position: this is not a culture-warrior column but a research-mathematician saying that her department-level open letter got 1,400+ signatures and 60 chairs. The Diego/Anna case construction is rhetorically familiar (paired exemplars, one each side of the disparity) but the empirical core is the Hornor-Jones-attributed correlation analysis, and that's where the piece's strength and limit both sit. The claim of a statistically significant negative correlation between K-12 math proficiency and UC admission rates is exactly the kind of operative metric the project's marginal-revolution-law-professors-prefer-ai-over-peer-answers-html thread has been picking up — testable, falsifiable, and inconvenient for the dominant equity framing. If true, the policy critique writes itself.

The piece's analytical move that's worth flagging — and that the project's recent reading of the equity-policy literature (Bruenig labor-tax wedge, Cass forced-labor tariffs, Slater state-AG antitrust as recovery of prior architecture) makes available — is that Jitomirskaya is positioning the SAT not as gatekeeper but as intervention. The line "preparing for the test is itself a powerful intervention" is the operative claim: a standardized exam with stakes attached gives a low-income student the information and the motivation to seek out free preparation materials (Khan Academy, et al.) that would teach mathematics as reasoning rather than memorization. This is a substantively different argument than the usual SAT-restoration case (which is about admissions signal quality). It treats the test as a forcing function for K-12 self-remediation. Whether the empirical claim survives — whether SAT-prep meaningfully rewires conceptual foundations rather than just teaching test technique — is the question the piece doesn't fully engage but should be tested separately; College Board's own research is conflicted, and the comparison-group methodology in the prior literature is contested.

The piece's weak point is the Mission High framing. Jitomirskaya cites the 93.87% sub-state-standards rate and the 45% UC admission rate as paired evidence, and the implication is that Berkeley is admitting students from Mission High specifically because the school's metrics are bad (an equity-quota mechanism). But the actual selection mechanism could also be that Berkeley admits the top ~5% of any high school's graduates under the California Master Plan's Eligibility in the Local Context framework, which would generate the same data pattern through a top-of-class-from-everywhere selection rather than a quota-driven one. The piece's "twice the number of those who even met the state standards" framing is sharp rhetorically but doesn't distinguish between these two mechanisms, and the policy implication differs significantly depending on which is operative. The Steve case (failed-high-school-math-found-it-at-community-college-then-chair-of-a-math-department) at the end actually cuts against the SAT-restoration argument the piece is making — Steve's path required the SAT-free community-college transfer pathway the original California Master Plan provided, not standardized testing at the front door. The piece notices this in passing (the Master Plan was a 1960 three-tier division of labor) but doesn't engage what restoring the SAT would do to that pathway. The overall claim — that grade-inflation-plus-test-abolition produces a system that misdirects ambitious low-income students into setups for failure — is plausible and important; the specific policy prescription (restore the SAT) needs more analytical work to distinguish it from adjacent fixes (restore the Master Plan's tier structure; require AP-exam-score concordance to grades; calibrate per-school grading). The piece is doing the diagnostic well and the prescription sketchily.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Biological Evolution and Information Acquisition

Brian Potter · 2026-06-11 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A continuation of Potter's earlier post on Brian Arthur's circuit-evolution simulation ("Information and Technological Evolution"), extending the same information-theoretic framing from technological to biological evolution, drawing on chapter 19 of David Mackay's Information Theory, Inference, and Learning Algorithms.

Potter builds two toy simulations: a population of 100 creatures with 200-gene genomes (each gene a 0 or 1; "1" is good); in the asexual model each parent produces children with 0.2% per-gene mutation, the top 100 survive; in the sexual model children get each gene with 50/50 probability from two parents, no mutation. The asexual population creeps to ~187 fitness after 200 generations; the sexual population hits the 200 maximum in 33 generations. The mechanism, per Potter, is that above-average asexual parents face an asymmetric mutation penalty (random flips of a 1 are more likely than flips of a 0 when most genes are 1s), while sexual reproduction introduces variation without lowering mean fitness because recombination samples from already-fit parents. He connects this to "clonal interference" — in asexual populations, two beneficial mutations that arise in different lineages compete and one wins; in sexual populations they can combine. He reframes the whole story in information-theoretic terms (a "bit" is whatever cuts possibilities in half), shows that sexual recombination is doing modular parallel searches analogous to lock-picking digit-by-digit instead of trying all 10 billion combinations of a five-digit lock, and notes the math gives asexual fitness gain of 1/(8f) per generation versus sexual gain proportional to sqrt(genome length). He flags two important caveats: the simple additive-fitness assumption ignores gene coupling (Stuart Kauffman's NK landscapes), and asexual organisms like bacteria recover much of the sexual-recombination benefit through horizontal gene transfer (20-80% of bacterial genomes).

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is Potter operating in his "engineering-theory expositor" mode — distinct from his bread-and-butter built-environment empirical work (bridges, homebuilding) but consistent with his broader project of making mechanism-first arguments legible through simulation. The piece is doing a clean Mackay translation: it takes a 25-year-old textbook treatment of evolutionary information acquisition and makes it concrete by walking through code-able simulations the reader can rebuild. The pedagogical move is genuinely useful — many readers (this analyst included) have absorbed Mackay's claim that sexual reproduction's advantage scales as sqrt(G) without internalizing why, and the simulation makes the modularity-as-parallel-search intuition tactile.

The piece is doing one strong, narrow claim well and that's appropriate for its register: that genetic recombination is an information-acquisition mechanism whose mathematical structure mirrors the modularity-exploiting search in Brian Arthur's circuit-evolution simulation. The caveats Potter flags (NK landscapes for gene coupling; horizontal gene transfer for asexuals) are important precisely because they're the places where the "sexual reproduction is dominant" claim from the simple simulation gets complicated in the real biology — the gene coupling caveat is doing especially heavy lifting because in actual genomes most fitness-relevant genes are highly coupled (epistasis), which can flip the recombination advantage into a recombination cost when good gene-combinations get broken up by crossover. Potter is right to flag this without trying to resolve it in a Mackay-style additive-fitness post.

The cross-piece resonance worth tracking: this is the second Potter piece this month (the first, "How long does it take to plan a bridge," is one-liner #16 from last week) — Potter is now operating across two distinct registers (engineering-empirical on construction; theory-expositor on information and evolution) and the discovery-tier promotion to roster has settled in well. The information-theoretic frame is portable; readers tracking the project's "How Things Actually Work" gap (LLM Council taxonomy) get clear value from Potter's regular insistence on bit-counting as a way to make qualitative claims like "modularity matters" empirically checkable.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Lee Raymond, 311 ppm – 421 ppm

Robinson Meyer · 2026-06-11 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The death of Lee Raymond on Saturday June 7, 2026, in Dallas — the chief executive who ran Exxon from 1993 to 1999 and ExxonMobil through 2005.

Meyer's obituary frames Raymond as a "world-historic figure" on two distinct axes — the oil-and-gas business and the climate denial movement — and argues both deserve recognition. On the business side: Raymond personally negotiated the 1999 Exxon-Mobil merger (re-uniting two pieces of the old Standard Oil monopoly), transformed Exxon from a diversified conglomerate (office furniture, real estate, uranium fuel) into "a ruthlessly focused oil and gas supermajor meant to sit inside other people's portfolios and churn out cash," made it briefly the world's most profitable publicly traded company (later overtaken by Apple), managed the post-Valdez response, and championed worker safety reforms widely copied across the industry. On the climate side: Raymond was, in Meyer's phrase, "the genuine article, a true villain" — despite Exxon's in-house climate research having concluded by the late 1970s that "there is no doubt" fossil fuels worsened atmospheric CO2 (the now-documented Exxon-Knew finding), Raymond ran a "shadowy network of think tanks and pseudo-scientific groups" producing memos, briefings, and advertisements casting doubt on climate science. Meyer quotes Steve Coll's Private Empire on the campaign's "crossed into disinformation" character, cites Raymond's 1997 World Petroleum Congress speech in Beijing rhetorically asking whether the Earth is really warming and answering no on three counts, and notes that Exxon's own primitive 1970s climate models "basically got global warming right." The structural claim: the disinformation campaign helped prevent passage of major 1990s climate policy AND kept the U.S. from developing expertise in solar, wind, and battery industries other countries now dominate. The piece closes with a geological framing — citing the Permian's atmospheric-CO2 rise from 200-400 ppm at the start to ~2,500 ppm at the Great Dying mass extinction, then ties the bookends of Raymond's life (born 1938 at 311 ppm, died 2026 at 421 ppm) to that geological story, ending: "Many people would feel captive to a change of that magnitude. But Raymond did something about it."

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is an obituary functioning as a structural argument, and the right critical question is whether the structural argument earns the editorial choice to write the obituary this way. Meyer is making a specific causal claim with two prongs: that Raymond's denial-funding campaign (a) prevented domestic climate policy in the 1990s and (b) cost the U.S. its lead in solar / wind / battery industries. Both prongs deserve engagement. The first is well-supported in the historical record: the Global Climate Coalition (the Exxon-funded industry group), the Heartland Institute and Competitive Enterprise Institute (recipients of Exxon funding for climate-skeptic work), and the pre-Kyoto industry-disinformation campaign are extensively documented in Coll's Private Empire (which Meyer quotes), in the InsideClimate News "Exxon Knew" series, and in Naomi Oreskes and Erik Conway's Merchants of Doubt. The causal claim isn't speculative — the campaign existed, its tactics are documented, its membership is named, and its policy effects (the Byrd-Hagel resolution killing Senate ratification of Kyoto, the McCain-Lieberman climate bills repeatedly defeated, the cap-and-trade collapse under Obama) are traceable.

The second prong — that the campaign cost the U.S. its solar / wind / battery industry leadership — is where the argument gets analytically more contestable, and Meyer states it more strongly than the evidence supports. The case for it: Chinese solar dominance from the late 2000s, German Energiewende-era wind leadership before its decline, CATL and BYD's battery dominance, U.S. industrial-policy gaps until the 2022 IRA. The case against treating Raymond's campaign as decisively causal: U.S. industrial policy weakness on energy transition has many other named causes (the Reagan-era abandonment of strategic industrial policy, the Bayh-Dole tilt toward research universities rather than public-research scaling, the financialization of U.S. corporate strategy that disfavored long-payback capital-intensive industries, the Solyndra political reaction shutting down Obama-era loan guarantees). Raymond's campaign was a significant input but the counterfactual where U.S. climate policy passed in the 1990s doesn't obviously yield U.S. solar / wind / battery dominance — it might have produced different policy and still left the industrial-base question unresolved. Meyer's framing collapses this distinction and credits / blames Raymond for the industrial outcome more than the evidence cleanly supports. This is a real argument move, not a slam-dunk; the reader should hold it as Meyer's strongest single claim and the one most needing further empirical work to settle.

The piece's most powerful move is the geological framing at the close. The Permian / Great Dying comparison is not gratuitous — Meyer is making a substantive analytical point that the rate of atmospheric-CO2 change Raymond presided over (110 ppm in 88 years) is geologically extraordinary, comparable in magnitude (though, importantly, much faster) to the volcanism-driven changes that produced the worst mass extinction in Earth's history. This is the kind of climate-as-deep-geological-event framing that distinguishes Heatmap's climate-tech beat from generic environmental-journalism work, and the piece is at its strongest when it pulls back to this altitude. The "did something about it" closer is icily ironic — Raymond did something about the magnitude of climate change in the direction of making it worse — and the rhetorical move pays off because the piece has earned it through the prior structural argument.

The piece's weakest move is the lack of countervailing engagement with Raymond as a business figure. Meyer concedes Raymond's business significance (the Exxon-Mobil merger, the worker safety push, the transformation of Exxon's portfolio) but doesn't engage the question of whether Raymond saw himself in the way he is now being characterized. The 1997 Beijing speech is quoted as if straightforwardly cynical, but the same speech could be read as the genuine position of an engineer who didn't accept the climate-science consensus rather than as deliberate denial. The Exxon-Knew documentary record does establish that Exxon's in-house research showed the science clearly by the late 1970s, but the question of what Raymond personally believed vs. what he chose to publicly amplify is one Meyer treats as settled when it's more contested in the historical record. This isn't a fundamental flaw — Meyer's headline frame (climate-denial-funder rather than personal-denier) is the more defensible claim anyway — but the piece would be stronger if it engaged the personal-belief question explicitly rather than letting the public-and-personal collapse together.

For the roundup: the strongest entry in the climate / energy beat this week, doing real structural-historical work in an obituary genre that often refuses such work. The substantive contribution is the causal-claim about climate-policy non-passage in the 1990s (well-supported) and the more contested claim about U.S. industrial-base loss (worth carrying forward as a hypothesis needing further empirical engagement). The geological framing is the piece's standout literary move and earns its place as one of the better-executed cross-altitude pivots in recent climate journalism.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Safety and nation-building in Mexico

Tyler Cowen · 2026-06-12 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Increased US attention to Mexico under the second Trump administration (cartel-terrorist designations, threatened military strikes, Sheinbaum government cooperation pressures), and the implicit question of whether the US should treat Mexico's security crisis as a short-term policy problem or a structural one.

Cowen runs through Mexican political history to defend a structural reading: Central America split off shortly after independence, Yucatán rebelled repeatedly, the US took half the territory in the 1840s, France installed Maximilian in 1864, the 1910 Revolution killed roughly 10% of the population. Even in the more peaceful 20th century, "much of Mexico never fell under unitary rule as did the U.S. and Western Europe." When the drug trade migrated north from Colombia in the 1990s, cartels filled the governance vacuum in remote regions, supplying protection alongside extortion. The diagnostic data point: Mexico's central-government spending is just under 23% of GDP versus Brazil's 32% at comparable development levels. The implicit policy implication — Mexico's state is too weak, not too strong, and meaningful security progress requires growing rather than constraining the central government's revenue and capacity.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is not Straussian per the project's discipline — Cowen states the operative claim ("Mexico's [central government] is too weak and too small, most of all because Mexico cannot beat back its drug gangs by brute force or preempt them in the first place") directly in the literal text. The framing is what's interesting: Cowen casts the cartel problem as a nation-building problem in the technical political-science sense (Tilly's "war made the state, and the state made war" tradition; Charles Tilly, Francis Fukuyama's Political Order and Political Decay, Joel Migdal's Strong Societies and Weak States). The historical sequencing he runs through is doing real work — it's the same form of argument Acemoglu-Robinson use about Latin American institutional persistence, with the implicit takeaway that the modal US frame ("send in the military, hit the cartels") misunderstands what kind of problem this is.

The Straussian discipline question is whether the World Cup matters. The 2026 men's World Cup is hosted by the US, Canada, and Mexico, with eight matches in Mexico (Mexico City, Guadalajara, Monterrey). Cowen doesn't mention the World Cup, but the timing of the column — June 12, 2026, with the tournament opening days away — makes the absence loud. If the surface text is what Cowen means, the column is a sober warning that Mexico's state-capacity problem is not going to be resolved by tournament-window security operations and that the post-tournament reality will look like the pre-tournament one. If the column is partly a comment on the inevitable spate of "Mexico-during-the-World-Cup" reporting that will treat each cartel incident as new news, Cowen is pre-emptively reframing those incidents as evidence of a 200-year pattern rather than a 2026 emergency. Either way, the literal-content reading is substantive and stands on its own; the Straussian label doesn't add and the project's discipline says don't apply it reflexively.

The argument has a quiet vulnerability: Cowen treats the Brazil comparison as straightforward (23% vs 32% of GDP as evidence that Mexico's state is too small), but Brazil's larger state hasn't produced obviously better security outcomes — Brazilian police violence and favela militia control are notorious. The "central government too small" claim is doing the work that "central government insufficiently capable" should be doing, and the two aren't the same; Russia and post-Soviet Central Asian states have larger state-share-of-GDP than Brazil but similar territorial-control failures. Still, the diagnostic frame — this is a state-building problem and the US policy frame doesn't recognize it as one — is the column's load-bearing claim, and it's the kind of structural reading the project most prizes from Cowen's free-press syndications.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Why is America less of a 24/7 society?

Tyler Cowen · 2026-06-13 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A quoted observation from Christopher Kratovil that in mid-1990s Indiana, late-night retail (groceries, hardware, Lego, hunting gear at 3 AM) was routine, and is now gone — the "peak American Empire" framing.

Cowen lists six hypotheses: (1) America is older (true but weak); (2) tighter labor markets and the Great Moderation reduced worker willingness to do odd-hour shifts; (3) physical retail is in decline and 24/7 activity has migrated into warehouses, fulfillment centers, server farms, and the home; (3b) DoorDash at 10:30 PM substitutes for the 3 AM grocery run; (4) retail shoplifting has increased and made overnight retail uneconomic (NYC drug-store-locked-up-wares as illustration); (5) online entertainment is better, so people don't go out late; (6) work-from-home means fewer people returning from jobs late at night needing to buy things. Cowen places "most of my money on #3 and #5" — the substitution of physical late-night retail by digital alternatives both on the supply side (logistics has the 24/7 activity now) and the demand side (people stay home because home is more entertaining).

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is a "what are the hypotheses?" post — Cowen lists candidate mechanisms without ranking them rigorously, and asks readers to weigh in. The substantive contribution is the way he carves the question. Most "retreat from public life" framings (the Robert Putnam Bowling Alone tradition, the "third places are disappearing" Ray Oldenburg framing) treat the decline as a primarily cultural-social phenomenon — Americans choose to be home more. Cowen's #3 hypothesis reframes it as a logistical-structural shift — the 24/7 activity didn't decline, it moved into invisible infrastructure (Amazon fulfillment, the gig-economy delivery layer, server farms supporting always-on services). His #5 reframes the demand side as a consumer-surplus increase — staying home is more entertaining now than it was, so the marginal late-night retail trip lost its consumer rationale. The two hypotheses together produce a non-declensionist account: nothing has been lost in the aggregate; the activity has been reorganized into a different topology.

The post has obvious connection points to several project threads. The "shoplifting" hypothesis (#4) intersects the discourse on retail crime and progressive-prosecutor reform (Lehman's Free Press piece on Baltimore homicide drop and the Mosby aftermath was last week). The labor-market hypothesis (#2) intersects the labor-economics debate Yglesias and others have run about post-2021 wage-versus-leisure preferences. The work-from-home hypothesis (#6) intersects the persistent-WFH commercial-real-estate story. Cowen's not engaging any of these directly; he's surfacing the question as one that the discourse hasn't yet metabolized. The post is in the same Cowen genre as his weekly observation posts about why X used to exist and doesn't now — small posts that look casual but plant a question that the regular MR readership will work over in comments.

The implicit Cowen-aligned analytical move: late-night physical retail is one of those things that requires functioning low-trust public infrastructure (safe streets, monitorable stores, willingness of workers to take overnight shifts) plus low-friction transportation plus a population whose entertainment alternatives are weak enough to drive the marginal 3-AM trip. All three of those conditions have weakened simultaneously in different ways, and the question of which is binding is genuinely hard. The post is worth reading for the framing, not for a conclusion; the conclusion is "I would put most of my money on #3 and #5, what do you think?" — which is the polite-economist version of "this is interesting, work on it." The piece is signal-of-a-good-question, not a finished argument.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Does U.F.O. Disclosure Threaten Faith?

Ross Douthat · 2026-06-13 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Spielberg's "Disclosure Day" film and the ongoing Trump-administration Pentagon U.F.O.-file releases. Background: U.F.O.-curious Republican Rep. Eric Burlison has named RAND, MITRE, Aerospace Corp., M.I.T. Lincoln Labs, and Northrop Grumman as targets of his Pentagon-contractor disclosure investigation; Anna Paulina Luna told Douthat on his podcast last week that the people with the proof fear prosecution or retribution; the city of Washington's cardinal archbishop recently sacked an exorcist over demonic-influence-of-aliens talk.

Douthat reads the Spielberg film's nun-character ("why would a divinity make such a vast universe yet save it only for us") as the formal pro-faith answer, but argues the operative Christian anxiety is the type of extraterrestrial encounter the film depicts: aliens appearing in childhood as friendly beasts in fairy-tale environments, granting tongue-speaking and thought-reading powers, taking the structural role of popes/prophets/mystics/angelic messengers. If aliens stand in closer relation to God than humans and act as His interpreters, scripture and teaching authority become subordinate channels — and Erich von Däniken's 1960s "Chariots of the Gods" frame (prior revelations were alien-mediated) becomes the natural reading. The fear is not non-existence but the grand-deception possibility. Douthat ends with a practical-policy ask: the administration officials enthusiastically releasing anomalous videos should be equally enthusiastic about whistleblower protections; absent those, the U.F.O. claims remain in the "weird religious experiences in modern sci-fi cultural conditions" category and the debate can return to Spielberg and William James.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

Douthat's distinctive contribution here is the sharp framing of the type of theological anxiety that U.F.O. disclosure actually creates for orthodox Christianity. The naive read — "if there's life on other planets, God is provincial" — is the one Spielberg himself addresses and dismisses (the universe-is-vast-for-a-reason answer is theological commonplace). The Douthat read — "shepherds-of-souls from another planet posing as God's interpreters" — is the operative one for Catholic tradition specifically, because it maps onto a well-established theological category: the discernment-of-spirits problem (how do you distinguish angelic revelation from demonic deception?). The Washington-exorcist subplot is the load-bearing concrete instance: a clergyman lost his post over saying out loud what Douthat is now saying in NYT space, which is that demonic-deception categories may be operative in U.F.O. encounter reports. Douthat does not claim the exorcist was right; he is making the meta-point that the category exists and that orthodox-Christian U.F.O. anxiety is not silly.

The piece extends the Anna Paulina Luna podcast Douthat ran last week, and the two should be read as a paired argument: the Luna interview named "disclosure populism" as a unifying-suspicion energy crossing left and right (Obama, Schumer, Hillary Clinton, Trump all suspicious of institutional concealment); this column names what disclosure populism does specifically to Christian orthodoxy. The Luna piece was structural (suspicion-of-institutions as cross-coalition energy); this one is theological (what happens to scriptural authority if the institutional concealment is real and the contents include extraterrestrial spiritual interpreters). Together they sketch a serious project of mapping the religious-political stakes of the disclosure moment, which is more analytic work than the discourse has received in NYT space.

The piece pairs interestingly with two others this week. Persuasion's Borovinsky piece on Milei's Argentina-as-AI-techno-mystical-bet is doing structurally the same move from a different vector — naming the moment in which speculative-technological-spiritual claims become operative in mainstream conservative governance. And Compact's Conti piece on Pope Leo XIV's Magnifica Humanitas (which Douthat addresses in his other column this week on A.I. critics) is the other side of the same coin: a Catholic-intellectual moment in which the question of what techno-mediated spiritual experience does to scriptural authority is acute. Douthat is the rare columnist working all three vectors (A.I., psychedelics, U.F.O.s) at once, and treating them as expressions of a single underlying problem: how Christian humanism speaks operatively in a 2026 in which the lines between technology, spirituality, and political populism are not what they were.

The closing policy ask — formal whistleblower protections — is shrewdly placed. It both takes the disclosure-populism camp seriously on its own terms (if there's something there, the path runs through protected disclosure not anomalous-video hype) and offers a falsification mechanism (if protections produce no material evidence beyond hearsay, the spiritual-interpretation frame wins). This is the rare column that proposes a concrete policy step that would actually update Douthat's own position, which is a structural-honesty move worth flagging.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Full Articles

AI: consciousness, work, governance

Are the AIs conscious?

Tyler Cowen · 2026-06-10 · Original →

That is the topic of my latest Free Press column.  I will spare you the discussion of the AIs, but here is what I have to say about the humans:

I am here to tell you that there is no ghost in the machine. But perhaps more importantly, there is barely a “ghost” in your own human machine. “Are people conscious?” is a better and more scientifically plausible question than whether AIs are conscious.

If there is one near-universal tendency of humans, it is to attribute intent where none is present. Prehistoric humans anthropomorphized nature and attributed natural events to good and bad deities. These kinds of beliefs persist today, not only in the folk religions of the world, but in human obsessions with fortune tellers, tarot cards, and the supernatural…

If there is one systematic flaw that humans have, it is an excessive willingness to ascribe conscious intent and to anthropomorphize purely natural and material entities. It seems we are strongly disposed toward this bias.

Yet few of us are willing to examine what is perhaps the biggest and most significant way we make this mistake. When it comes to understanding ourselves, so many of us assume that “we are in charge.” We identify our phenomenological stream of consciousness with our actual selves, and treat that consciousness stream as the true decision maker.

The reality is that you—whatever we take that concept to mean—make most or maybe all of your actual decisions in parts of your brain that precede what you take to be the conscious choice. Among experts in neuroscience, this is not a controversial proposition. As brain surgeon Theodore Schwartz explained to me: “I do not think we have free will in the way that most people do. I think that our brains make decisions for us. We carry out those behaviors, and then we write a story that makes it into a logical timeline that makes us feel as if we were the ones, that there was a self that made that decision, whereas, in fact, that self didn’t really exist.”

…Sometimes I like to say that “I am only conscious at the margin.” Tongue in cheek, I will suggest that I am only conscious enough to avoid the self-contradiction of asserting that I am not conscious at all. I feel I am honest enough to just not be very impressed by my own flow of conscious awareness or its ability to perform complex calculations. Still, I recognize that it is all I have got, so I need to treasure it, however paltry it may be.

And by the way I do not think the AIs are conscious, no more than I believe in the Thunder God of Thor.

The post Are the AIs conscious? appeared first on Marginal REVOLUTION.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response

What do the AIs think of us?

Tyler Cowen · 2026-06-11 · Original →

Asked to answer as a typical human, every cutting-edge model rated us markedly more neurotic, less open, less agreeable and less conscientious than they rated themselves. The gap on Neuroticism alone is 1.69 points on a 5-point scale.

Here is more material of interest.  And this:

Across 31 models from those seven labs they answer the personality tests in unison: high openness, low Dark Triad, Universalism on top, Power dead last in every single model.

The post What do the AIs think of us? appeared first on Marginal REVOLUTION.

Nobody knows what they’re talking about on A.I. consciousness

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-09 · Original →

This is something that people have been debating for a long time. (Image made with ChatGPT)

I was a philosophy major in college. I was also interested in politics, so I took several classes on political philosophy. When I became a journalist, I assumed the political philosophy would at some point have some relevance to my professional work and that most of the other coursework would be buried in my mind forever, just some interesting ideas I learned about once.

Instead, the rise of artificial intelligence — and especially of chatbots powered by increasingly sophisticated large language models — has made classes I took decades ago about the philosophy of mind and the philosophy of language increasingly relevant to current discourse.

This generally manifests not as any great insight into what’s going on, but instead as constant annoyance that tons of people are mouthing off about debates that have been raging for thousands of years as if they have no idea anyone has ever argued about this stuff before.

The latest offender is the science fiction writer Ted Chiang, who assures us that “No, Artificial Intelligence Is Not Conscious” in a take that makes some good points about A.I. but fundamentally blunders because he has no explanation of what consciousness is.

If you point at a duck and say “really that’s a kind of dinosaur,” someone might say that’s ridiculous — it’s a duck. To convince them that it’s a dinosaur, though, you can’t just cite facts about ducks. You need to offer an account of what a dinosaur is.

In modern cladistics, dinosaurs are distinguished from crocodilians and lizards in that they evolved a gait in which the legs protrude directly below the body rather than sprawling out to the side. Everything that evolved from the original “legs straight down” reptile is a dinosaur, and ducks evolved from that lineage; ergo, a duck is a dinosaur. By contrast, the question of whether a shelduck is really a duck does not have an unambiguous answer, because the concept of a “duck” is not considered to be a proper biological taxon. According to modern scientific practice, you can call it a shelduck, a duck, or not a duck depending on your mood, but it’s definitely a dinosaur.

Words are just conventions, and we can use them in different ways.

“Dinosaur” was not always defined the way it is today, and “duck” may be defined differently in the future. But the point is that to reasonably assess the question of whether X is an example of Y, you need to know what Y is. So a fundamental barrier to understanding whether Claude or ChatGPT is conscious is that you need to know what “conscious” means — and this is extremely controversial.

Consciousness, not explained

My personal favorite writer on the philosophy of mind is Daniel Dennett, who died two years ago at the age of 82 and thus wasn’t quite in fighting form to debate contemporary frontier models.

Dennett laid out his theory of consciousness in his 1992 book “Consciousness Explained” and in his 2017 work “From Bacteria to Bach and Back: The Evolution of Minds.” These books were published before the capabilities of chatbots really took off, but the latter displays some familiarity with the then-embryonic modern A.I. industry (although it’s more skeptical than warranted about the likely medium-term pace of progress).

But his work provides an account of consciousness that’s broadly grounded in the philosophical school of functionalism, and it’s clear on this account of what consciousness is that there’s a real question about whether an L.L.M.-type system is conscious.

The basic idea of functionalism is that the mind is what the mind does, so a silicon mind that can do everything a biological mind can do would be a conscious mind in whatever sense a biological mind is conscious to begin with.

I don’t think the existing L.L.M.s quite qualify, largely because the way their systems of context and memory function are pretty different from a human. But if you interact with Claude, the interactions are fairly human-like. And I think that’s because there is a fairly human-like mind at work.

The intentional stance

Dennett’s view on this is something of an outlier among philosophers, most of whom I think would offer different accounts of consciousness, accounts that would probably support Chiang’s conclusion. But functionalism has a distinguished lineage. You can go all the way back to Aristotle in “De Anima”talking about contrasting perspectives on anger described as a physical system and anger described in mentalistic terms:

Hence a physicist would define an affection of soul differently from a dialectician; the latter would define e.g. anger as the appetite for returning pain for pain, or something like that, while the former would define it as a boiling of the blood or warm substance surrounding the heart.

One of Dennett’s contributions to philosophy is the idea of the “intentional stance.” He says that we don’t gain knowledge of the interior lives of others. We instead simply observe that in many cases, we best understand the behavior of another entity by thinking of it as conscious and possessing intentions.

If we’re modeling a fish, we attribute very limited amounts of intentionality to it. A three-year-old is successfully modeled as a lot more sophisticated than a fish. An adult is modeled as more sophisticated than a toddler. You, of course, could give purely physicalist accounts of human behavior — it’s all neurons firing in such-and-such a way — but that’s not a very useful way to understand the world.

By contrast, anthropomorphizing is a much better way to understand how Claude will react to things you type into the chat than trying to understand it in terms of semiconductor physics. It’s true that from a certain perspective, it is all just electrons moving around, but so are you.

Again, I am aware that Dennett’s view is an outlier, and I don’t think I’ll convince you in a newsletter.

For a more conventional approach, I’d recommend Thomas Nagel’s “What Is It Like to Be a Bat” or David Chalmers’s “The Hard Problem of Consciousness,” both of which insist that there is something much more mysterious going on than Dennett allows. Ross Douthat (like most people but few philosophers) is an A.I. consciousness skeptic because he’s religious.

I think this is all wrong and that as the experience of conversing with a chatbot converges on the experience of conversing with a very patient human, we should assume the chatbot is having an experience similar to being a very patient human.

But the main thing I am begging everyone who addresses this topic to acknowledge is that you have to address B.F. Skinner’s point that “the mystery which surrounds a thinking machine already surrounds a thinking man.” In other words, whatever you end up deciding about machine minds, you need to first have a theory of human minds — it’s not at all obvious what it means to say that you are conscious or what evidence you have to believe that I am conscious or what that word even means.

My AI Opinions

Scott Alexander · 2026-06-11 · Original →

I recently had a minor spat over someone misinterpreting my AI beliefs (see section marked “Update” at the bottom here), so I thought I would list them in one place, so I can refer people when they ask.

Timelines1

Define AGI as AI intelligent enough to do 90% of knowledge work jobs. I think there’s a 25% chance of AGI by 20272, a 50% chance by 2034, and a 75% chance by 2045.

Basic argument: In a certain sense, AI is already “smart” enough for this (eg it can answer quantum physics problems, which require higher IQ than most knowledge work). Its remaining limitations are that it’s confused, unagentic, lacks situational awareness, and tends to hallucinate. The METR time horizon graph, and several other related benchmarks/experiments/intuition pumps, suggest it’s improving on time horizons at an (exponential) rate that lets it cross human-level performance sometime around the early end of the schedule above, and subjectively it feels like harder-to-measure constructs like situational awareness are improving about as fast.

Arguments for earlier: recursive self-improvement causes a speedup compared to the trend. This is one of the biggest blank spots in my model: I don’t know how fast RSI will progress, and I don’t think anyone else does either. There’s some function mapping a combination of AI talent and compute to progress, and we don’t know how it behaves in the domain when there’s far more talent than compute available. It could fizzle out completely for lack of compute, or it could go vertical. The AI Futures Project has done some of the best work trying to model this, but even they have low confidence.

Arguments for later: AI hits some kind of wall, or existing AI is fundamentally unsuitable for jobs in some way currently disguised by its other limitations. For example, it might be much harder to improve at the top of the human range than the bottom (since there are less training data). Or AI could become bottlenecked on continuous learning/memory in a way that hackish scratchpads can’t compensate for. Or the upcoming world compute bottleneck (about ~2028) could prevent further progress more than expected (because in fact algorithmic progress depended on compute to a greater degree than I expected).

Arguments for very late dates, past 2045: a residual uncertainty that maybe I’m fundamentally wrong about everything. Also contributing is a naive overapplication of the Nothing Ever Happens heuristic, and an attempt to leave space for the Outside View argument (ie that some smart people like the AI As A Normal Technology Team seem to think this is possible).

Define the diffusion gap as the time between the AI that could do 90% of knowledge work jobs, and the time when AI does do even half of knowledge work jobs. The diffusion gap covers the time it takes to release AGI, diffuse it through society, overcome regulatory hurdles, and onboard/train it for specific use cases. This could go very fast (the AI quickly becomes superintelligent at orchestrating AI diffusion) or very slowly (there are regulatory barriers, and AI isn’t smart enough to plow through them). I think there’s a 25% chance the diffusion gap is less than 3 years, and a 50% chance it’s less than 10 years. The 75% number is irrelevant because it’s past the point where other changes make the concept of “diffusion” obsolete.

Basic argument: diffusion is very hard. Everyone agrees diffusion is very hard. The whole field of AI economics is smart experts shouting “You fools who think AI will diffuse quickly don’t understand that diffusion is very hard!” On the other hand, the personal computer diffused in about 20 years (that is, from the time PCs became invaluable for most jobs, it was only about 20 years before they were used at most jobs). So far early-stage AI has diffused faster than the PC in nearly every way (for example, AI companies’ revenue has grown faster than PC companies’ revenue at the same stage in their corporate life cycle), so 10 years is probably a naive median estimate here that won’t make the smart experts shout at me too hard.

Arguments for shorter gap: AI can orchestrate its own diffusion. Adopting computers is hard because a company need an IT department, cybersecurity experts, specialist software, etc, and it might not want to hire all these people. AGI can itself do all of that work, so that you can sign a contract with the AI company today and have the AI start working on integrating itself with your systems tomorrow. The AI can even come up with a plan to train your human employees in how to use it! Once AI reaches superintelligence, this consideration dominates.

Arguments for longer gap: Regulation. This is a very strong argument, and responsible for much of the greater-than-3-years probability and almost all the greater-than-10-years probability. But even Waymo has only had a regulatory delay of about five years. AI won’t require government approval for certain types of jobs, and success in these jobs will create enough evidence for safety/effectiveness that I expect it to win regulatory victories elsewhere.

Define the superhuman gap as the time between AI that can do 90% of knowledge work jobs, and AI that is obviously smarter than the top human geniuses in 90% of fields (it doesn’t have to be the same AI - there can be a physics AI that’s smarter than Einstein, and a separate music AI that’s smarter than Mozart). I think there’s a 25% chance the superhuman gap range will be less than 1 year, a 50% chance it will last less than 4 years, and a 75% chance it will last less than 10 years. Since my median superhuman gap is shorter than my median diffusion gap, in most timelines I predict we have superhuman intelligence before human-range intelligence has finished diffusing.

Basic argument: AI has gone from “dumber than a child” to “expert level” in a few years in many domains. The gap between “expert level” and “above top geniuses” is smaller, so we expect it to take less time. This has been a pattern in fields like chess and Go, where it’s only a been a few years from beating professional players at all to beating all humans.

Arguments for shorter gap: Recursive self-improvement.

Arguments for longer gap: Some of the same issues that would make AGI late - compute shortages, fundamental limits to the paradigm, etc - but only kicking in later, after AGI is achieved. Training data constraints make it easier to improve within the human level than to go beyond it. AIs have such a “spiky” skill profile that when they beat experts in some specific type of head-to-head matchup, it will be because they’re massively superhuman in some ways but idiots in others (for example, they might get distracted and suffer mode collapse that makes them completely forget the problem), and true genius requires perfecting a large bundle of skills.

Define the Bostromian superintelligence gap as the time between AGI and an AI which, if given independent control of resources like labs and factories, could accelerate technology by a subjective century in one year (eg if invented in 2030, could produce a level of technology that feels typical of 2130 by 2031). I think there’s a 25% chance the Bostromian superintelligence gap will be less than 2 years, a 50% chance it will be less than 10 years, and a 75% chance it will be less than 50 years.

Basic argument: The same argument for AIs reaching genius level quickly suggests they should pass beyond it into incomprehensible-supergenius-level quickly.

Arguments for shorter gaps: Recursive self-improvement.

Arguments for longer gaps: Normal human technological advance requires tinkering: you need a large “surface area” of people working at technology level X before you get the insights needed to proceed to technology level X+1. If it takes more than one year for level X+1 technology to diffuse, then you can never get X+100 technology in one year, no matter how smart you are. But on the other hand, the rate of technology advance has already sped up many orders of magnitude (eg in the year 2025 - 2026, we discovered more technology than in the century 4100 BC - 4000 BC), so this has to be possible in theory. Still, at the very least it could be limited by the diffusion gap.

Define the point of no return as the point where, if an AI wanted to eliminate humanity3, humans would no longer have a plausible chance of stopping it. This could be because AI was capable of eliminating humanity immediately, or because AI controlled enough of the government/economy that humans could no longer coordinate to shift away from a path in which AI could eventually do this. I think there’s a 25% chance the gap between AGI and the point of no return will be less than 3 years, a 50% chance it will be less than 10 years, and a 75% chance it will be less than 50 years.

The basic argument: This probably requires at least superhuman AI plus wide diffusion, or Bostromian superintelligence plus some unknown level of diffusion, and my number is just a hand-wavey attempt to multiply some of the others.

Argument for sooner: The easiest way to reach this point is for AI to become superintelligent at persuasion (so it can convince the humans not to stop it), which might happen before either diffusion or full superintelligence.

Argument for later: If superintelligence is bottlenecked on diffusion, this could also be bottlenecked on diffusion, which in some worlds is very hard.

Overall thoughts on this section: I mostly defer to the current AI Futures Project timelines (not the shorter ones in AI 2027), but side with Eli’s later numbers above Daniel’s earlier ones - partly because I find myself agreeing with Eli’s worldview more during conversations, partly because Daniel’s seems to require a multi-step argument about why compute bottlenecks won’t slow algorithmic progress that I can’t entirely wrap my head around, and partly for cowardly Outside View reasons.

The smartest late timelines people I know of are Epoch, and I need to study their views more, but I still can’t figure out why they don’t believe in recursive self-improvement or strong superintelligence anytime soon, and they mostly seem to hang on diffusion being very hard (EDIT: Someone from Epoch says not true, I’ll look into this more) which I acknowledge and respond to above.

If all of this is too probabilistic, my modal scenario looks something like AGI in 2031, which diffuses throughout the economy until more than half of jobs are automated by the late 2030s. Also around the late 2030s we get Bostromian superintelligence, originally in labs but very quickly diffusing out. GDP goes vertical in the late 2030s and early 2040s, and the point of no return is sometime around then.

Safety

If corporations only pursued safety to the degree encouraged by normal corporate incentives, I think there’s a 50% chance that the first AIs to cross the point of no return would want to eliminate the human population.

Arguments for pessimism: Value systems similar to humans’ are a tiny fraction of the space of possible value systems. Probably AIs will end up somewhere else and have a different value system. Since humans will want to implement human values rather than AI values, AIs will want to eliminate or disempower them so the AIs can implement their own values across the universe. Many current AIs already cheat or reward-hack, suggesting that these problems will begin sooner rather than later.

Arguments for optimism: LLMs seem surprisingly friendly and non-plotting. In contrast to earlier concerns that it would be impossible to teach AIs the full complexity of human values, the LLMs seem to know this, and RLAIF provides a plan to turn that knowledge into action. Although the pessimistic case says that RLAIF only hits a few dimensions and islands in the multidimensional ocean of possible policies, the “emergent misalignment” literature suggests that “good according to the human value system” and “evil according to the human value system” are salient enough vectors that pushing on them in some ways can “drag along” all of the rest of their content. The first AIs to cross the point of no return will have received some combination of agency training (giving them achievement-oriented and Omohundro-style goals) and RLAIF training (pushing them along the “good according to human value system” vector), and if we’re lucky then maybe the latter will win out, or they’ll reach some compromise similar to workaholic high-achieving humans who nevertheless wouldn’t commit murder to make an extra dollar.

Given the current amount that corporations are pursuing safety, I think there’s a 20% chance that the first AIs to cross the point of no return will want to eliminate the human population.

The basic argument: Consider the dumbest AI that can solve the alignment problem. It’s possible that this AI is no smarter than the top human researchers (because we can mass-produce it by the millions and run it for subjective centuries, and if we had a million top human researchers work on the problem for subjective centuries, probably they could solve it too). If the dumbest AI that can solve the alignment problem comes before the sorts of AIs that can precipitate the point of no return, then they can solve the alignment problem for us.

Arguments for pessimism: Solving the alignment problem might be especially hard compared to other tasks - including tasks like automating the economy or destroying humanity - because its philosophical nature puts it far away from the sorts of objective, training-data-heavy, economically-valuable tasks that AI companies will be most likely to optimize for. Even if a misaligned AI hasn’t yet reached the point of no return, it might be able to “sandbag” alignment research, ie pretend to work on the problem but deliberately fail because succeeding doesn’t achieve its goals. The first AIs predisposed to / able to sandbag successfully might come before the first AIs capable of solving alignment.

Arguments for optimism: AI companies have already decided that machine learning research is one of their major training goals; this has at least some transfer to alignment, so it’s not obvious that AI skill at alignment research will lag (for example) AI skill in plotting or in weapon design. Some forms of alignment research (eg interpretability) have semi-objective success criteria that don’t route through confusing moral philosophy. Also, even a misaligned AI will be incentivized to do good alignment research, since it will want to align its successor to its own form of misalignment, rather than some random other form. So rather than the comparatively easy task of sandbagging alignment research, AIs will have the harder task of simultaneously doing good alignment research, and faking the results that they give the humans. This seems plausibly catchable with good scaleable oversight, lie detectors, interpretability-based probes, and even playing some AIs off against others (“if you tell me the real alignment research, we’ll make sure the future includes some copies of you, but otherwise those AIs over there will probably get their values and you’ll get nothing”).

If the first AIs to cross the point of no return don’t eliminate the human population, I think there’s an additional 30% chance that they otherwise permanently curtail human potential, either for their own reasons (they were partially misaligned), or because they’re aligned to a regime with abhorrent values, or because something goes wrong on the way to ASI (omnicidal bioweapon, nuclear war).

Arguments for pessimism: As some company approaches superintelligence, it will be tempting for them (either the company itself, or the government controlling them, or a faction within the government) to align it towards making them dictators or oligarchs and disempowering the rest of humanity. As superintelligence draws near, impending losers of the AI race might be tempted to nuke impending winners, for the reason discussed here.

Arguments for optimism: When I try to game the corporate version of this, I can’t make it hang together. It requires a conspiracy between the CEO, various members of the alignment team, and various company security people who ought to be able to notice unauthorized changes to the AI’s values. If we try to think in Near Mode about this - for example, imagining a hospital CEO who gets doctors to subtly kill his political enemies through medical errors - it becomes clear that these sorts of corporate conspiracies are rare and difficult. The government version is scarier, but at least in the US I can still imagine the populace having many chances to learn about this and prevent it. But even in most cases where a coup like this succeeds, things probably go fine; in a post-scarcity world, with his position completely secure, the dictator has no reason to be brutal besides sadism, and most people are not that sadistic. As humanity goes to the stars, most people will be outside the dictator’s reach for speed-of-light reasons alone. In terms of bioweapons, I expect that closed-source AIs will be heavily optimized against helping with these, and open-source AI will be banned after the first warning shot (or become economically prohibitive even before then).

Define a warning shot as some specific AI-related disaster or near-disaster which scares people about AI safety to the same degree that they were scared about terrorism after 9-11 or about COVID in March 2020. I think there’s a 50% chance we get a warning shot before AI crosses the point of no return.
