Standalones / shorter notes

Title Author Publication Summary
Washington Is Treating Africa as a Target, Not a Partner — and Africa Knows It Habib Badawi African Arguments (publication) Badawi argues Frank Garcia's bloc-vote confirmation as Trump's top Africa diplomat — after a 15-month vacancy and 37 of 51 ambassadorships still empty — formalizes the transactional pivot that has already cost the US influence African governments are now leveraging against it.
Translation: Foreign Forces, Fake Patriots, and Adulterated Berries SW China Digital Times (discovery) China Digital Times translates a viral WeChat essay (Old Xiao's Random Jottings) anatomizing the three types of Chinese commentator who blame "foreign forces" for the Fujian adulterated-bayberries scandal — a rare unedited domestic attack on the patriotism-as-deflection rhetoric.
Estonia: consent law passed Euro|topics (curated debate) Euro|topics (discovery) Euro|topics aggregates two Estonian-press defenses of the new "yes model" consent law (69-8 vote), framing the shift from no-means-no to affirmative-consent as moral progress and Istanbul Convention alignment — both excerpts read the law approvingly.
Mario Vargas Llosa and the Possibility of a Populist Novel Geoff Shullenberger Compact Substack Shullenberger reads Vargas Llosa's posthumous final novel I Give You My Silence as the late-career attempt to imagine a populist novel — one where national-cultural unity is sublimated from demotic music (the Peruvian vals criollo) rather than imposed by elite art or revolutionary politics.
David Mamet's Theory of the Lindbergh Kidnapping Joe Nocera Free Press Mamet, in a Free Press bonus podcast with Joe Nocera, advances a dramatist's hypothesis that Charles Lindbergh accidentally killed his own son during a "hide-the-baby" practical joke and then staged the kidnapping — staking the case on the voice-ID problem (Lindbergh had 10,000 hours behind loud aviation engines), the Violet Sharp suicide, and the trial-orchestration anomalies.
Some Leftists Are Boycotting This Film. Everyone Should Watch It. Michelle Goldberg NYT Goldberg defends Israeli director Nadav Lapid's "Yes" — an impossibly scathing film about Israeli national-anthem-for-genocide complicity — against a European-left boycott of its Israeli-state-funded creator, arguing the boycott vindicates Israel's claim that critics target Jewish identity rather than state conduct.
How quickly do electric car sales translate into cars on the road? Hannah Ritchie Sustainability by Numbers (Ritchie) Ritchie reports 2025 IEA data — one in four new cars sold globally was electric, China crossed 50%, Norway near 100%, US stuck at 10% — and offers a fleet-turnover model showing the lag between sales share and on-road share.
Bring Back the SAT Svetlana Jitomirskaya Free Press Jitomirskaya argues UC's 2020 SAT abolition — paired with Newsom's Higher Education Compact and high-school grade inflation — is producing a statistically significant negative correlation between a California high school's math proficiency and its students' UC-Berkeley admission rates.
Biological Evolution and Information Acquisition Brian Potter Construction Physics (Potter) Potter shows via toy simulations that sexual reproduction's advantage over asexual reproduction is an information-theoretic one: gene recombination converts a high-dimensional global search into many parallel local searches.
Lee Raymond, 311 ppm – 421 ppm Robinson Meyer Heatmap (publication) Meyer writes the obituary for ExxonMobil's Lee Raymond as a structural argument that the climate-denial-funding campaign Raymond ran from the late 1990s prevented domestic climate legislation and forfeited U.S. leadership in solar, wind, and battery industries.
Safety and nation-building in Mexico Tyler Cowen Marginal Revolution Cowen argues Mexico is "still not a mature nation-state" — drug-cartel territorial control reflects a 200-year pattern of central-state weakness, and US policy should treat the country as an ongoing nation-building project.
Why is America less of a 24/7 society? Tyler Cowen Marginal Revolution Cowen surfaces the observation that American physical-retail nightlife has thinned dramatically since the 1990s and proposes the load-bearing causes are the migration of 24/7 activity into logistics/digital infrastructure and the improvement of at-home online entertainment.
Does U.F.O. Disclosure Threaten Faith? Ross Douthat NYT Douthat reads Spielberg's "Disclosure Day" film as a marker of the current U.F.O. moment and argues the operative Christian anxiety is not that aliens disprove God but that "brothers from another planet" could pose as shepherds-of-souls — a deception risk that justifies whistleblower protections rather than continued anomalous-video hype.

Washington Is Treating Africa as a Target, Not a Partner — and Africa Knows It

Habib Badawi · 2026-06-10 · Original →

Frank Garcia has just been confirmed as Washington’s top Africa diplomat. His appointment tells African governments everything they need to know about how little they matter to this administration.

Last month, the U.S. Senate confirmed Frank Garcia as Assistant Secretary of State for African Affairs, ending a vacancy that had run for more than fifteen months. Garcia is a former Navy officer and longtime staffer on the House Intelligence Committee. He has no significant published work on African affairs and is, by most accounts, unknown in Washington’s Africa policy community. One Nigerian newspaper described him as a figure ’ His own written testimony to the Senate Foreign Relations Committee traced his first contact with the continent to a port call in Mombasa as a junior naval officer — a detail he offered as evidence of a long fascination with Africa.

Frank Garcia is the new US Assistant Secretary of State for Africa.

The confirmation came as part of a bloc vote covering 49 Trump nominees. Africa was, in other words, one item on a list. That tells you something.

Garcia’s stated priorities are clear enough. In his March testimony, he declared that US policy had ‘for too long’ emphasised ‘aid and dependency, with open-ended commitments and a focus on spreading divisive ideologies.’ The new approach, he said, would be guided by ‘a realistic calculation of costs, risks, and benefits to U.S. interests’ — trade over aid, investment over assistance. He cited the Lobito Corridor, a 1,300-kilometre railway linking Angola’s Atlantic coast to the mineral belts of the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Zambia, as the model: infrastructure that simultaneously generates profit and secures critical mineral supply chains for the US , while also rivalling Chinese investments in the region.

It sounds coherent. The problem is that Washington has already been running this playbook for sixteen months, and the results are visible.

Shortly after taking office in January 2025, the Trump administration suspended USAID funding, which had provided African countries needs with more than $11 billion in 2023 — primarily for health and humanitarian programmes. PEPFAR, the emergency AIDS relief programme that has saved more than 25 million lives since 2003, was effectively wound down. In Malawi, HIV/AIDS services have all but disappeared. In Ethiopia, 16 million people who depend on food aid faced an immediate shortfall. The DRC, already in the grip of devastating conflict, had relied on Washington for roughly 70 percent of its humanitarian assistance. The Prosper Africa Trade and Investment programme — designed specifically to increase US-Africa trade and reduce dependence on China — lost almost $165 million in unspent funds.

Garcia’s framework calls this a reset. What it actually describes is the systematic dismantling of the institutional presence through which influence is built and maintained. Thirty-seven of the 51 U.S. embassies in Africa currently operate without a confirmed ambassador. The Bureau of African Affairs that Garcia now leads churned through three interim heads between January 2025 and his arrival. China, Russia, Turkey, and Gulf states have maintained fully staffed missions throughout, cultivating relationships while Washington’s chairs stood empty.

The administration does have genuine diplomatic activity to point to. The Washington Accords for Peace and Prosperity, signed in June 2025 and formalised in December, brokered a ceasefire between the DRC and Rwanda after more than thirty years of conflict. Trump’s personal involvement mattered, and the deal is real. But its framing — centred on a Regional Economic Integration Framework built around critical minerals trade — tells you what Washington’s primary interest actually is. Analysts at the Institute for Security Studies have described the DRC-Rwanda process as a template: peace bartered for resource access, rather than built on the institutional foundations that make ceasefires hold. Garcia’s testimony explicitly pledged to ‘hold the parties to their commitments.’ The record of the administration’s follow-through on its African engagements offers limited reassurance.

Meanwhile, the transactional pivot has generated its own backlash. Tariffs of 30 percent on South African goods and up to 50 percent on Lesotho products — combined with the expiry of the African Growth and Opportunity Act in October 2025 — have disrupted the trade relationships Garcia claims to be building. South Africa, following a public confrontation between Trump and President Ramaphosa over fabricated claims of ‘white genocide,’ is actively diversifying toward BRICS partners. Kenya has reached a preliminary agreement with China granting its exports duty-free access. Both governments drew the rational conclusion that Washington’s partnerships come with unpredictable costs attached.

The deeper problem with Garcia’s framework is what it leaves out. Trade and investment generate profit. They do not, on their own, generate influence, security, or goodwill. Those things are built slowly, through sustained diplomatic presence, genuine expertise, and a demonstrated willingness to stay engaged when there is nothing immediate to extract. They are lost quickly, and rebuilding them costs far more than maintaining them. Herman Cohen, who held this same role under President George H.W. Bush and who has watched the Africa bureau for decades, said it plainly after Garcia’s confirmation: this is a grave responsibility, and the trust that needs to be rebuilt is a direct result of the Trump administration’s own choices.

African governments are not passive observers in this. They have built real leverage — through the African Union, the G20, BRICS+, and multilateral climate and trade forums — and they are using it. They are comparing what Washington offers against what Beijing, Moscow, Riyadh, and New Delhi are offering. A United States that staffs its missions inadequately, dismantles its health programmes, imposes punitive tariffs, and then arrives with a mining railway and calls it partnership is going to find that the terms it assumed it could dictate are no longer on the table.

Garcia’s confirmation hearing lasted long enough for him to receive four questions before the committee moved on. Africa, once again, was not the main event. The continent, as ever, noticed.

The post Washington Is Treating Africa as a Target, Not a Partner — and Africa Knows It appeared first on African Arguments.

Translation: Foreign Forces, Fake Patriots, and Adulterated Berries

SW · 2026-06-09 · Original →

Hostile foreign forces have long been accused of fomenting unrest and other ills in China. The Ministry of State Security sparked widespread ridicule recently with its claim that external influence has been fomenting slackerism among the country’s youth. After authorities in Fujian punished nearly two dozen officials last month for oversight failures regarding illegal adulteration of fresh bayberries, one online comment reflected this climate of suspicion with the suggestion that outside elements had maliciously hyped the story to stir public anxiety. The post below, from WeChat public account Old Xiao’s Random Jottings, responded with a scathing assault on the rhetorical strategies used to deflect or diminish China’s many food safety scandals, the weaponization of patriotic posturing, and the tendency to blame those who highlight problems instead of those who actually caused them. This point resonates broadly with, for example, commentary on the detentions of investigative journalists Liu Hu and Wu Yingjiao earlier this year. In the Fujian case, bayberries were found to have been soaked in chemicals including the controlled preservative sodium dehydroacetate, which is not permitted for use on fresh fruit in China.

While eating bayberries recently, I bit into the most ridiculous "truth" under heaven: it turns out that all those cases of bayberries soaked in illegal preservatives were not, at heart, a matter of unscrupulous homegrown profiteering. It was all the sneaky doing of vile "external forces."

With one flap of the lips, someone elevates a clear matter of right and wrong to the level of us-vs-them contradiction and the fate of the nation.

"First strawberries, then bayberries, then lychees: it feels like external forces are actively working to harm our Chinese fruit farmers."

Each of these incidents is a deliberate attempt by external forces to "strike at our country’s agricultural sector" by "amplifying food safety anxiety."

The rise and fall of nations rests upon a single poisoned bayberry … what a waste that the creative genius behind that concept isn’t in Hollywood writing spy thrillers.

In the eyes of "strategic geniuses" like this, the concrete pools for soaking adulterated bayberries were secretly dug by spies in the dead of night. The illegal sodium dehydroacetate was smuggled over the mountains and furtively poured in. All that’s missing is a signed confession from a crooked local vendor caught red-handed, twisted to show "an innocent coerced into a life of crime by malign external forces."

As if this gang of crooked scoundrels were just honest merchants, pure as the driven snow, who would never have dreamed of doing something so grubby were it not for “external forces’” pointing a gun at their heads.

The implication is clear: if you dare to dig for the truth, to raise uncomfortable questions, then you’re a traitor in the pay of the U.S., helping "external forces" wage public opinion warfare.

Those able to muddy the waters like this are more cunning than a swindler at the mahjong table. After sneaking good tiles into their own hand and filling their pockets with winnings, they insist on sweeping the whole table into chaos, banging their fist and yelling "Someone messed with the shuffle!" and leaving everyone else at the table in confusion and wondering who the guilty party is.

Every case of excessive agricultural residue that’s been made public to date, like illegally adding sodium dehydroacetate to bayberries, or detection of the illegal insecticide carbofuran in freeze-dried strawberries or excessive levels of carbendazim in lychees, involved illegal practices by domestic traders to increase shelf life or sweetness and cut costs. In none of them has any official investigation revealed the involvement of foreign forces.

Agricultural espionage cases reported by the state security organs have focused on theft of parent seed stock or agricultural data, not issues of pesticide residue in agricultural produce. The goals, methods, and legal nature of these two things are completely unrelated to one another.

Some might say these braindead arguments aren’t worth responding to. The problem is, however you look at it, their blathering this time doesn’t seem merely thoughtless. This kind of malicious, deliberate misdirection is even more detestable than mindlessly spewing bullshit. Bullshit is thoughtless, while malice is calculated. This is not a trivial distinction.

After all these years, I’ve come up with this rule of thumb: almost inevitably, whatever the situation, when someone brings up hostile foreign forces, they have no intention of being reasonable about it.

Thinking it over, those dragging external forces into the bayberry doping affair tend to be one of three types of lowlife:

First, those who dress stupidity up as sense using patriotism as a business strategy.

They can twist any topic into a political stance: even eating fruit can be spun as some sort of “divine mission.” The moment you raise an issue with bayberries, they accuse you of being “unpatriotic.” In one breath, they’ll say “the enemy wants us in disarray”; in the next, they’ll claim, “you’re aiding the enemy.”

They can’t even be bothered to learn what sodium dehydroacetate is, and are hazy on whether you can add preservatives to fresh fruit, yet they dare denounce people as traitors for asking questions. When you bring up facts and regulations with them, they’ll respond with political posturing and conspiracy theories. Patriotism is an all-purpose brick you can use anywhere, ideal for blocking up the mouths of ordinary people.

Are they stupid? Not necessarily: it’s just that they’re rotten to the core, wielding patriotism as a talisman while helping bad guys cheat our own people and imagining that they’re doing Heaven’s will.

The second group are shills for corrupt business interests.

They know better than anyone who’s really soaking bayberries: local brokers who’d pour any illegal chemical into the tanks if it meant a longer-lasting product and doubled profits. These types have less shame than snake-oil salesmen.

But they insist no one can lift the lid on it, and lay the whole thing at the feet of “external forces,” like a thief who snatches a wallet, points the finger at someone else, shouts “Stop that thief!” and while everyone’s distracted by the commotion, hides the loot neatly away.

Since foreign forces are invisible and intangible, it costs nothing to throw the label around. If you can muddy the waters, it’ll completely obscure the homegrown corruption, and the ill-gotten gains will pour in.

Do they believe these conspiracy theories, you might ask? Not in the least, it’s all to help the villains keep things under wraps. The whole fruit market suffers the rot, the masses get cheated, and these purveyors of conspiracy theories are the ones raking in the cash.

The third kind, most odious of all, are the craven officials trying to duck their regulatory duties.

There are massive loopholes in oversight, additive bans go unenforced, and inspections of violations are cursory at best. When the public raises questions, instead of reflecting on their own dereliction of duty, the officials leap straight to yelling “This is sabotage by external forces!” Inadequate oversight isn’t our fault, they claim—the enemy is simply too cunning.

Every case investigated by our own law enforcement agencies has been traced back to domestic illegal manufacturing operations, with not a hint of foreign forces to be found.

Why not say external forces meddled with that sampling gear you’re holding, and forced you to dilute those regulations you made?

Pulling out the great banner of “external forces” whenever problems arise is typical scapegoating rhetoric. In essence, it’s evasion of responsibility for industrial oversight and management.

This bunch of garbage people have scraped together another pile of spurious excuses: "Talking about toxicity without taking into account the dosage is just scaremongering."

There’s some scientific truth in this: the error is in using it as a blanket denial of dangers from agricultural residue. The key distinction is that detecting trace amounts of legal residue ≠ detecting excessive or illegal residue.

Refusing to carry out spot-checks or accept responsibility because "trace amounts are harmless" amounts to tacitly condoning the argument that "as long as it’s within statutory limits, anything goes."

Tell them that "this chemical isn’t allowed anyway," and they reply "toxicology tests are part of the compliance process." Tell them "consuming ten times the limit is harmful," and they’ll say "trace amounts never killed anyone."

If little old me was to sprinkle 0.01g of arsenic onto your rice right now, could I then claim with confidence, "Toxicity depends on the dosage! A little residue won’t kill you, so all this moaning of yours is just scaremongering!"

When did the state say it was OK to soak fresh fruit in banned preservatives? The point of the GB 2763 National Food Safety Standard MRLs for Pesticides in Foods is to set red lines for safe use of compliant agricultural chemicals, not to give illegal or illegally applied ones the green light.

‌"Trace residues aren’t harmful" is fine in the context of legally compliant use, but it has no explanatory power when it comes to illegal additives. Consumers are worried about "unwitting high-risk exposure," not "theoretical risk at lab-measured doses."

The nonsense deepens with this claim: "No food’s clean under a microscope." Consumers can eat fresh fruit that meets national safety standards without a care in the world; there’s no need to talk about "no food being clean under a microscope" to justify illegal practices.

Pursuing absolute zero toxicity was never the point of the current regulations. The point is effective enforcement of bans and limits, and accountability for overstepping, so the masses have a baseline of safety they can rely on.

What the public objects to has never been legally compliant residue, it’s people secretly using banned chemicals, endangering others’ lives by overusing them, and not being held to account when people get sick. When has anyone ever demanded absolutely zero residue?

Why not mention that our fruit exports to the E.U. consistently meet standards far higher than those we have at home? We can control these things when we want to, but domestic regulations are lax, and the cost of flouting them is low.

The most stomach-turning thing of all is using the argument "But it harms fruit growers" as a cudgel. Saying that exposing the problem has wrecked their livelihoods—but who is it who’s really driven honest farmers to despair?

Fruit growers who play by the rules incur costs 50% higher than those selling adulterated fruit, and now sales are down across the industry in the wake of the scandal. And then you don’t blame the profiteers, you don’t fix the regulatory loopholes—you just attack those who exposed and questioned these practices. Isn’t that a bit like a murderer taking hostages to use as human shields, and then having the nerve to claim it’s for their own good?

What’s really hurting fruit farmers isn’t the people raising the problem, it’s conspiracy theories about external forces that undermine normal regulatory efforts, hinder solutions to the problem, and end up harming the whole fruit sector.

The real crisis isn’t "external forces" magnifying anxiety, it’s the discourse trap that equates "raising questions” with “hurting farmers” and “being unpatriotic." This zips people’s mouths shut so that no one dares speak out, and allows dishonest vendors to go on making their dirty money and negligent regulators to keep slacking off.

The toxins in doped bayberries won’t become a miracle cure if you stick a "foreign forces" label on them, just as the dogshit on your shoe won’t become less sticky or less stinky if you say "external forces" shat it out.

If you’re using tricks like this to cover up problems, you’d be better off blaming everything on the moon’s gravitational pull. At least that way, you’ll still look pretty smart, because no “cosmic forces” are going to show up and start disputing your point. It’s certainly a step up from lazily scapegoating "external forces."

Is there anything lower than making dirty money and shirking one’s official duties, on one hand, while raising a memorial arch proclaiming "We care about the nation and its people," on the other?

As long as these human offal exist and are able to go on conning people, they’ll just keep wreaking general havoc, and the nation and society will suffer for it. [Chinese]

Another response to the bayberries scandal came from Wang Wusi at the serially reincarnated WeChat account New New New Silence, who used it as the launchpad for an energetic barrage against prominent nationalist academics.

Estonia: consent law passed

Euro|topics (curated debate) · 2026-06-11 · Original →

The Estonian Parliament has passed a law that fundamentally changes its Penal Code on sexual offences after total of 69 members of parliament voted in favour with only 8 against. The basic principle of the 'yes model' is that sexual contact without clear consent is considered rape. This brings Estonian law into line with the Istanbul Convention on preventing violence against women .

Delfi (EE) — Victims no longer forced to explain

Hannaliisa Uusma / 2026-06-11

“Sex without consent is rape. Responsibility is finally shifting from the victim to the rapist. The law passed today is a moral decision that helps to better protect victims of sexual violence and sends a clear message to society: a person's right to their own body, their sense of safety and their dignity is inviolable. Too many people have had to explain after being raped why they didn't defend themselves. For decades, the system has been based on the premise that the victim must say 'no'. The victim had to be able to explain why they didn't scream, run away or fight back.”

Õhtuleht (EE) — Even issues that seem obvious need rules

2026-06-12

“Why do we have laws? Why is it still necessary to stipulate in law that taking another person's property against their will is not allowed? Unfortunately, human nature has a dark side. The Consent Law is necessary to ensure that in the most personal of all areas – matters concerning one's own body and sexual self-determination – the boundaries are absolutely clear. The law emphasises a simple principle: sexual contact must be based on voluntary and clearly expressed consent. Furthermore, consent, once given, does not automatically imply consent to everything that follows.”

Mario Vargas Llosa and the Possibility of a Populist Novel

Geoff Shullenberger · 2026-06-06 · Original →

Casa de América

In my obituary for the great Peruvian novelist Mario Vargas Llosa, who died last year, I cited Fredric Jameson’s controversial 1986 text “Third-World Literature in the Era of Multinational Capitalism.” In this essay, Jameson argued that “third-world texts … necessarily project a political dimension,” such that “the story of the private individual destiny is always an allegory of the embattled situation of the public third-world culture and society.” Whatever the broader defensibility of the thesis, I noted, it is straightforwardly true of Vargas Llosa’s body work, which never ceased to pose the question asked at the beginning of his 1969 novel Conversation in the Cathedral, “¿En qué momento se había jodido el Perú?” (“At what moment had Peru fucked itself up?”) After asking himself this, that novel’s protagonist, Zavalita, reflects that he is “like Peru” because “he fucked himself up somewhere along the line.” Vargas Llosa’s epigraph, from Balzac, also anticipated Jameson’s thesis: “the novel is the private history of nations.”

Vargas Llosa’s final novel, I Give You My Silence, now out in the lucid English translation of Adrian Nathan West, is perhaps his most didactic attempt to date at what the Marxist theoretician José Carlos Mariátegui called “interpretación de la realidad peruana.” More than any of Vargas Llosa’s prior work, the new novel not only articulates a grand unified theory of Peruvian society and culture but proposes a utopian vision of how it might un-fuck itself. That might make it sound less entertaining than it is. The text’s didacticism is channeled through the voice of its tragicomic hero, Toño Azpilcueta, and thereby humorously ironized.

In this, Vargas Llosa was once again pursuing the novelistic vocation as he came to see it. As I explain in my obituary, he saw novels as creating a space for exploring the utopian “temptation of the impossible,” while using literary techniques to establish a critical distance from radical projects of political and social transformation. In Vargas Llosa’s 1984 tour de force The Real Life of Alejandro Mayta, the eponymous protagonist is a failed revolutionary whose career offers the novelist a way to reflect back on his own youthful left-wing commitments and Sartrean vision of littérature engagée.

Vargas Llosa was a cosmopolitan polyglot who held a Spanish noble title and a seat in the Academie Française; his protagonist is a proudly provincial denizen of a lower middle-class Lima neighborhood who has barely left the city where he ekes out a living as a music journalist, cultural critic, and self-described “proletarian intellectual.” In Toño as in Mayta, he created an alter ego who represented a path he might have but did not take.

However, many of the views of Peru attributed to Toño coincide with the author’s own mature positions; in fact, some sections of the text that are ostensibly excerpts from the protagonist’s book are cribbed from his newspaper columns. In addition to ideas, the author and protagonist share an alma mater, Lima’s Universidad Nacional de San Marcos, where cutbacks have deprived Toño of his promised sinecure as the successor to his mentor, who held the now disbanded chair in Peruvian Studies. Adrift due to the challenges of his avocation and the dissatisfactions of his family life, Toño discovers new purpose one evening when he encounters the guitarist Lalo Molfino, whom in a fit of enthusiasm he anoints the country’s, and perhaps the world’s, greatest ever musician. Some months later, he is crushed when he learns of of Molfino’s untimely death, seemingly by suicide. He sets out to investigate the obscure origins and career of the deceased musician, and then write a book that will lay out his theory of Peruvian culture, centered on the style of music Molfino played—the vals criollo (creole waltz).

The works that made Vargas Llosa’s name—Conversation, Time of the Hero, and The Green House—were kaleidoscopic “total novels” that attempted to encompass Peruvian society in all its brutal stratification, bewildering variety, and sordid hypocrisy. He revived the ambitions of the great fictional chroniclers of the nineteenth century (Balzac, Dickens) while wielding the technical repertoire of modernism (Faulkner, Dos Passos) to dazzling effect. Because the social reality of Peru was fragmented, chaotic, and rife with deception, only a radically experimental novelistic project could hope to capture it. As bleak as this diagnosis was, novelistic form, with its ability to contain the disparate strands of a fractured reality and thereby transcend it, could align itself with revolutionary projects of social transformation; hence, Vargas Llosa’s close early connection with the Cuban Revolution.

Like many others in the 1970s, Vargas Llosa repudiated his revolutionary enthusiasms and settled on the “classical liberal” creed of Hayek and Thatcher, to which he remained faithful until his death. He continued to write political epics, notably The War At the End of the World and The Feast of the Goat. But it was now that he came to see novelistic form as a means of critiquing and ironizing political utopianism, which he did most explicitly of all in Mayta. At the same time, he gave himself the liberty to write lighter, briefer, often humorous texts like Aunt Julia and the Scriptwriter and Captain Pantoja and the Special Service; and to dabble in less highbrow genres including erotica (In Praise of the Stepmother) and detective fiction (Death in the Andes).

I Give You My Silence itself belongs to the latter group of lighter, breezier novels, but its protagonist, Toño, has all the ambition that drove Vargas Llosa’s efforts at the total novel. Although Toño is writing a work of journalistic nonfiction and cultural criticism, he treats his main character (the late musician Molfino) in Jamesonian terms as an allegorical stand-in for Peru as a whole. He is convinced, moreover, that by telling his story he can make “a contribution toward solving the great problems that plagued the nation.” It is not literature Toño sees as the vehicle of nation building, but music, specifically the vals that Molfino mastered and that is his own lifelong passion. Peru is fucked, but in the vals is it is temporarily un-fucked by the “bond of music that transcended enmities to forge a connection between all the people born of this land.” The total novel uses literary form to transcend the contradictions of social reality, whereas the vals, in Toño’s account, resolves these same contradictions and suspends all divisons. Evoking the area outside Lima that gave rise to this musical style, he writes:

Amancaes is more an idea than a reality: a notion of conviviality, of brotherhood uniting Limans of all classes, races, and colors, people who went there for diversion and delight, forgetting their prejudices, loving their neighbors, giving rise, on mysterious afternoons of music and glee, to that magical dance, the Peruvian vals, invention of no one and property of none. Fights were few on the Pampa de Amancaes: if there were a brawl, a dust-up, even an argument, the adversaries were quickly separated, and friendly words stilled the hostilities. All were as one, then, and in some sense, all loved each other. And that was Peru.

Again, Vargas Llosa establishes an ironic distance from Toño’s woozier proclamations, but there is also a clear sense that the author shares some of his creation’s cultural enthusiasms. Most notably, the second principle of national cultural unity proposed by Toño is huachafería, an untranslatable term Toño describes at one point as “peculiarly Peruvian kind of flamboyance” and “a vision of the world as well as an aesthetics: a way of feeling, thinking, and rejoicing, a mode of self-expression and a criterion for judgment.” These lines, as well as the rest of a chapter ostensibly from Toño’s book about Molfino, come from Vargas Llosa’s own 1983 essay on the subject.

Huachafería, which somehow combines outlandish pretension and utter earnestness, ascends from bathos to pathos for both author and protagonist because of its intimate connection to the national character. It is, as Toño says, “innocent, tender, less refined than intuitive, common to all classes of society.” A striking paragraph of this section concerns huachafería in politics, specifically populist politics:

A speech, if it is to seduce the nation, must include a dash of huachafería. A politician who fails to gesticulate, who gets to the point instead of talking in circles, who avoids metaphors and talks drily instead of singing and growling, will struggle to reach the hearts of his listeners. When we speak of a “great orator” in Peru—take Víctor Raúl Haya de la Torre, the socialist founder of aprismo—we are speaking of someone florid and flamboyant, theatrical, operatic. In other words, a snake charmer.

The original version of this passage was written in 1983, and the most likely contemporary allusion was to Alan García, the charismatic young leader and noted orator who succeeded Haya de la Torre as leader of APRA, a distinctively Peruvian left-populist movement somewhat cognate with Argentine Peronism. At the time, Vargas Llosa’s assumption was that populism belonged to the political left. He could be affectionate toward huachafería as a cultural phenomenon, but seemed to be hinting that its introduction into politics could only lead to disaster.

The irony here is that seven years later, when Vargas Llosa ran for president to succeed García, he was defeated not by a left-wing populist but by Alberto Fujimori, a man who presented himself as a competent technocrat, but once in office went on to implement the same Thatcherite shock therapy program Vargas Llosa himself had proposed. At the same time, be built a potent cult of personality and a political machine that remains a formidable force in Peru to this day. Fujimori was the region’s most successful neoliberal reformer, but he was also a corrupt and authoritarian megalomaniac. It is hard not to attribute the enduring potency of fujimorismo, a sort of Peruvian proto-MAGA, to its political channeling of the spirit of huachafería.

Fujimorismo was a key precursor to the current right-populist wave sweeping the continent; if the deceased dictator’s daughter Keiko wins the presidency this weekend, it will converge with that wave. In his later years, after decades denouncing the excesses of fujimorismo, the octogenarian Vargas Llosa embraced the emerging trend of right populism, even endorsing Keiko for president when she faced the leftist Pedro Castillo in the 2021 election.

One way to read I Give You My Silence is as an attempt to create a novel commensurate with this late populist turn, the third phase of Vargas Llosa’s political evolution, still underway when he died. Toño, who seems to be living during Fujimori’s presidency in the 1990s, is indifferent to electoral politics, but there’s a palpable resonance between his ideas and a broader program of petty-bourgeois nationalist populism, where class and racial differences are not abolished but sublimated into a transcendent cultural unity.

It is notable that, for Toño, that unity comes from demotic culture and not the precincts of the elite. In contrast, Vargas Llosa often presented himself as a mandarin critic of pop culture and defender of high art, even if at other times his work evinced a deep familiarity with and appreciation of popular forms. His early novels represent the final flowering of high modernism in fiction before the postmodern turn, and in his 2012 jeremiad Notes on the Death of Culture, he revisited the work of T.S. Eliot and asked whether the novel had a future in an era of mass media spectacle.

For Toño, the “proletarian intellectual” whose cathedrals of culture are the taverns of old Lima where poor, obscure musicians play sentimental waltzes, it is clearly not the novel, but popular music, that charts “the private history of nations.” Vargas Llosa, however, conveys Toño’s idea to us within a novel. In his earlier work, he offered powerful accounts of the vocation of the novelist in the age of revolutions and the era of neoliberal retrenchment. I Give You My Silence leaves us with a question: What can the novel be, if anything, in a populist age? Unfortunately, the great writer can now give us only his silence.

  • Matthew Schmitz on the “heteropessimist horror” of Obsession
  • Thomas Fazi on the interests behind Hungary’s new government
  • Greg Conti on what Pope Leo should have said about AI
  • Juan Rojas on working inside the Biden era immigration machine
  • Fabio Vighi on digital currency and war

David Mamet's Theory of the Lindbergh Kidnapping

Joe Nocera · 2026-06-12 · Original →

David Mamet's Theory of the Lindbergh Kidnapping The Lindbergh Conspiracies Joe Nocera June 12, 2026

After hearing about our podcast, the acclaimed playwright sent Joe Nocera a letter outlining his own theory of the crime—one that puts Charles Lindbergh at the center of the mystery.

A few weeks ago, I received a typewritten letter from David Mamet—the acclaimed playwright, screenwriter, and longtime TGIF cartoonist for The Free Press. He'd been listening to our podcast about the Lindbergh baby kidnapping, and he had thoughts. A lot of them.

Enclosed with the letter was something unexpected: a monologue he'd written in the voice of Dorothy Kilgallen, the legendary journalist who covered the Hauptmann trial and later died under circumstances that remain the subject of speculation after she suggested she was on the verge of a major revelation about the JFK assassination.

Naturally, I wanted to hear more. So I set up an interview. What followed was a fascinating conversation about the Lindbergh case and the way a dramatist approaches an unsolved mystery. Mamet has his own theory about what happened—and it places Charles Lindbergh very close to the center of the story.

This bonus episode is a master class in how one of America's great dramatists thinks about crime, narrative, and doubt—and why, nearly a century later, the Lindbergh case still doesn't add up.

AUTOMATICALLY GENERATED TRANSCRIPT

[0:00] A few weeks ago, I got a typewritten letter from the great playwright and, of course, TGIF cartoonist David Mamet. He began by saying he was looking forward to listening to our Lindbergh podcast that Poppy Damon and I had done, but the rest of the letter made clear that he knew a lot about the kidnapping of Charles Lindbergh's son, and he had his own theories about who might have done it. He also included a monologue he had written in 2018 in the voice of Dorothy Kilgallen, who was one of the leading reporters of that time and who covered the trial of the man accused of kidnapping and murdering Little Lindy. It was also fascinating. So Poppy and I thought it would be fun to get David on the podcast and ask him about the kidnapping. David, welcome.

[Mamet] Thank you. It's always interesting to ask somebody how they became absorbed in the Lindbergh kidnapping. What was it in your case?

Well, I think it was Henry Ford. I was born in 1947, and my dad and everybody else we know, their dad, fought in the war. And a lot of the guys, my friends, their dads were flyers, so we all grew up engaged in the romance of the 20th century, which is aviation, and its little sister of the movies. Any case, Henry Ford was a vicious anti-Semite, and he published a newspaper called The Dearborn Independent, which he force-fed to every Ford dealership. They had to buy so many Dearborn Independent. It was just an anti-Semitic screed.

And his partner in anti-Semitism, the America First Movement, was Charles Lindbergh. So as I grew up, no Jew ever bought a Ford. Zero. Forget about it. Because he was vicious. He was a Nazi, right? He went and he fell in love with Goebbels and they gave him medals and he refused to give them back, etc., etc., etc.

Then I started thinking about aviation, and I found out that my ex-cousin, whose name was Julien Mamet, was Louis Blériot's mechanic, and he worked with Louis Blériot, and you can see my cousin in an early film of Blériot preparing to fly the English Channel. So I got very interested in aviation for a lot of reasons. I became a pilot. I started flying planes. I've always been nuts about aviation. And one of the things I recognized, I was flying around one day, is that Lindbergh said that he identified Richard Hauptmann by his voice. Yes. Right, so there was a, he's in a courtroom and somebody said, "hey, doctor," because the guy who got the ransom money said, "hey, doctor," and Lindbergh said, "that's him." What I realized that this guy had maybe 10,000 hours in an open cockpit plane behind a very, very noisy engine, a couple of them. One of them was a nine-cylinder radial, which was incredibly noisy, and the other one was 12 cylinders. He had to be absolutely vastly hearing impaired, if not near deaf.

So then I started reading a whole bunch of books written about, you know, I'm sure you've read Lindbergh's Ladder, about the facts of the case. And you're going to go through all the facts. Because I was talking to a friend of mine who was a retired FBI agent about the case. And she said the first thing that the FBI would do today would be to interrogate the family. That's what they always do. Right. And the first thing they would have asked is, Lindbergh, where were you as the kidnapping was taking place? And he said, "I was in New York," and he never answered a follow-up question, "What were you doing in New York?" And then when he came back to the house, he came back from the wrong direction. So that got—so I say okay.

Further, I think he was a little bit of a psychopath.

He kept playing these vicious practical jokes on people. He definitely was a sociopath, at least. Yes.

And a very good clue is if you look at him, he always has the same expression on his face. It never varies. I mean, that's certainly true. It could be true if he was a celebrity, but it's also true in the candid shots. He shows no emotion, whatever. So he was famous for playing practical jokes. One of them was he used to hide the baby. He would hide the baby someplace and say to everybody, "Oh my God, the baby's gone, the baby's gone." And then they were like, "Ha ha, the baby's still here." So I became fascinated in the case because it always looked like it wanted to become a tragedy. But it never coalesced into a tragedy. It was always a sick drama. And it was very influential in other crimes of the century. For example… They called him Bruno Richard Hauptmann, right? And he'd never been called—that was his birth name—he'd always been called Richard Hauptmann. So why did they call him Bruno Richard Hauptmann? Because of John Wilkes Booth. So the tradition continues in Lee Harvey Oswald. Nobody ever called him Lee Harvey Oswald, right?

So this guy was—there he was, he was jammed up, Richard Hauptmann, and he said that he was holding the money for his running buddy Isidore Fish, who was a crook, and Hauptmann had been a low-level crook in Germany. So perhaps he was just holding the money for Isidore Fish, and perhaps he just collected the money, right? Which is very possible, but that doesn't mean he did the kidnapping. And he had an airtight alibi that he was at work, and they went to look at the employment records and somebody spilled ink all over them. And then he said, "what did you do then?" "Then I went home and there was a birthday party. Me and my wife and a couple of friends," they all said, "yeah, he was here." Nonetheless, they fried him.

Well, they actually told one of his friends wanted to testify that he was at the birthday party, and the cops basically said, "if you do that, we'll charge you with being an accessory to the crime." Yeah. So he was so scared he didn't do it. Now, in your note to me, and I didn't really realize this, you said that not only was Lindbergh an anti-Semite, but Anne Morrow Lindbergh, his wife, the daughter of Dwight Morrow, was also an anti-Semite. And you made it sound like she was like a big-time anti-Semite.

Well, I don't think she was a big—I think that she was going along with her husband, right? And she wrote a book called The Wave of the Future, which is in praise of fascism. And she says, yes, the wave of the future is going to sweep everything clean. But of course… it's going to—has to sweep away the scum, the scum which rides on the face of the wave. Wow. So any Jew reads that and says, I get it. I see what you're talking about here. Wow. Also, she was a viciously bad poet. I mean, the stuff—I mean, we couldn't even make it on a greeting card today.

She's a pretty good diarist, though. She was a very good flyer, too. Yeah. Yes, that's right. And there's some—I don't know how much stock to put in this, but there's some, one of the rumors or theories is that she was flying so late in her pregnancy that it affected little Lindy physically. And of course, you know, there's all these theories that Lindbergh wanted to hide him or get rid of him because he had some physical problems.

Yeah. Speaking of poetry, there was a guy who was the poetry editor for, I believe for the New Yorker, for it might have been one of the other weeklies, for years and years, named John Ciardi, and he wrote reviews of Anne Morrow Lindbergh's book that were—he was just not kidding. He said, "dude, baby, go home." And also I found out that he'd been a navigator in B-29s in the Pacific. So I thought, why did he super have it in for Anne Morrow Lindbergh? Because her husband was a Nazi. And, you know, he went over there and he looked at the German Air Force, which they shouldn't have even had under the Versailles Treaty. And he came back and said, "dude, there's no way that anyone could win a war with Germany." Yeah.

Tell me about Dorothy Kilgallen and why you chose her to write the monologue, to speak the monologue.

I've always been interested, as all of us are, in conspiracy theories, right? Which means looking at what's happening, right? So if two facts go together and somebody doesn't like the conclusion, they say you're a conspiracy theorist. But I was hired to write a movie about the Kennedy assassination, and one of the things I found out was that Dorothy Kilgallen was the Oprah Winfrey of her day. She was maybe the most famous woman in America after Eleanor Roosevelt. She had a daily radio show. She was syndicated in 500 papers and she was on a weekly television show called What's My Line? Everybody knew who she was. She used to go to the sites of news and her Rolls Royce was in Morse code. And she was the only person I believe who had an interview with Jack Ruby. Wow. After he was arrested. So she goes down to Texas, has an interview with Jack Ruby, and after that she goes to New Orleans where we think she met with Carlos Marcello's people, and then she flew back to New York and she did her show What's My Line. And after the show she said to her people, "tomorrow I'm going to break the biggest story since the crucifixion." And then she went to the hotel around the corner and she had a couple of drinks with an unknown man, and the next morning she winds up dead in bed. So, you know, somebody killed Dorothy Kilgallen.

So she was in my Kennedy show. And so then I found out that she'd been one of—she was also a huge crime reporter. And her co-evil was Adela Rogers St. John, who was Hearst's great crime reporter. And Kilgallen and Adela Rogers St. John and Damon Runyon covered the Lindbergh case. And I was reading Adela Rogers—that's so interesting. Why would they say that? Why would they think that?

Well, because they knew he did do it, or else you don't say he didn't do it. You don't agree that somebody didn't do it. This was obvious to them because they'd been covering crimes all their lives. It was obvious to them that the whole thing stank to high heaven that he took charge of the investigation. He took it away from the New Jersey state cops and said, "I'm going to run it." And all of these grifters glommed onto him, a couple of low-level crooks, and this guy called Jafsie, who was a wonderful—he's Till Eulenspiegel, right? He's this trickster figure who inserted himself into the thing.

It's unbelievable how much you know—you know, when Dorothy Kilgallen and Runyon and the rest of the journalists were at the bar or having dinner, you know, the jury that was supposed to be separated and sequestered, the only thing that protected them was linen, it was sheets. So they heard everything the reporters said, which is kind of incredible.

Well, you know, he was railroaded. They found a wonderful tall guy who either he was the victim of bad luck because his friends said, "hold this money," or more probably he was one of the guys who said, "screw it, somebody's got to get this money. I'll write a note and give the money to me." It's unclear, but in any case, he didn't commit the kidnapping. So if he didn't, who did?

So what is your theory?

My theory is that Lindbergh, for whatever reason—he used to hide the kid. It may be that the kid was anoxic, right, after he was blah blah in utero, or it may just be that he was playing hide the kid and he was screwing around with the kid. And we saw Michael Jackson doing when he hung his baby out the window, right? And he dropped the kid. I think whether he did it on purpose or not, I wouldn't—I don't know whether he was trying to hide the kid, I don't know. But he took the kid out of the crib and something happened and the kid died, whether he did it on purpose or not.

So then he leaves the house and he went and he cobbled together this stupid ladder and put it up in the window. And he comes back and they say, "wait a second, where's the kid? where's the kid?" The maid goes upstairs—the maid, this is Violet Sharp, eventually killed herself. So it happened, after when the New Jersey cops came in, they wouldn't interrogate the Lindberghs because they were the Lindberghs. But then they wanted to interrogate the staff, and Lindbergh said, "you can't talk to the staff." Several weeks later, maybe months later, one of the staff killed herself. I think she knew it was happening.

So anyway, Lindbergh comes back and he puts this ladder up, I think, and I would like to ask some leeway from your audience—I'm a dramatist, right? That's what I do. As a little kid, my mother used to say, "David, why must you dramatize everything?" And you said, "because that's how I'm going to make my living." Yeah, well, and so that's how I make my living. So this is, I'm looking at it, say, if I were writing this thing, that's what I would write.

Then he cobbles together this ladder, and the ladder is found outside the only window in the house, which is unlocked, which happens to be the window that leads into the nursery. So there's no way in the world that anyone other than the people in that house knew where the nursery was, because, you know, he had guards and blah, but he's way off the beaten track. But there's the ladder.

So now the ladder becomes the one clue, and the cops stage this phony baloney manhunt. It's like, you know, cast Scarlett O'Hara—except, you know, cast Scarlett O'Hara was this huge manhunt that David O. Selznick's brother said, "here's how we're going to sell the movie," right? "We're going to have a nationwide search for who's going to play Scarlett O'Hara." Of course, it's always going to be Vivien Leigh.

So what the Lindberghs—what the cops are doing is they say, "this ladder is very oddly made. We're going to take some wood out of this ladder and find out where it was made." And there's all sorts of film on it, newsreels, that they do this analysis of how many saw teeth to the inch and what is the grain, blah, blah, blah. And they finally chased this piece of wood down to some lumberyard in Georgia. It's going to be bullshit. And then later, after they found Hauptmann, they said, "oh my God, we happened to look in his closet and we found that this one rung of the ladder fits exactly into a space in the closet," right? Well, A, if you look at it, it doesn't fit exactly. And B, the guy was a carpenter, right? When they asked him, "did you make the ladder?" He said, "I'm a carpenter."

Right. Well, you know, in the podcast, one of the things we talk about is how America accepted Hauptmann as the villain of until about the 1970s. And so post-Kennedy, post-Martin Luther King, post-RFK, post-Watergate, post-Watergate hearings, suddenly you have this bubbling up, "oh, let's go back and look at Lindbergh. Maybe that's not, you know, quite what we thought it was, too." And it seems to me, I mean, we call it in the podcast the first conspiracy because there are so many holes, not only in what happened, but also in the trial, which was a travesty. That's right, yeah.

Why don't you describe a little bit more about the "Hey Doctor," because that is such a key moment in the trial. Tell us about the cemetery, about the original, how it happened, and how two years later becomes a key moment in the trial.

The kidnappers, whoever they were, gave instructions of what they were supposed to do. They were supposed to take the money and go into the cemetery at night, and Lindbergh is there with this guy Jafsie, John Condon, who inserted himself into the proceedings. Which is, you know, when you make a drama, it's always important to look at the outliers. Like, when you're looking at a dream, you're analyzing a dream—a drama's just a dream. You say, I don't know, you know, I was in bed and I thought I was in bed with—I thought it was my mother and then all of a sudden she turned into a dog and as we went out of the door and the maid was there and as we ran down the street all of a sudden the dog turned into my father and all of a sudden then I find, I realize I'm naked, and because I'm naked I'm trying to—okay, very good. Why is the maid there? Remember the maid. Yeah yeah yeah, okay. So that's the key to—it's a very Talmudic notion, right? That's the key to analysis is the overlooked—the overlooked idea is there for some reason. So when you're writing a drama you always want to write the big stuff, you know, but then this little thing comes out, you throw it in the wastepaper basket and that's the maid. Wait a second, you say, I looked at the obvious, I wrote the obvious, and my subconscious was trying to tell me something about that little overlooked thing.

And so the thing in the overlooked is this guy, Cemetery John, he says, "hey, doctor," and they hand over the money. And then all of a sudden Hauptmann's in the courtroom and somebody says, "hey, doctor," which any lawyer, right, who didn't want to see Hauptmann fried would have said, "that's inadmissible. The best that I'll do is a lineup, you know, a blind lineup and see if Lindbergh can identify him."

Well, you know, there was a blind—there was a lineup, but Condon couldn't identify Lindbergh. Oh, I didn't know that. I mean, excuse me, he couldn't identify Hauptmann. And, you know, then he gets on the stand, whatever, six weeks later, seven weeks later, and identifies him. There's all sorts of things like that.

Do you think Lindbergh's eugenicist tendencies or his—he was a eugenicist. Do you think that had anything to do with the death of his son?

Oh, it's interesting because I thought you were going to say that you think that had anything to do with his anti-Semitism. Oh. But, you know—his anti-Semitism is a proven fact, right? His feelings about his son are conjectural, right? But, you know, I'll buy a conjecture. What the hell?

Jafsie, give us your take on Jafsie.

Jafsie is the outlier. What is he doing there? He just doesn't belong in the picture. Al Capone was in Alcatraz. Al Capone said, "let me out. I'll find the kidnapper in two days." They said, "no, no, no. Lindbergh is running the thing." He tells the state police to stand down. He finds these two little thugs who are running him hither and yon. And all of a sudden, this guy comes out of nowhere and he's not even mythic, he's a fairytale figure like Rumpelstiltskin, like Till Eulenspiegel. He's the trickster figure who says, "I'm gonna make everything right." Why does Lindbergh accept him? He's nobody. He's nobody at all. And so he becomes the running buddy of Lindbergh. He's a perfect fool. Why does Lindbergh go with him? Because he's a perfect fool.

In both the monologue and your letter, you know, there's a larger—there's a larger point that you're making about the necessity to see that Hauptmann—America's necessity to see that Hauptmann dies.

Well, yeah. I mean, you know, it's not too many years since the end of World War I. And in World War I, people were running around America shooting dachshunds, right? And they called frankfurters "Liberty Sausage." It was this wonderful abolition of anti-German sentiment. And there's old Hauptmann. It just happened to be a crowd.

I was going to ask you why you think people still care about this case 90 years later.

Well, because it never resolved. Right? It never resolved. It's an open question. So the question is, how do we treat the great? Right? So it's not a tragedy until their fall. And we see in their fall how wrong they were, which means because the hero stands for us, we see how wrong we were, right? Which is why tragedy is cleansing. We say, my God, I followed the whole thing. Every scene I thought he knew what was going to happen, just like the hero. But at the end I say it was completely wrong, and it was in front of me the whole time.

Have you ever thought about writing a play or a movie about Lindbergh? About the case?

No, I have—I wrote the monologue. You did, and it's wonderful. See, the whole thing about writing biography is the hardest form to write because…

David, it's been a wonderful experience talking to you, and I'm thrilled to see that you've been in this rabbit hole longer than us and have some of the same theories we do. So thanks for being here.

Yeah, you're welcome.

Some Leftists Are Boycotting This Film. Everyone Should Watch It.

Michelle Goldberg · 2026-06-12 · Original →

In 2021, the Israeli film director Nadav Lapid moved to France because, he told me, he felt his country was in a state of "total moral collapse." After Oct. 7, as Israel ground much of Gaza into dust, Lapid returned to Israel to make "Yes," an impossibly scathing, bitterly surreal attempt to capture that collapse on film.

"Yes" tells the story of a struggling pianist and party entertainer known only as Y who is commissioned to write a new national anthem celebrating genocide in Gaza. (The song borrows from a real one in a video by an Israeli nationalist group.) Lapid's film is a vision of a traumatized country where life has become a frenzy of orgiastic partying and histrionic self-righteousness. It's like "The Zone of Interest," the 2023 film about the banal life of a Nazi family living next to Auschwitz, with Eurovision aesthetics. Though of course, as Lapid has pointed out, "The Zone of Interest" wasn't made while the Holocaust was happening. In "Yes," you can sometimes see and hear the real-world annihilation of Gaza in the distance.

Making "Yes" in a country at war and bursting with national fervor wasn't easy. Many in the Israeli film industry refused to have anything to do with it; Lapid had to bring a makeup artist in from Serbia. He'd received money from the Israel Film Fund, which gets Israeli taxpayer funding but works independently of the government, and he feared that if the Ministry of Culture found out what he was up to it would find a way to shut the production down. So he forbade his actors from posting anything about the project online. He dodged the military to film guerrilla-style along the Gaza border.

"Yes" has been furiously denounced by leaders in Israel. The night of the Ophir awards — Israel's version of the Oscars — police officers detained its star, the left-wing performance artist Ariel Bronz, on dubious grounds. (They said they were investigating whether a poem he'd posted on Facebook months earlier incited terrorism.)

Israeli outrage about the film was predictable. More surprising is that some leftists in Europe are boycotting Lapid and his work in the name of Palestinian solidarity. Last month, about a dozen pro-Palestinian filmmakers threatened to pull out of the Marseille International Film Festival because Lapid, an Israeli who'd taken public money, was going to be on the jury. Not wanting to cause trouble for the organizers, Lapid agreed to step down and instead hold a public master class on his films, but that too was canceled under pressure. "For them, even if I would have been selling hot dogs in the festival, it wouldn't be legitimate," he told me.

This wasn't the first time that Lapid has felt stung by the European left. In Spain, he said, the movie screened under police protection because of bomb threats. An Italian distributor turned it down, he said, because she didn't want to be accused of releasing movies from a genocidal state. And though "Yes" premiered at Cannes, the most prestigious film festival in the world, he thinks others have shied away from him and his work because they feared precisely the sort of dispute that broke out in Marseille.

Given that Lapid believes in boycotting Israel, the boycott of him has elicited a smug satisfaction in some quarters. After all, Israel's partisans often claim that the country is hated not for what it does, but for its essential identity. Israel's culture minister gloated that no matter how much Lapid tries to curry favor with the country's enemies, they will always see him as nothing but "a Jew from Israel."

"Yes" mocks Israelis who view themselves as eternal victims and insist that all their critics are antisemites. After being driven from the film festival in Marseille, Lapid felt like the butt of his own film's joke. For about 10 minutes, he said, he thought that "maybe those people in Israel were right."

But since then, he said, the film industry has rallied around him. Open letters supporting Lapid have been signed by leading figures in French cinema, as well as by the Palestinian intellectual Elias Sanbar and the actress Natalie Portman. The letter signed by Portman calls Israel a criminal state but argues — I think irrefutably — that its dissident artists should be treated like those from any other rogue regime. "Russian, Israeli and Iranian filmmakers should not be threatened with erasure to atone for crimes committed by governments they often fiercely oppose," it says.

All this support, said Lapid, has exposed where cultural power really lies. The overwhelming consensus in his favor even makes him a little uncomfortable. "I don't enjoy this feeling," he said. "I don't like to be a part of the majority."

Surveying the whole debacle, Lapid puts much of the blame on cultural institutions that let themselves be cowed by a noisy purist minority. Most festival programmers and film distributors, he believes, don't care all that much about Israel or Palestine. They just don't want trouble. Decades ago, he said, conflict and controversy "made cinema relevant and important and sexy." Now a sort of prudence reigns.

The result is that relatively few films reflect the discombobulating, absurd and often apocalyptic texture of modern life. Sometimes Lapid feels that the most truthful moment in a cinema is when patrons read news updates on their phones before the feature starts. "Then they see the true face of the world," he said. "Then they see the chaos, then they see the madness." When the film starts, they're lulled into distraction. "While the present is playing crazy punk death metal," he said, movies are playing "a nice intermezzo by Chopin."

"Yes" is in part a disgusted condemnation of such escapism. Almost two hours into it, a voice-over says, "The Israelis, who grew up with the question 'How could people live normally while perpetrating horror?,' have themselves become the answer." The inanity of the leftists who'd censor such a film shouldn't distract us from the right-wing nightmare it reveals.

How quickly do electric car sales translate into cars on the road?

Hannah Ritchie · 2026-06-12 · Original →

Last month, the International Energy Agency (IEA) published its estimates of electric car sales data for 2025. I updated all of this data in our charts on Our World in Data, if you want to dive into more detail.

Here, I wanted to share just a few headline figures, then go on to tackle the question of how long it takes for electric car sales to translate into cars on the road.

First, the interesting figures from the new data.

1. One-in-four new cars sold globally was electric. That’s up from one-in-five the year before.

2. China passed the 50% mark, although the year-on-year growth was a bit slower than the year before.

3. Essentially all cars sold in Norway are now electric. I’m guessing that if you buy a combustion car there, people now think you’re a bit odd. A lesser-known example of the EV rollout is Denmark: in 2025, over 70% of new cars were electric.

4. Electric cars continue to grow across Europe, but there was no growth in the United States in 2025.

Its EV share has been stuck on 10% for several years. This is also true for the total number of electric car sales: in 2025, it was 1.5 million, the same as the year before.

5. There are huge differences in the rate of adoption across countries.

Here is just a selection; you can find data for more here.

6. The world is well past the peak of combustion engine car sales. These sales peaked in 2017.

How quickly do electric car sales translate into cars on the road?

A lot of the data on the rollout of new electric car sales seems very promising for the energy transition. But it’s hard to get a sense of how quickly this filters through to cars on the road.

How long before half of the cars on China’s roads are electric? What about all of them? And how sensitive are these timelines to specific factors around car ownership?

For my own curiosity, I built a quick, simple tool that attempts to model this. I thought I’d share it here in case you want to play with it.

It seems like there’s a lot going on, but the inputs and outputs are quite simple. Pick a given country. The chart on the left show electric car sales. The chart on the right shows how this filters through to cars on the road.

You can adjust three inputs:

  • How quickly the EV sales share increases. This is a very simple model, so you can’t adjust this for individual years.
  • The average lifetime of cars: how long people keep their cars for. If people ditch them after 10 years, electric cars will take over faster than it they hold on to them for 15 or 20 years.
  • How quickly the car fleet is growing. In low- and middle-income countries, total car sales are still increasing a lot. In high-income countries, they’re pretty much stagnant; this means EVs are essentially replacing petrol ones, rather than meeting additional new demand, too.

As a quick example: if China’s EV sales share were to grow at 5 percentage points each year, it would reach 100% sales in 2035.

How would this translate into cars on the road?

Today, 14% of cars on the road are electric. If the average lifespan of a car is 15 years, and the fleet is still growing strongly at around 6% per year, this would pass 50% by 2032, and 90% in the early 2040s.

If the car lifespan were 10 years, it would reach 90% in the late 2030s. You can play with these options for a range of different countries to see how sensitive the outcomes are.

Explore the tool
